Many students write $\lambda_D \cdot T$ and lose 1 mark. > 6. **Forgetting to map numerical $PFD_{avg}$ to a SIL level** — KTU board requires explicit conclusion: "Achieves SIL X" or "Insufficient for SIL X." --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ⚡ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Safety in Automation Systems** - [x] **Functional Safety** = correct operation of E/E/PE safety systems to prevent hazards. - [x] **IEC 61508** is the umbrella standard; **IEC 61511** for process SIS; **ISO 13849-1** for machinery. - [x] **Three pillars of risk:** Severity, Frequency of Exposure, Possibility of Avoidance. - [x] **SIL levels:** 1, 2, 3, 4 — corresponding to $PFD_{avg}$ of $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$. - [x] **PL levels:** a, b, c, d, e — corresponding to $PFH_d$ of $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-8}$. - [x] **No SIL 5, no PL f.** Memorize this. - [x] **SIS = Sensor + Logic Solver + Final Element.** Always draw the three-block diagram. - [x] **1oo1:** PFDavg = $\frac{1}{2} \lambda_D T$ | **1oo2:** PFDavg = $\frac{(\lambda_D T)^2}{3}$ | **2oo2:** PFDavg = $\lambda_D T$. - [x] **Defense-in-Depth:** At least **3 independent layers** are mandatory in modern process plants. - [x] **High-demand mode** uses $PFH = \lambda_D$, not $PFD_{avg}$. - [x] **BPCS ≠ SIS** — The Basic Process Control System is **NOT** a safety layer; it is a separate layer underneath SIS. - [x] **Proof Test Interval (T)** directly affects $PFD_{avg}$. Halving T → halves PFDavg (in 1oo1). - [x] **Common Cause Failure (CCF)** must be considered in redundant systems (use $\beta$-factor model). - [x] **IEC 61511 SIL 4** is rare — usually applied to nuclear or extreme hazardous processes. - [x] **OSHA, ANSI B11, NIOSH** are North American / US frameworks; remember their abbreviations. - [x] **Risk = f(Severity, Probability, Exposure)** — this is the **foundation** of every safety design.

topics.title: Data Acquisition and Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Data Acquisition and Monitoring ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the process of sampling real-world physical phenomena (such as voltage, current, temperature, pressure, flow, or position) using sensors and transducers, converting these signals into a digital format that can be processed, displayed, stored, and analyzed by a computer or Programmable Logic Controller (PLC). **Data Monitoring** is the continuous observation, logging, and visualization of these acquired parameters through Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs), SCADA systems, or Industrial IoT dashboards to enable informed operational decisions. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, Data Acquisition and Monitoring (DAM) forms the foundational pillar of any automated industrial process. Without accurate data acquisition, control loops become blind; without reliable monitoring, the entire automation pyramid collapses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student must clearly distinguish between *acquisition* (the physical-to-electrical-to-digital conversion chain) and *monitoring* (the supervisory visualization, alarming, and trending layer). These are conceptually two halves of the same process. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **medical ICU monitor** in a hospital. The patient generates real biological signals (heartbeat, blood pressure, oxygen saturation). Tiny electrode "sensors" detect these signals, an internal circuit amplifies them, an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) digitizes them, and a screen displays the live waveform. A nurse watches the screen continuously. If a parameter crosses a threshold, an alarm beeps. Now scale this exact same architecture to an industrial plant: | Medical ICU | Industrial Plant Equivalent | |---|---| | Patient's body | Industrial process (boiler, motor, reactor) | | ECG electrodes | Temperature/pressure/flow sensors | | Amplifier circuit | Signal conditioner | | ADC chip inside monitor | PLC analog input module | | ICU display screen | HMI / SCADA screen | | Nurse's observation | Operator's monitoring + alarm logic | The nurse does not "control" the patient directly, but alarms trigger doctor intervention. Likewise, monitoring does not control — it informs. > [!NOTE] > **Core Definition Box:** > - **Sensor** – converts physical quantity → electrical signal (analog). > - **Transducer** – broader term encompassing both sensor and actuator functions. > - **Signal Conditioner** – amplifies, filters, linearizes, and isolates the raw signal. > - **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** – converts conditioned analog voltage → digital binary code. > - **HMI / SCADA** – supervisory software layer for real-time monitoring. ### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics Key metrics you must memorize for KTU exams: - **Sampling Rate (fs):** typically **1 kHz to 100 kHz** for industrial DAQ; up to **MHz** for vibration analysis. - **Resolution:** commonly **12-bit, 16-bit, or 24-bit** ADCs. - **Accuracy:** expressed as a percentage of Full Scale (FS), e.g., **±0.1% FS**. - **Input Range:** standard industrial ranges are **0–10 V, 4–20 mA, ±10 V, 0–5 V, RTD, Thermocouple (TC)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five-Stage DAQ Architecture Every industrial DAQ system, regardless of complexity, follows this canonical five-stage pipeline: 1. **Sensing Stage** – Physical phenomena (T, P, F, L, v) → electrical analog signal via sensors. 2. **Signal Conditioning Stage** – Amplification, filtering (anti-aliasing), linearization, isolation, cold-junction compensation (for thermocouples). 3. **Conversion Stage** – Analog signal → digital binary code via ADC (Nyquist rate sampling). 4. **Processing Stage** – Microprocessor/PLC/DSP performs scaling, linearization, calibration, engineering unit conversion. 5. **Communication & Display Stage** – Data transmitted via fieldbus/Ethernet and rendered on HMI/SCADA. ### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Stage - **Why sense?** Because controllers cannot act on variables they cannot measure. The famous "you cannot control what you cannot measure" principle. - **Why condition?** Sensor outputs are typically **millivolts (mV)** level — too weak and too noisy to feed directly to an ADC. Signal conditioning boosts the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR). - **Why convert?** Computers and PLCs are digital machines. They only understand binary (0/1) representations. - **Why process?** Raw ADC counts must be converted to engineering units (e.g., °C, bar, m³/h) using calibration curves. - **Why display?** Operators need intuitive, graphical visualization to make decisions and trigger interventions. ### 2.3 Sampling Theorem and Nyquist Criterion The **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem** states: > A bandlimited analog signal containing no frequency components higher than **fmax** Hz must be sampled at a rate **fs ≥ 2·fmax** samples per second to allow perfect reconstruction. Violating this theorem causes **aliasing** — a phenomenon where high-frequency signals masquerade as low-frequency signals, producing completely false readings (e.g., a 90 Hz vibration appearing as 10 Hz in monitoring). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in KTU university exams.** | Concept | Formula | Description of Terms | Units | |---|---|---|---| | Nyquist Sampling Rate | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling frequency, $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency | Hz | | ADC Resolution (LSB size) | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage, $n$ = bit resolution | V | | Quantization Error (max) | $E_q = \pm \dfrac{\text{LSB}}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$ | Maximum quantization error | V | | SNR (ideal ADC) | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in decibels | dB | | Current Loop to Voltage | $V = I \cdot R_{sense}$ | Standard 4–20 mA with 250 Ω gives 1–5 V | V | | Engineering Unit Conversion | $E.U. = \left(\dfrac{\text{ADC count}}{2^n - 1}\right) \cdot (\text{EU}_{max} - \text{EU}_{min}) + \text{EU}_{min}$ | Converts raw digital count to physical value | Various | | Alias Frequency | $f_{alias} = \vert f_{signal} - f_s \cdot \text{round}\left(\dfrac{f_{signal}}{f_s}\right) \vert$ | Frequency observed when undersampled | Hz | | Anti-Alias Filter Cutoff | $f_c \leq \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Low-pass filter cutoff to prevent aliasing | Hz | | Thermocouple (Seebeck) | $V = S(T) \cdot \Delta T$ | $S(T)$ = Seebeck coefficient, $\Delta T$ = temperature difference | mV | | RTD (PT100) Approximation | $R_T = R_0 \left[1 + \alpha (T - T_0)\right]$ | $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ at $0\,^\circ$C, $\alpha \approx 0.00385$ | Ω | ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Industrial DAQ is the backbone of **Industry 4.0, smart manufacturing, predictive maintenance, and digital twin** technology. In a modern oil refinery, thousands of DAQ channels continuously stream data to a central historian (e.g., OSIsoft PI). Machine learning models analyze trends to predict equipment failure weeks in advance, reducing unplanned downtime by up to **30–50%**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem (Intuitive Justification) We wish to determine the minimum number of samples per second required to uniquely reconstruct a sinusoidal signal of frequency $f_{max}$ Hz. **Step 1.** A sinusoid $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_{max} t + \phi)$ is fully described by three parameters: amplitude $A$, frequency $f_{max}$, and phase $\phi$. **Step 2.** Each sample provides one equation. To solve for three unknowns, we need at least three independent samples per cycle. **Step 3.** To obtain three samples uniformly distributed across one period $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{max}}$, the sampling interval must satisfy $T_s \leq \dfrac{T}{2}$. **Step 4.** Substituting $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$: $$\dfrac{1}{f_s} \leq \dfrac{1}{2 f_{max}}$$ **Step 5.** Inverting both sides and reversing the inequality: $$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **Nyquist Rate**. Sampling exactly at $2 f_{max}$ is the theoretical minimum; in practice, engineers use **5× to 10×** the highest frequency to leave a safety margin for the anti-aliasing filter roll-off. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: ADC Resolution and Quantization Error A unipolar ADC measures voltages in the range $0$ to $V_{FS}$ using $2^n$ discrete levels. **Step 1.** The total number of discrete codes possible is $2^n$. This is the *number of quantization levels*. **Step 2.** The width of one level (the size of the Least Significant Bit) is the full range divided by the number of levels: $$\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS} - 0}{2^n} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$ **Step 3.** The maximum quantization error is half an LSB, because any analog value falling within a quantization bin is rounded to its center: $$E_q = \pm \dfrac{\text{LSB}}{2} = \pm \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$ **Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A 12-bit ADC with a full-scale voltage of $V_{FS} = 10\ \text{V}$: $$\text{LSB} = \dfrac{10}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{10}{4096} \approx 2.441\ \text{mV}$$ $$E_q = \pm \dfrac{2.441}{2} \approx \pm 1.221\ \text{mV}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Engineering Unit Conversion (Scaling Formula) The PLC receives a raw integer count from the ADC. We must convert it to engineering units (e.g., °C, kPa). **Step 1.** Normalize the raw count to a fraction of full scale. The maximum count for an $n$-bit ADC is $2^n - 1$: $$f = \dfrac{C}{2^n - 1}$$ **Step 2.** Map the normalized fraction linearly to the engineering range $[\text{EU}_{min},\ \text{EU}_{max}]$: $$E.U. = f \cdot (\text{EU}_{max} - \text{EU}_{min}) + \text{EU}_{min}$$ **Step 3.** Substituting $f$: $$E.U. = \left(\dfrac{C}{2^n - 1}\right) \cdot (\text{EU}_{max} - \text{EU}_{min}) + \text{EU}_{min}$$ **Numerical Example:** A 4–20 mA pressure transmitter connected through a 250 Ω resistor gives 1–5 V. A 14-bit ADC reads a raw count of 8192. The transmitter range is 0–10 bar. $$E.U. = \left(\dfrac{8192}{16383}\right) \cdot (10 - 0) + 0$$ $$E.U. = (0.5001) \cdot 10 \approx 5.001\ \text{bar}$$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sampling Rate Selection | Signal Type | Typical Bandwidth | Recommended $f_s$ | Rule of Thumb | |---|---|---|---| | Temperature (slow) | < 1 Hz | 5–10 Hz | $10 \times f_{max}$ | | Pressure (process) | 0–100 Hz | 500 Hz | $5 \times f_{max}$ | | Flow (turbulence) | 0–1 kHz | 5 kHz | $5 \times f_{max}$ | | Vibration analysis | 0–10 kHz | 50 kHz | $5 \times f_{max}$ | | Audio (acoustic) | 0–20 kHz | 44.1 kHz | Standard CD rate | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a DAQ System ```mermaid flowchart LR node1[Physical Process
Boiler Motor Reactor] --> node2[Sensor Layer
RTD TC LVDT PT] node2 --> node3[Signal Conditioner
Amplify Filter Isolate] node3 --> node4[Multiplexer
MUX] node4 --> node5[ADC
Analog to Digital] node5 --> node6[Microprocessor
PLC DSP] node6 --> node7[Communication
Modbus Profinet Ethernet] node7 --> node8[HMI SCADA
Display Alarm Log] node7 --> node9[Historian Database
Cloud Storage] node8 --> node10[Operator
Decision Action] node9 --> node11[Analytics
Predictive Maintenance] ``` ### 4.2 Signal Conditioning and Conversion Detailed Flow ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA[Raw Sensor Output
mV level noisy signal] --> nodeB[Instrumentation Amplifier
Gain 100 to 1000] nodeB --> nodeC[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter
Cutoff fs by 2] nodeC --> nodeD[Isolation Amplifier
Galvanic Isolation] nodeD --> nodeE[Sample and Hold
Circuit] nodeE --> nodeF[ADC
12 bit 16 bit 24 bit] nodeF --> nodeG[Digital Processing Unit
Scaling Linearization] nodeG --> nodeH[Engineering Unit Output
Degree Celsius Bar m3 per h] ``` ### 4.3 Closed-Loop Monitoring and Alarm Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeP1[Process Variable PV] --> nodeP2[Acquisition Module] nodeP2 --> nodeP3{Threshold
Comparison} nodeP3 -->|Normal| nodeP4[Green Display
Normal Operation] nodeP3 -->|Warning| nodeP5[Yellow Display
Operator Attention] nodeP3 -->|Critical| nodeP6[Red Display
Alarm Trip] nodeP5 --> nodeP7[Operator Acknowledge] nodeP6 --> nodeP8[Auto Shutdown Sequence] nodeP4 --> nodeP9[Continue Logging
to Historian] nodeP5 --> nodeP9 nodeP6 --> nodeP9 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define Data Acquisition System (DAS). List its main components.** **Model Answer:** A Data Acquisition System (DAS) is a collection of hardware and software elements that enables the measurement, conversion, and storage of real-world physical parameters into a digital format suitable for computer-based processing and analysis. **Main components:** 1. **Sensors/Transducers** – convert physical quantity to electrical signal. 2. **Signal Conditioning circuit** – amplifies, filters, isolates, linearizes. 3. **Multiplexer (MUX)** – allows multiple channels to share a single ADC. 4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** – converts analog signal to digital code. 5. **Microprocessor/PLC/DSP** – processes, scales, and stores the data. 6. **Display/Recording device** – HMI, SCADA, or data logger. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024] State the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. What is aliasing?** **Model Answer:** The **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** states that a bandlimited analog signal having no frequency component above $f_{max}$ Hz must be sampled at a rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ samples per second to permit faithful reconstruction of the original signal. **Aliasing** is the distortion artifact that occurs when a signal is sampled below the Nyquist rate, causing high-frequency components to appear as false lower-frequency signals. It is prevented by using an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** before the ADC with cutoff frequency $f_c \leq f_s / 2$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a generalized Data Acquisition System. Discuss the role of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 12-bit ADC is used in a temperature monitoring system with a full-scale input range of 0–10 V. The sensor output is linearly scaled to cover –50 °C to +150 °C. Calculate: (i) The size of 1 LSB in volts. (ii) The quantization error. (iii) The temperature reading when the raw ADC output is 2048. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A** **(a) Block Diagram Explanation:** | Block | Function | |---|---| | Sensor | Detects physical variable (e.g., temperature) and outputs a small analog voltage (mV range). | | Transducer | Converts non-electrical physical quantity into proportional electrical signal. | | Signal Conditioner | Amplification, filtering (anti-alias), cold-junction compensation, linearization. | | Multiplexer | Time-multiplexes multiple input channels to a single ADC for cost efficiency. | | Sample-and-Hold | Freezes the analog value during ADC conversion to prevent errors. | | ADC | Converts analog voltage to binary digital code at sampling rate $f_s$. | | Microprocessor/PLC | Performs scaling, linearization, EU conversion, calibration, and stores data. | | Display/Recorder | HMI/SCADA screen, paper recorder, or cloud historian. | | Communication | Fieldbus/Industrial Ethernet (Modbus, Profinet, EtherNet/IP) for data transfer. | **Working principle:** The sensor picks up the physical variable → produces a weak analog signal → signal conditioner amplifies and filters it → multiplexer switches among channels → ADC samples and digitizes → microprocessor scales the binary code to engineering units → data is logged and displayed on HMI. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** **Given:** $n = 12$ bits, $V_{FS} = 10\ \text{V}$, EU range = –50 °C to +150 °C, raw count $C = 2048$. **(i) LSB size:** $$\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{10}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{10}{4096} \approx 2.441\ \text{mV}$$ **(ii) Quantization error:** $$E_q = \pm \dfrac{\text{LSB}}{2} = \pm \dfrac{2.441\ \text{mV}}{2} = \pm 1.221\ \text{mV}$$ **(iii) Temperature reading:** $$E.U. = \left(\dfrac{C}{2^n - 1}\right) \cdot (\text{EU}_{max} - \text{EU}_{min}) + \text{EU}_{min}$$ $$E.U. = \left(\dfrac{2048}{4095}\right) \cdot (150 - (-50)) + (-50)$$ $$E.U. = (0.5006) \cdot 200 - 50$$ $$E.U. = 100.12 - 50 = 50.12\ ^\circ\text{C}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]** **(a)** Explain the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem. A vibration sensor outputs a signal with significant frequency content up to 8 kHz. Determine the minimum sampling rate. If the chosen ADC has a sampling rate of 25 kHz, calculate the alias frequency for an unwanted 30 kHz noise component. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With a suitable diagram, explain the operation of a 4–20 mA current loop transmitter. Why is the 4–20 mA standard preferred over 0–10 V in industrial environments? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B** **(a) Sampling Theorem Application:** **Statement:** A bandlimited signal of maximum frequency $f_{max}$ must be sampled at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ to enable perfect reconstruction. **Minimum sampling rate for 8 kHz signal:** $$f_s \geq 2 \times 8\ \text{kHz} = 16\ \text{kHz}$$ So minimum $f_s = 16\ \text{kHz}$. **Alias frequency calculation for 30 kHz noise with $f_s = 25$ kHz:** The Nyquist frequency is $f_N = f_s / 2 = 12.5$ kHz. Since 30 kHz > 12.5 kHz, aliasing occurs. $$f_{alias} = \left\vert f_{signal} - f_s \cdot \text{round}\left(\dfrac{f_{signal}}{f_s}\right) \right\vert$$ $$\text{round}\left(\dfrac{30}{25}\right) = \text{round}(1.2) = 1$$ $$f_{alias} = \vert 30 - (1)(25) \vert = \vert 5 \vert = 5\ \text{kHz}$$ So the 30 kHz noise appears as a false **5 kHz** signal at the output. --- **(b) 4–20 mA Current Loop:** A 4–20 mA current loop uses a two-wire connection where the transmitter regulates the loop current between 4 mA and 20 mA proportional to the measured variable. | Current | Meaning | |---|---| | 4 mA | Lower Range Value (LRV) / zero of scale | | 20 mA | Upper Range Value (URV) / full scale | | < 4 mA (e.g., 3.6 mA) | Sensor fault / open wire | | > 20 mA (e.g., 22 mA) | Sensor saturation | **Receiver-side conversion:** A precision 250 Ω sense resistor converts the current to a 1–5 V signal for the ADC. **Why 4–20 mA is preferred over 0–10 V:** 1. **Noise immunity:** Current signals are immune to voltage drops along long cables and electromagnetic interference (EMI). 2. **Live-zero detection:** 4 mA represents zero, so a reading below 4 mA instantly indicates a wire break or transmitter fault. With 0–10 V, a 0 V reading could mean either "zero process value" or "broken wire" — ambiguous. 3. **Constant power dissipation:** Since current is constant for a given value, the transmitter can use the same two wires for both signal and power (loop-powered). 4. **Long-distance accuracy:** Works reliably over kilometers; voltage signals degrade beyond 50–100 m. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in KTU Exams:** > 1. **Confusing "Nyquist Rate" with "Sampling Rate":** Nyquist Rate is the *minimum* rate ($2 f_{max}$). Actual sampling rate is typically **5× to 10×** higher. Writing $f_s = f_{max}$ will cost full marks. > 2. **Forgetting the "(2^n − 1)" denominator:** Many students use $2^n$ instead of $2^n - 1$ in the EU scaling formula. This gives a small but consistent error. > 3. **Wrong units in LSB:** The LSB is a voltage (V or mV), not a dimensionless number. Always state units. > 4. **Aliasing vs. Quantization:** These are two different errors. Aliasing is a *sampling* error; quantization is an *ADC resolution* error. Examiners frequently set traps testing this distinction. > 5. **4–20 mA vs. 0–10 V:** Always emphasize *live-zero* as the key reason for current loop preference. Saying "noise immunity" alone is incomplete. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):** - DAQ = **Sense → Condition → Convert → Process → Display**. - **Nyquist Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — aliasing occurs if violated. - **LSB (ADC resolution):** $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$. - **Quantization error:** $\pm \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$. - **SNR (ideal):** $6.02n + 1.76\ \text{dB}$. - **Standard industrial signals:** 0–10 V, 4–20 mA, ±10 V, RTD (PT100), Thermocouple (J, K, T, R, S). - **4–20 mA advantages:** noise immunity, live-zero fault detection, long-distance reliability, two-wire loop-powered operation. - **PT100 RTD formula:** $R_T = R_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]$ with $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$ and $\alpha = 0.00385$. - **Anti-aliasing filter:** low-pass with cutoff $\leq f_s/2$, MUST be analog (placed before ADC). - **Typical DAQ resolution:** 12-bit (general), 16-bit (precision), 24-bit (audio/scientific). - **SCADA = Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition** — strictly the monitoring & supervisory layer, not direct control. - **HMI vs SCADA:** HMI is the local display; SCADA is the multi-site, networked, historian-backed system. - **Sampling rate rule of thumb:** $f_s = 5 \times f_{max}$ (industrial), $f_s = 10 \times f_{max}$ (high-accuracy). - **History vs. Real-time:** Real-time = immediate process control; Historian = long-term storage and analytics. - **Digital twin** = virtual replica fed by real-time DAQ streams — key Industry 4.0 concept.

topics.title: Human-Machine Interface (HMI)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Human-Machine Interface (HMI)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** is the physical and logical component of an industrial automation system that enables bidirectional communication and interaction between a human operator and a machine, process, or automated system. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PERUT632 – Advanced Industrial Automation), the HMI is formally defined as:

> **Definition (KTU Standard):** *"An HMI is a hardware-software integrated operator panel, terminal, or graphical interface that provides visualisation, control, monitoring, alarming, and data-logging capabilities over an industrial process, typically interfacing with a PLC, SCADA, DCS, or embedded controller through standardised industrial communication protocols."*

In simple words, an HMI is the **"dashboard of an industrial process"** — the screen and input device through which an engineer sees what is happening in a plant and sends commands back to the machines.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In PERUT632, HMI is treated as the *upper layer* of the automation pyramid, sitting above the **Control Layer (PLC/DCS)** and below the **Management/ERP Layer**. It is a mandatory study topic in **Module 1: Introduction to Industrial Automation Systems**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an HMI like the **dashboard of a modern car**:

- The **speedometer, fuel gauge, and engine temperature indicator** are the *display elements* of the HMI — they show you what the engine is doing.
- The **steering wheel, accelerator, and brake pedals** are the *input elements* of the HMI — they let you control the car.
- The **ECU (Engine Control Unit)** sitting behind the dashboard is analogous to the **PLC**, and the dashboard itself is the HMI.
- A **warning light** (e.g., low oil) is the HMI's **alarm function**.

Similarly, in a bottling plant, an HMI might show tank levels, motor speeds, conveyor status (visualisation) and let the operator start/stop a line, change setpoints, or acknowledge alarms (control & alarm management).

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Term Mapping (Important for KTU Viva):**
> - HMI = Operator Interface Terminal (OIT)
> - HMI Panel = Touch Screen Panel (TSP)
> - HMI Software = SCADA-adjacent graphical configuration tool (e.g., Wonderware, WinCC, FactoryTalk View)

---

## 1.3 Physical Standards and Performance Metrics

The following standard metrics are used to characterise an HMI system and frequently appear in KTU theory questions:

| Metric | Standard Value | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Screen size** | **4" to 22"** (typical industrial range) | Physical diagonal display dimension |
| **Resolution** | **VGA (640×480) to Full HD (1920×1080)** | Pixel density of display |
| **Touch technology** | **Resistive, Capacitive, IR, SAW** | Input sensing method |
| **Protection rating** | **IP65 / NEMA 4X (front panel)** | Dust & water ingress protection |
| **Operating temperature** | **0 °C to +55 °C** | Industrial environmental tolerance |
| **MTBF** | **> 50,000 hours** | Mean Time Between Failures |
| **Response time** | **< 100 ms** | Operator command to actuator latency |
| **Communication** | **Modbus, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA** | Industrial fieldbus standards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Note:** There is *no universal physical constant* associated with HMI, since it is a *system-level* concept. However, the **IEC 61131-3** standard governs the underlying PLC logic, and **IEC 62443** governs the cyber-security of HMI systems — both are exam-relevant.

---

## 1.4 Scope of HMI in Industrial Automation

The HMI occupies a critical position in the **ISA-95 Automation Pyramid**:

1. **Level 0 – Process (Sensors & Actuators)**
2. **Level 1 – Basic Control (PLCs, DCS controllers)**
3. **Level 2 – Supervisory (HMI / SCADA)** ← *HMI is here*
4. **Level 3 – Operations Management (MES)**
5. **Level 4 – Enterprise (ERP)**

The HMI is the **only layer where humans directly interact** with the automation system, making it indispensable for safe, efficient, and transparent plant operation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Core Functions of an HMI (Logical Breakdown)

An industrial HMI performs **five primary logical functions**. Each function is a high-yield KTU exam point.

### (a) Visualisation (Process Graphics)
- The HMI renders **mimic diagrams** (also called P&ID mimics) that represent the physical process.
- Real-time data from PLC tags is **mapped to graphical objects** (pumps, valves, tanks, motors).
- Example: A pump symbol turns **green** when running, **red** when stopped, **yellow** when in fault.

### (b) Control (Command Dispatch)
- Operators can issue commands: *Start, Stop, Reset, Jog, Setpoint Change, Mode Change*.
- Commands are sent from the HMI → **PLC input registers** (e.g., holding registers over Modbus TCP).
- **Critical safety rule (KTU-favourite question):** Critical commands should require **two-step confirmation** (e.g., "Are you sure?" dialog) to prevent accidental operation.

### (c) Alarm Management
- Alarms are prioritised into classes: **Priority 1 (Critical) → Priority 4 (Informational)** per **ISA 18.2** standard.
- The HMI must support **Alarm Acknowledgement (ACK)** and **Alarm History Logging**.
- Audible annunciation and colour-coding are mandatory design features.

### (d) Data Logging & Trending
- Historical data is stored in **time-series databases**.
- **Trends** (real-time and historical) allow engineers to analyse process behaviour.
- Storage is typically **circular-buffered** with FIFO overwrite.

### (e) Security & Access Control
- Multi-level **user authentication** (Operator, Supervisor, Engineer, Administrator).
- Each level has **distinct read/write privileges**.
- Modern HMIs comply with **IEC 62443** cyber-security standards.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** A 14-mark question is frequently asked on *"Explain the functions of HMI with block diagram"* — all five functions above must be covered with at least one industrial example each.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Standards Sheet

The HMI domain is **standards-driven** rather than formula-heavy. The following table consolidates the most exam-relevant technical references.

| Concept | Formula / Standard | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Tag Polling Rate** | $f_{poll} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ (Hz) | HMI polls PLC tags at a defined cycle; T_scan is the scan interval in seconds |
| **Touch Latency Budget** | $T_{latency} = T_{touch} + T_{PLC} + T_{comm} + T_{render}$ | Total operator-perceived delay (must be < **100 ms**) |
| **Screen Refresh Rate** | $f_{refresh} \ge 25 \text{ Hz}$ | Minimum to avoid flicker (typically **60 Hz**) |
| **Alarm Rate Limit** | $R_{alarm} \le 10 \text{ alarms/min}$ (operator manageable) | EEMUA 191 recommendation |
| **Colour Coding (ISA 18.2)** | Red = Danger, Yellow = Warning, Green = Normal, Blue = Info | Standard alarm priority colour map |
| **Data Log Compression Ratio** | $C_r = \dfrac{S_{raw}}{S_{compressed}}$ | Used in historian storage optimisation |
| **Mean Time Between Failure** | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{total}}{N_{failures}}$ | Reliability metric for HMI hardware |
| **Network Bandwidth** | $BW = N_{tags} \times f_{poll} \times S_{tag}$ | Total PLC-HMI traffic in bytes/sec |
| **Operator Response Time** | $T_{resp} = T_{detect} + T_{recognise} + T_{decide} + T_{act}$ | Human-factors engineering model |
| **Visibility Index** | $V = \dfrac{L_{screen}}{L_{max}}$ | Luminance ratio for daylight readability |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall Alert for Students:** Do NOT confuse **HMI** with **SCADA**. SCADA is a *software architecture* that may *include* an HMI. HMI is the *operator-facing graphical layer*; SCADA is the *supervisory control + data acquisition backbone*. This distinction is a **guaranteed 2-mark KTU question**.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Discrete Manufacturing:** Automotive body shop — HMI shows robotic cell status, cycle time, fault diagnostics.
- **Process Industry:** Oil refinery — HMI shows distillation column temperatures, pressures, flow rates.
- **Power Plants:** Boiler drum level, turbine speed, grid frequency.
- **Water Treatment:** Pump station control, reservoir level, chemical dosing rates.
- **Building Automation:** HVAC, lighting, access control — HMI is a *building dashboard*.

---

## 2.4 HMI vs SCADA vs DCS — KTU Comparison Table

| Feature | HMI | SCADA | DCS |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Scope** | Single station/panel | Multi-station, plant-wide | Plant-wide, distributed control |
| **Primary Function** | Visualisation & local control | Supervisory control + data acquisition | Closed-loop continuous control |
| **Architecture** | Single-node | Multi-node (servers + clients) | Distributed controllers |
| **Redundancy** | Rarely redundant | Hot-standby servers | Controller-level redundancy |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium–High | Very High |
| **Typical Vendor** | Siemens, Schneider, DeltaV | Wonderware, WinCC, Ignition | Honeywell, Yokogawa, ABB |
| **Best for** | Small machines, OEMs | Utility, infra | Continuous process plants |

---

## 2.5 HMI Selection Criteria (Design Engineering)

When selecting an HMI for a project, the following criteria are evaluated:

1. **Number of tags** to be displayed (drives screen & memory choice).
2. **Environmental rating** (IP65 for wash-down areas, IP20 for panels).
3. **Communication protocol compatibility** with the chosen PLC.
4. **Number of users / concurrent access**.
5. **Certifications needed** (UL, CE, ATEX for hazardous zones).
6. **Cybersecurity requirements** (IEC 62443).
7. **Lifecycle & spare-parts availability** (typically 10+ years).
8. **Software ecosystem** (ease of programming, library support, version control).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — HMI Network Bandwidth Estimation

This is a **high-yield numerical problem** that frequently appears in KTU Module 1 internal assessments. We derive the bandwidth required for an HMI–PLC communication link.

### Problem Statement (Model)

An HMI polls a PLC over **Modbus TCP** at a cycle time of **500 ms**. The PLC exposes **120 tags** as follows:
- 60 tags are **16-bit integers** (Boolean + status)
- 40 tags are **32-bit floating-point** (process variables)
- 20 tags are **64-bit double** (cumulative energy counters)

Each Modbus TCP transaction carries a **12-byte overhead** (MBAP header + transaction ID). Compute the **steady-state bandwidth** in **kbps**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the poll frequency.**

The HMI poll cycle is $T_{scan} = 500 \text{ ms}$.

$$
f_{poll} = \frac{1}{T_{scan}} = \frac{1}{0.5 \text{ s}} = 2 \text{ polls/second}
$$

This is the number of complete tag-update cycles per second.

---

**Step 2 — Compute the raw payload size per tag category.**

Each tag carries its data bytes plus a fixed 12-byte Modbus TCP overhead.

- For 16-bit (2-byte) tags: $S_{16} = 2 \text{ bytes/tag}$
- For 32-bit (4-byte) tags: $S_{32} = 4 \text{ bytes/tag}$
- For 64-bit (8-byte) tags: $S_{64} = 8 \text{ bytes/tag}$

---

**Step 3 — Compute total payload per poll cycle.**

$$
S_{total} = (N_{16} \times S_{16}) + (N_{32} \times S_{32}) + (N_{64} \times S_{64})
$$

Substituting values:

$$
S_{total} = (60 \times 2) + (40 \times 4) + (20 \times 8)
$$

$$
S_{total} = 120 + 160 + 160 = 440 \text{ bytes}
$$

This is the **application-layer payload**.

---

**Step 4 — Add protocol overhead.**

The Modbus TCP overhead per poll transaction is 12 bytes. For $N_{poll} = 3$ tag groups polled (one per category), the total overhead is:

$$
S_{overhead} = N_{poll} \times 12 = 3 \times 12 = 36 \text{ bytes}
$$

> **Reasoning:** In practice, each tag-type may be polled as a separate Modbus request; the overhead is added per request. We assume 3 grouped requests here.

---

**Step 5 — Total bytes per second.**

$$
S_{sec} = (S_{total} + S_{overhead}) \times f_{poll}
$$

$$
S_{sec} = (440 + 36) \times 2 = 476 \times 2 = 952 \text{ bytes/second}
$$

---

**Step 6 — Convert to kbps.**

Since 1 byte = 8 bits:

$$
BW = \frac{952 \times 8}{1000} = \frac{7616}{1000} = 7.616 \text{ kbps}
$$

$$
\boxed{BW \approx 7.62 \text{ kbps}}
$$

---

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**

A standard **100 Mbps Ethernet** link can support $\frac{100{,}000}{7.62} \approx 13{,}123$ such HMIs simultaneously. This confirms that **bandwidth is rarely the bottleneck**; the **bottleneck is the PLC scan time and tag I/O latency**, not the HMI link. This insight is a common KTU follow-up question.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — HMI Function vs. Industrial Need

| HMI Function | Operator Need Addressed | Engineering Standard | Failure Mode if Missing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Visualisation** | Situational awareness | ISA 101.01 (HMI Design) | Operator blind to process; unsafe decisions |
| **Control** | Remote actuation | IEC 61131-3, IEC 61508 | Manual local operation, slow response |
| **Alarms** | Event-driven attention | ISA 18.2, EEMUA 191 | Unnoticed faults → equipment damage |
| **Trending** | Performance analysis | ISA 88, IEC 62890 | No root-cause data; quality drift undetected |
| **Security** | Authorisation & audit trail | IEC 62443 | Unauthorised access → safety & IP risk |
| **Logging** | Regulatory compliance | FDA 21 CFR Part 11, ISO 9001 | Audit failure; legal non-compliance |

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Operator Response Time (Human Factors)

For a critical alarm, the total operator response time is the sum of four cognitive sub-tasks:

$$
T_{resp} = T_{detect} + T_{recognise} + T_{decide} + T_{act}
$$

**Typical values (EEMUA 191 baseline):**
- $T_{detect} = 1.0 \text{ s}$ (visual detection of alarm)
- $T_{recognise} = 0.5 \text{ s}$ (reading the message)
- $T_{decide} = 2.0 \text{ s}$ (deciding corrective action)
- $T_{act} = 1.5 \text{ s}$ (pressing the button)

$$
T_{resp} = 1.0 + 0.5 + 2.0 + 1.5 = 5.0 \text{ s}
$$

For a high-priority alarm where the process variable changes at rate $\frac{dx}{dt}$, the **deflection** in the variable before operator action is:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{dx}{dt} \times T_{resp}
$$

If the safe limit is $x_{max}$, the safety margin is:

$$
\text{Margin} = x_{max} - x_{current} - \Delta x
$$

A **negative margin** indicates the operator cannot respond fast enough — the HMI design must then incorporate **automatic safety interlocks** (a frequent KTU design question).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The HMI is *not* a safety system by itself (per IEC 61511). Critical protection must be implemented in a **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** at the PLC/controller level, with the HMI only providing **indication**.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step — Alarm Priority Mapping (ISA 18.2 / EEMUA 191)

The following is the de-facto industrial standard for HMI alarm prioritisation:

1. **Priority 1 (Critical):** Immediate operator action needed; risk to life/equipment. Audible siren + red flash.
2. **Priority 2 (High):** Action needed within minutes; risk to production. Yellow flash + soft chime.
3. **Priority 3 (Medium):** Action within hours; performance degradation. Steady yellow.
4. **Priority 4 (Low):** Informational; no action needed. Steady blue/white.

EEMUA 191 recommends **no more than 150 displayed alarms per day** and **no more than 10 per hour** for sustainable operator workload. Exceeding these limits is called **"alarm flooding"** — a KTU essay topic.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Framework — HMI Architecture Styles

| Architecture | Description | Pros | Cons | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Standalone Panel** | Single HMI ↔ single PLC | Low cost, simple | No redundancy, no history | OEM machines |
| **PC-Based HMI** | Industrial PC + SCADA software | High graphics power | High cost, OS vulnerability | Large plants |
| **Web-Based HMI** | Browser-rendered screens | Remote access, no client install | Cybersecurity risk | Multi-site utilities |
| **Distributed HMI** | Multiple panels, shared database | Redundancy, scalability | Complex configuration | Refineries, power plants |
| **Mobile HMI** | Tablet/phone app | Operator mobility | Limited situational context | Field service |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — HMI in the Automation Stack

The following Mermaid block diagram shows the HMI's position and data flow within an industrial automation system.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L0["Level 0: Field Devices"]
        s1["Pressure Sensor"]
        s2["Temperature Sensor"]
        s3["Level Sensor"]
        a1["Motor Starter"]
        a2["Control Valve"]
    end

    subgraph L1["Level 1: Control Layer"]
        plc["PLC / DCS Controller"]
    end

    subgraph L2["Level 2: Supervisory Layer"]
        hmi["HMI Touch Panel"]
        scada["SCADA Server"]
        hist["Historian Database"]
    end

    subgraph L3["Level 3: Operations Layer"]
        mes["MES System"]
        erp["ERP / Business System"]
    end

    s1 --> plc
    s2 --> plc
    s3 --> plc
    plc --> a1
    plc --> a2
    plc <--> hmi
    plc <--> scada
    scada --> hist
    hmi --> scada
    scada --> mes
    mes --> erp
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The PLC sits at Level 1 and is the *only* device directly controlling the field.
- The HMI at Level 2 is connected **bidirectionally** to the PLC — it reads tags and writes commands.
- Data flows upward to MES and ERP, completing the **ISA-95 automation pyramid**.

---

## 4.2 Internal Architecture Block Diagram — Inside an HMI Panel

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CPU["HMI Processor Core"]
        cpu["ARM / x86 Processor"]
        ram["DDR Memory"]
        rom["Flash Storage"]
        os["Embedded OS / RTOS"]
    end

    subgraph IO["I/O Subsystem"]
        touch["Touch Controller"]
        lcd["LCD / TFT Display"]
        audio["Buzzer / Speaker"]
        key["Function Keys / LEDs"]
    end

    subgraph NET["Communication Subsystem"]
        eth["Ethernet Port 100/1000 Mbps"]
        ser["Serial RS-232/485"]
        usb["USB Host / Device"]
        fieldbus["Profibus / CAN Optional"]
    end

    subgraph PWR["Power Subsystem"]
        ps["24 V DC Isolated Supply"]
        batt["Backup Battery for RTC"]
    end

    ps --> CPU
    batt --> CPU
    cpu --> touch
    cpu --> lcd
    cpu --> audio
    cpu --> key
    cpu <--> eth
    cpu <--> ser
    cpu <--> usb
    cpu <--> fieldbus
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **CPU subsystem** executes the HMI runtime (e.g., WinCC Runtime, iX Developer, Crimson).
- The **I/O subsystem** handles operator interaction.
- The **Communication subsystem** is the *only* gateway to the outside world — making it a primary cyber-security perimeter.

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — HMI ↔ PLC Tag Polling Cycle

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant OP as Operator
    participant HMI as HMI Panel
    participant PLC as PLC Controller
    participant FD as Field Device

    OP->>HMI: Touch "START PUMP" button
    HMI->>PLC: Write Coil Address 0x001 = 1
    PLC->>FD: Energise Motor Contactor
    FD-->>PLC: Auxiliary contact closes
    PLC-->>HMI: Read Input 0x101 = 1 (running feedback)
    HMI-->>OP: Pump symbol turns GREEN
    Note over HMI,PLC: Periodic tag poll every 500 ms
    loop Every 500 ms
        HMI->>PLC: Read Holding Register 0x010
        PLC-->>HMI: Process value 45.6 degrees C
        HMI-->>HMI: Update trend chart
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **command path** (operator → HMI → PLC → field) is *one-shot* on operator action.
- The **feedback path** (PLC → HMI → operator display) is *continuous polling*.
- The 500 ms loop is the **scan cycle** that defines the HMI's perceived real-timeness.

---

## 4.4 State Machine — HMI Operator Session

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Login
    Login --> Authenticated: Valid credentials
    Login --> Locked: 3 failed attempts
    Locked --> [*]: Administrator reset
    Authenticated --> Monitor: Default screen
    Monitor --> Control: Touch command
    Control --> Monitor: Command acknowledged
    Monitor --> Alarm: Active alarm raised
    Alarm --> Monitor: Operator ACK
    Authenticated --> Logout: Session end
    Logout --> [*]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The HMI enforces a **finite-state machine** for each operator session.
- The **Locked state** is the cyber-security safeguard against brute-force login.
- The **Alarm state** overlays all other states (alarm can interrupt any screen).

---

## 4.5 Schematic — HMI Tag Address Mapping Table

| Logical Tag Name | Physical Address | Data Type | Direction | PLC Variable |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `PUMP_01_RUN` | `%I0.1` | BOOL | Read | Pump 1 running feedback |
| `PUMP_01_CMD` | `%Q0.1` | BOOL | Write | Pump 1 start command |
| `TANK_01_LEVEL` | `%MW100` | REAL (32-bit) | Read | Tank 1 level in % |
| `TEMP_01_PV` | `%MW102` | REAL (32-bit) | Read | Reactor temperature |
| `SETP_TEMP_01` | `%MW104` | REAL (32-bit) | Read/Write | Temperature setpoint |
| `ALARM_ACK` | `%M200` | BOOL | Write | Master alarm ACK |
| `HOUR_METER` | `%MD300` | DINT (32-bit) | Read | Motor runtime hours |

> [!NOTE]
> The `%I`, `%Q`, `%M`, `%MW`, `%MD` prefixes are **IEC 61131-3** standard address prefixes used by KTU-curriculum PLCs (Siemens S7 / Schneider M241 / Allen-Bradley ControlLogix). Mapping physical addresses to **symbolic HMI tags** is the *first* step in HMI project development.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Define Human-Machine Interface (HMI). List any four primary functions of an HMI in an industrial automation system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** A Human-Machine Interface (HMI) is the hardware-software operator panel that provides a graphical means for a human to monitor, control, and acquire data from an industrial process, typically interfacing with a PLC or DCS controller through industrial communication protocols.

> **Four Primary Functions (2 marks — ½ mark each):**
> 1. **Visualisation** — display of process mimic diagrams with real-time values.
> 2. **Control** — sending start/stop, setpoint, and mode commands to the controller.
> 3. **Alarm Management** — raising, displaying, acknowledging, and logging alarms.
> 4. **Data Logging & Trending** — historical archiving of process variables.

*(Alternative valid function: Security & Access Control.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between HMI and SCADA. State one industrial application where each is preferred.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | HMI | SCADA |
|---|---|---|
| **Scope (1 mark)** | Single station, local visualisation & control | Multi-station, plant-wide supervisory control |
| **Function (1 mark)** | Operator-facing graphical panel | Data acquisition + centralised control + historian |
| **Application (1 mark)** | A standalone CNC machine → **HMI** | An electrical substation with 30 feeders → **SCADA** |

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern Note:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and is divided into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students are given an internal choice between **Question A** and **Question B**.

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

#### (a) Explain the architecture of an industrial HMI with a neat block diagram. List the major components and their functions. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

An industrial HMI consists of the following major subsystems, organised in a layered architecture:

**1. Processor Subsystem (1.5 marks)**
- Central processing unit (ARM Cortex-A series or x86) executes the HMI runtime software.
- **RAM** holds dynamic screen objects and runtime data.
- **Flash memory** stores the project, OS, and logs.
- An **RTOS or Embedded Linux** manages multitasking.

**2. Display & Touch Subsystem (1.5 marks)**
- **LCD/TFT panel** (4"–22") renders the mimic graphics.
- **Touch controller** (resistive or projected capacitive) detects operator input.
- A **backlight** (LED) provides luminance; auto-dimming may be supported.

**3. Communication Subsystem (1.5 marks)**
- **Ethernet port** for Modbus TCP, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA.
- **Serial ports (RS-232/485)** for legacy Modbus RTU, MPI/DP.
- **USB** for project download and log export.
- Optional **fieldbus modules** (Profibus, CAN).

**4. Power Subsystem (1 mark)**
- **24 V DC isolated supply** (IEC 61131-2 compliant) with surge protection.
- **Backup battery** for RTC and retentive memory.

**5. Audio & Indicator Subsystem (1 mark)**
- **Buzzer/speaker** for alarm annunciation.
- **LEDs and function keys** for hardwired shortcuts.

**6. Enclosure (0.5 marks)**
- Industrial-grade **IP65 / NEMA 4X** front panel for harsh environments.

*(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes for the schematic.)*

---

#### (b) Discuss the design principles of an effective HMI screen as per ISA 101.01 standard. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **ISA 101.01 Human-Machine Interfaces for Process Automation Systems** standard prescribes the following design principles:

**1. Task-Based Organisation (1.5 marks)**
- Screens must be organised by the *operator's task*, not by the *control system's structure*.
- Example: a "Startup" screen, a "Normal Operation" screen, a "Shutdown" screen.

**2. Consistent Colour Coding (1.5 marks)**
- Red = danger/abnormal, Yellow = warning, Green = normal, Blue = informational.
- Avoid using red for "running" (a common but unsafe design error).

**3. Minimalist Mimic Diagrams (1.5 marks)**
- Use **P&ID-style** representations.
- Limit to **one primary process unit per screen** (per EEMUA 201).
- Avoid clutter; 30–50 % of the screen should be whitespace.

**4. Explicit Operator Action Confirmation (1 mark)**
- Critical commands must use a **two-step confirmation dialog**.
- Display the *consequence* of the action ("Starting Pump 1 — this will energise Motor M-101").

**5. Alarm Visibility (1 mark)**
- Alarms should be displayed in a **dedicated summary bar** at the top of the screen.
- The currently unacknowledged alarm count must always be visible.

**6. Consistency Across Screens (0.5 marks)**
- The same colour, font, and icon must mean the same thing on every screen.

> **Industry Reference:** Good HMI design reduces operator error by up to **60 %** and alarm floods by **80 %** (per ARC Advisory Group studies).

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### (a) With a neat diagram, explain the position of HMI in the ISA-95 automation pyramid. Discuss its interfaces with the PLC and SCADA layers. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **ISA-95 automation pyramid** defines five hierarchical levels of an industrial enterprise. The HMI occupies **Level 2 – Supervisory Control**.

**Level-wise breakdown (3 marks):**
- **Level 0 – Process:** Sensors, actuators, motors, valves.
- **Level 1 – Basic Control:** PLCs and DCS controllers performing closed-loop control.
- **Level 2 – Supervisory:** **HMI** and **SCADA servers** providing visualisation and centralised monitoring.
- **Level 3 – Operations Management:** MES (Manufacturing Execution Systems).
- **Level 4 – Enterprise:** ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning).

**Interfaces of HMI (4 marks):**

| Interface | Direction | Protocol Examples | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| HMI ↔ PLC | Bidirectional | Modbus TCP, Profinet, EtherNet/IP, OPC UA | HMI reads process tags and writes control commands |
| HMI ↔ SCADA | Bidirectional | OPC UA, SQL, proprietary | HMI can act as a SCADA client; SCADA aggregates data from multiple HMIs |
| HMI ↔ Field | Unidirectional (read) | 4–20 mA, HART (via PLC) | Indirect — HMI does not connect directly to field; PLC is intermediary |
| HMI ↔ MES | Upward | REST API, OPC UA, SQL | Production data, batch records, KPIs |

**Key Concept (1 mark):** The HMI is a *Level 2* device and must **never bypass** the Level 1 controller to directly control field devices — this would violate the safety-instrumented function principle of **IEC 61511**.

*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1.)*

---

#### (b) Explain the concept of alarm management as per ISA 18.2 / EEMUA 191. What is alarm flooding and how is it prevented? (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition of Alarm (1 mark)**
An alarm is an *audible and/or visible notification* of a process condition that requires timely operator response.

**2. Alarm Priority Classification (1.5 marks)**
Per ISA 18.2:
- **Priority 1:** Life/equipment safety; immediate action.
- **Priority 2:** Production safety; action within minutes.
- **Priority 3:** Equipment health; action within hours.
- **Priority 4:** Informational; no action.

**3. Alarm Lifecycle (1.5 marks)**
The four states of an alarm are:
1. **Raised** (active, unacknowledged).
2. **Acknowledged** (operator has seen it; condition still present).
3. **Cleared** (condition returned to normal; alarm removed).
4. **Suppressed** (intentionally hidden, e.g., during maintenance).

**4. EEMUA 191 Recommendations (1.5 marks)**
- Maximum **150 alarms per day** across the plant.
- Maximum **10 alarms per hour** per operator.
- Maximum **30 alarms on display** at any time.
- Average **Priority 1 alarm rate** of < 1 per shift.

**5. Alarm Flooding — Definition and Prevention (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** When the alarm rate exceeds the operator's manageable workload (> 10/hour), leading to missed alarms, operator stress, and possible plant incidents.
- **Prevention strategies:**
  - **Rationalisation** — review every alarm against a documented cause-and-consequence matrix.
  - **Deadbands and delays** — suppress nuisance alarms from process noise.
  - **Shelving** — temporarily hide non-critical alarms.
  - **Root-cause analysis** — eliminate the *source* of recurring alarms rather than silencing them.

> **Famous Industry Reference:** The **Buncefield Oil Storage Depot fire (2005, UK)** was partly attributed to alarm flooding — the operator could not respond to the critical high-level alarm due to 200+ nuisance alarms overwhelming the HMI.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HMI with SCADA** (loses 2 marks easily). Always clarify: HMI = *operator interface*; SCADA = *supervisory software system* that *uses* HMIs as client terminals.
> 2. **Writing "HMI controls the process"** — WRONG. HMI *sends commands* to the PLC; the PLC *controls* the process. This distinction is critical in 7-mark and 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Ignoring standards** — Always cite the relevant standard (ISA 18.2, ISA 101.01, IEC 62443, EEMUA 191) in your answer. Examiners award extra marks for standard references.
> 4. **Drawing a diagram without labelling** — A block diagram without labels gets only partial credit. Always label every block and every arrow.
> 5. **Skipping numerical examples** — When asked about bandwidth or response time, provide a *worked-out number* rather than just the formula. KTU examiners reward numerical rigour.
> 6. **Ignoring safety** — In any HMI answer, mention that **HMI is NOT a safety system**; safety must be implemented in the controller per IEC 61511.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1, Topic: HMI**

### 📌 Core Definitions
- HMI = Operator-facing hardware-software interface for monitoring and control.
- HMI sits at **Level 2** of the ISA-95 automation pyramid.
- HMI is **not** a safety system (per IEC 61511).

### 📌 Five Primary Functions
1. Visualisation (mimic diagrams)
2. Control (commands to PLC)
3. Alarm Management (ISA 18.2)
4. Data Logging & Trending
5. Security & Access Control (IEC 62443)

### 📌 Key Standards to Remember
- **ISA 18.2** — Alarm Management
- **ISA 101.01** — HMI Design
- **EEMUA 191** — Alarm Rationalisation
- **IEC 62443** — Industrial Cyber-security
- **IEC 61131-3** — PLC Programming Languages
- **IEC 61511** — Safety Instrumented Systems
- **ISA-95** — Automation Pyramid

### 📌 Key Hardware Specs
- **IP65 / NEMA 4X** front-panel rating
- **24 V DC** industrial supply
- **Touch:** Resistive (gloves ok) vs Capacitive (multi-touch)
- **MTBF > 50,000 hours**

### 📌 Key Protocols
- Modbus TCP / RTU
- Profinet / Profibus
- EtherNet/IP
- OPC UA (modern, secure)

### 📌 Common KTU Essay Triggers
- "Explain the architecture of an industrial HMI."
- "Discuss HMI design principles as per ISA 101.01."
- "Differentiate HMI, SCADA, and DCS."
- "Explain alarm management and the concept of alarm flooding."
- "Describe the role of HMI in the ISA-95 pyramid."

### 📌 Common Numerical Topics
- Bandwidth calculation for HMI ↔ PLC link.
- Operator response time decomposition.
- Touch latency budget.
- Alarm rate limits (EEMUA 191).

### 📌 Common Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Treating HMI as a control system.
- ❌ Confusing HMI with SCADA.
- ❌ Ignoring safety standards.
- ❌ Forgetting to label diagrams.
- ❌ Omitting standard citations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modbus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Modbus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Modbus** is an open, serial communication protocol originally published by **Modicon (now Schneider Electric)** in **1979** for use with its programmable logic controllers (PLCs). It has since become a *de facto* standard communication protocol in industrial automation due to its simplicity, openness, and royalty-free nature. Modbus enables communication between many devices connected to the same network — for example, a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) system talking to a Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) or a Human-Machine Interface (HMI) querying a sensor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Modbus is the foundational serial/industrial bus protocol students must master before progressing to fieldbuses (Profibus, Foundation Fieldbus) and Industrial Ethernet (Profinet, EtherCAT). Examiners frequently test frame structure, function codes, and CRC/LRC calculation.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context, Modbus belongs to the *Application Layer* of the **OSI model (Layer 7)**. It does not define a physical layer — it can be deployed over RS-232, RS-485, or TCP/IP networks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Waiter

Imagine a busy restaurant where you (the **Master / Client**) are seated at a table and need food from the kitchen (the **Slave / Server**). You don't go to the kitchen yourself. Instead, a waiter (**the Modbus protocol**) carries your written order slip to the kitchen and brings back the dish.

- Your **order slip** = a Modbus **frame** (PDU + addressing).
- The **waiter** = the communication medium (RS-485 or TCP/IP).
- The **kitchen stations** = Slaves with unique **Unit IDs (1–247)**.
- The **dish** = the response data (registers/coils).

Only one customer (master) talks at a time, and each kitchen station (slave) responds only when called. This is the **master–slave (request–response)** nature of Modbus.

## 1.3 Variants of Modbus

| Variant | Physical Layer | Encoding | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Modbus RTU** | RS-232 / RS-485 | Binary, compact | Industrial plants, factory floors |
| **Modbus ASCII** | RS-232 / RS-485 | ASCII hex characters | Legacy systems, slow links |
| **Modbus TCP / Modbus IP** | Ethernet (TCP/IP) | Binary over TCP | Modern SCADA, IIoT, cloud gateways |
| **Modbus Plus** | Proprietary token-passing | HDLC-like | High-speed Schneider networks |

> [!NOTE]
> The **PDU (Protocol Data Unit)** is identical across all variants (`Function Code + Data`). What differs is the **ADU (Application Data Unit)** — the additional addressing and error-checking layer wrapped around the PDU.

**Critical Constants (KTU Must-Know):**
- **Unit ID range:** 1–247 (0 = broadcast, 248–255 = reserved)
- **Function codes:** 1–127 (public), 128–255 = exception responses
- **Maximum data per PDU:** **253 bytes** for RTU/ASCII; **260 bytes** for TCP
- **Standard TCP port:** **502**
- **Baud rates (RTU):** 1200, 2400, 4800, 9600, 19200, 38400, 115200 bps
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Modbus Architecture — Master/Slave Principle

Modbus operates strictly on a **Master–Slave (Client–Server)** communication model:

1. The **Master** initiates all transactions.
2. A **Slave** listens silently on the bus and responds **only** when addressed.
3. A slave **cannot** initiate communication independently.
4. On RS-485 (multi-drop), only **one master** is permitted, but up to **247 slaves** can be addressed.
5. Broadcast messages (Unit ID = 0) are sent with no expected response.

> [!NOTE]
> This deterministic access pattern is what makes Modbus predictable and reliable for SCADA, but also limits real-time determinism — hence its evolution toward **Modbus TCP** and industrial Ethernet protocols.

## 2.2 Modbus Data Model — Four Primary Tables

Every Modbus device maps its internal memory into **four standardized tables**:

| Table | Type | Access | Address Range | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Coils** | 1-bit (Boolean) | Read/Write | 00001 – 09999 | Digital outputs (relays, valves) |
| **Discrete Inputs** | 1-bit | Read-only | 10001 – 19999 | Digital inputs (limit switches) |
| **Input Registers** | 16-bit | Read-only | 30001 – 39999 | Analog inputs (sensor data) |
| **Holding Registers** | 16-bit | Read/Write | 40001 – 49999 | Analog outputs, setpoints |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A single **register = 16 bits = 2 bytes**. Two consecutive registers can be combined to represent a 32-bit IEEE-754 floating-point value (Big-Endian or Little-Endian, configurable — a frequent KTU exam question).

## 2.3 Function Codes — The Modbus Vocabulary

The **function code (FC)** is a single byte that tells the slave *what action* to perform. Some essential KTU-mandated codes:

| FC (Decimal) | Hex | Name | Data Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 01 | 0x01 | Read Coils | Coils |
| 02 | 0x02 | Read Discrete Inputs | Discrete Inputs |
| 03 | 0x03 | Read Holding Registers | Holding Reg. |
| 04 | 0x04 | Read Input Registers | Input Reg. |
| 05 | 0x05 | Write Single Coil | Coils |
| 06 | 0x06 | Write Single Register | Holding Reg. |
| 15 | 0x0F | Write Multiple Coils | Coils |
| 16 | 0x10 | Write Multiple Registers | Holding Reg. |
| 23 | 0x17 | Read/Write Multiple Registers | Holding Reg. |

> [!WARNING]
> If the slave cannot fulfill a request (e.g., invalid FC or address), it returns an **Exception Response**: `Function Code + 0x80`, followed by an **Exception Code** (e.g., 01 = Illegal Function, 02 = Illegal Data Address, 03 = Illegal Data Value).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Definition | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **PDU size (RTU/ASCII)** | $\text{PDU}_{\max} = 253\ \text{bytes}$ | Fixed limit |
| **ADU size (RTU)** | $\text{ADU} = \text{UnitID} + \text{PDU} + \text{CRC}_{16}$ | $\leq 256$ bytes |
| **ADU size (TCP)** | $\text{ADU} = \text{MBAP}(7) + \text{PDU}$ | MBAP = Modbus Application Header |
| **RTU silent interval** | $t_{\text{silent}} \geq 3.5 \cdot T_{\text{char}}$ | $T_{\text{char}} = \dfrac{11}{B}$ (11 bits/char) |
| **Character time** | $T_{\text{char}} = \dfrac{11}{B}$ | $B$ = baud rate |
| **CRC-16 polynomial** | $G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$ | Polynomial: 0xA001 (reflected) |
| **LRC-8** | $\text{LRC} = -\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i\right] \mod 256$ | Two's-complement of byte sum |
| **TCP MBAP length field** | $\text{Length} = 1\ (\text{UnitID}) + \text{PDU}_{\text{size}}$ | Excludes itself (6 bytes before) |

> [!NOTE]
> $t_{\text{silent}}$ is the **3.5-character inter-frame delay** that separates Modbus RTU frames. Violating it causes the UART to mis-align the frame — a very common practical exam question.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **SCADA Systems:** Polling RTUs over RS-485 at 19.2 kbps.
- **Smart Energy Meters:** Reading holding registers (kWh, voltage) over Modbus TCP.
- **HVAC Controllers:** Writing setpoint values via FC 06 to variable-frequency drives (VFDs).
- **IIoT Gateways:** Translating Modbus RTU into MQTT/OPC-UA for cloud ingestion.
- **Solar Inverters:** Most expose Modbus maps for grid monitoring.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Modbus RTU Frame Structure — Step-by-Step Construction

A Modbus RTU frame (ADU) follows the layout:

$$ \text{ADU} = \underbrace{\text{Unit ID}}_{\text{1 byte}} + \underbrace{\text{PDU}}_{\text{FC + Data}} + \underbrace{\text{CRC-16}}_{\text{2 bytes, LE}} $$

### Worked Example — Read Holding Registers (FC 03)

**Request:** Master wants to read **2 holding registers** starting from address **40019 (0x0013)** of slave **0x11 (17)**.

**Step 1 — Identify the PDU:**

The PDU is the master request payload:
- **Function Code:** `0x03` (Read Holding Registers)
- **Starting Address Hi/Lo:** `0x00 0x13`
- **Quantity of Registers Hi/Lo:** `0x00 0x02`

$$
\text{PDU} = \underbrace{0x03}_{\text{FC}} + \underbrace{0x00\,0x13}_{\text{Addr}} + \underbrace{0x00\,0x02}_{N}
$$

**Step 2 — Assemble the full message (excluding CRC):**

$$
M = \underbrace{0x11}_{\text{Slave}} + \underbrace{0x03}_{\text{FC}} + 0x00\,0x13 + 0x00\,0x02
$$

Numerical byte stream:

$$ M = [0x11,\ 0x03,\ 0x00,\ 0x13,\ 0x00,\ 0x02] $$

**Step 3 — Compute CRC-16 (Modbus Polynomial 0xA001):**

Initialize:
$$ \text{CRC} = 0xFFFF $$

Process each byte sequentially using:

$$
\text{CRC}_{k+1} = \left[\left(\text{CRC}_k \oplus B_k\right)\ \&\ 0x0001\right] \neq 0\ \ ?\ \ \text{rotate\ \&\ XOR\ 0xA001} : \text{rotate}
$$

Walking through the bytes (tabulated below):

| Step | Byte $B_k$ | CRC In (hex) | Operation | CRC Out (hex) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Init | — | FFFF | — | FFFF |
| 1 | 11 | FFFF | XOR → FEEE, LSB=0, ROT → FDDD | FDDD |
| 2 | 03 | FDDD | XOR → FDDE, LSB=0, ROT → FBBC | FBBC |
| 3 | 00 | FBBC | XOR → FBBC, LSB=0, ROT → F778 | F778 |
| 4 | 13 | F778 | XOR → F76B, LSB=1, ROT+0xA001 → E0D6 | E0D6 |
| 5 | 00 | E0D6 | XOR → E0D6, LSB=0, ROT → C1AC | C1AC |
| 6 | 02 | C1AC | XOR → C1AE, LSB=0, ROT → 835C | 835C |

Final CRC = **0x835C**. Modbus transmits CRC **Little-Endian**: byte order becomes `0x5C 0x83`.

**Step 4 — Final transmitted frame:**

$$ \text{Frame} = [0x11,\ 0x03,\ 0x00,\ 0x13,\ 0x00,\ 0x02,\ 0x5C,\ 0x83] $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The receiving node computes the CRC over the entire received frame (including the CRC bytes). A *correct* frame yields a residue of **0x0000**. If not, the frame is **silently discarded** — Modbus has no built-in retransmission; that is the master's responsibility.

## 3.2 LRC Derivation for Modbus ASCII

For Modbus ASCII, the error check is **Longitudinal Redundancy Check (LRC-8)**.

**Step 1 — Sum all data bytes (excluding the leading `:` and trailing `CR LF`):**

$$
S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} D_i \mod 256
$$

**Step 2 — Two's complement:**

$$
\text{LRC} = (\sim S + 1) \mod 256 = -S \mod 256
$$

**Worked Example:** Data bytes `0x11 0x03 0x00 0x13 0x00 0x02`

$$
S = 0x11 + 0x03 + 0x00 + 0x13 + 0x00 + 0x02 = 0x29
$$

$$
\text{LRC} = -0x29 \mod 256 = 0xD7
$$

ASCII frame transmitted as: `: 1 1 0 3 0 0 1 3 0 0 0 2 D 7 CR LF`.

## 3.3 Modbus TCP — MBAP Header Derivation

Modbus TCP wraps the PDU with a **7-byte MBAP (Modbus Application Protocol) header** instead of CRC:

| Field | Size | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Transaction ID | 2 bytes | Echoed back for pairing request/response |
| Protocol ID | 2 bytes | Always `0x0000` for Modbus |
| Length | 2 bytes | Number of bytes **following** this field |
| Unit ID | 1 byte | Same as RTU Slave Address |

**Step 1 — Build PDU** (same as RTU):
$$ \text{PDU} = [0x03,\ 0x00,\ 0x13,\ 0x00,\ 0x02] $$

**Step 2 — Compute Length Field:**

$$ L = 1\ (\text{Unit ID}) + \text{PDU size} = 1 + 5 = 6 $$

**Step 3 — Construct MBAP:**

$$
\text{MBAP} = [T_{ID}^H,\ T_{ID}^L,\ 0x00,\ 0x00,\ 0x00,\ 0x06,\ \text{Unit ID}]
$$

**Step 4 — Final TCP payload:**

$$ \text{Frame}_{\text{TCP}} = \text{MBAP} + \text{PDU} = [Tx_H,\ Tx_L,\ 0x00,\ 0x00,\ 0x00,\ 0x06,\ 0x11,\ 0x03,\ 0x00,\ 0x13,\ 0x00,\ 0x02] $$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice **no CRC** is needed — the TCP layer already guarantees integrity via its own 16-bit checksum and reliable stream delivery.

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Property | Modbus RTU | Modbus ASCII | Modbus TCP |
|---|---|---|---|
| Encoding | Binary | ASCII hex | Binary |
| Error Check | CRC-16 | LRC-8 | TCP checksum |
| Delimiter | 3.5-char silence | `:` start, `CRLF` end | TCP stream |
| Throughput | Highest | Lowest (~½ of RTU) | Very High |
| Typical Use | RS-485 plants | Diagnostic terminals | Ethernet IIoT |
| OSI Layers | Layer 1, 2, 7 | Layer 1, 2, 7 | Layer 3, 4, 7 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Modbus Network Topology — RS-485 Multi-Drop

```mermaid
graph LR
    M[SCADA Master / HMI]:::master -->|RS-485 Bus| S1[Slave ID 1 - PLC]:::slave
    M --> S2[Slave ID 2 - VFD]:::slave
    M --> S3[Slave ID 3 - Energy Meter]:::slave
    M --> S4[Slave ID 4 - Sensor Hub]:::slave
    S1 -.->|Response only if polled| M
    S2 -.->|Response only if polled| M
    S3 -.->|Response only if polled| M
    S4 -.->|Response only if polled| M
    classDef master fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef slave fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed return arrows emphasize that slaves **never initiate** communication. They respond only when the master addresses their Unit ID. This is the hallmark of Modbus's deterministic master-slave paradigm.

## 4.2 Modbus RTU Transaction Flow — Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MA as Master
    participant BU as RS-485 Bus
    participant SL as Slave ID 0x11
    MA->>BU: 3.5 char silence
    MA->>BU: [0x11 0x03 0x00 0x13 0x00 0x02 CRC_LO CRC_HI]
    BU->>SL: Forward request
    SL->>SL: Validate CRC & Unit ID
    alt Valid Request
        SL->>BU: [0x11 0x03 0x04 Data1 Data2 Data3 Data4 CRC_LO CRC_HI]
        BU->>MA: Response frame
        MA->>MA: CRC check OK -> Process
    else Invalid FC / Address
        SL->>BU: [0x11 0x83 0x02] (Exception: Illegal Data Address)
        BU->>MA: Exception response
    end
    MA->>BU: 3.5 char silence -> Next transaction
```

## 4.3 Modbus Data Table Mapping — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph MT[Modbus Device Memory Map]
        C[Coils - 00001 to 09999 - 1 bit R W]:::coil
        DI[Discrete Inputs - 10001 to 19999 - 1 bit R]:::di
        IR[Input Registers - 30001 to 39999 - 16 bit R]:::ireg
        HR[Holding Registers - 40001 to 49999 - 16 bit R W]:::hreg
    end
    subgraph FC[Function Code Dispatcher]
        F1[FC 01 - Read Coils]:::fc
        F2[FC 02 - Read DI]:::fc
        F3[FC 03 - Read HR]:::fc
        F4[FC 04 - Read IR]:::fc
        F5[FC 05/15 - Write Coils]:::fc
        F6[FC 06/16 - Write HR]:::fc
    end
    FC --> MT
    classDef coil fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef di fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef ireg fill:#87CEFA,stroke:#1E90FF,color:#000
    classDef hreg fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
    classDef fc fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#BDB76B,color:#000
```

## 4.4 ADU Construction Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Application Data]:::app --> P[PDU - FC + Data]:::pdu
    P --> RTU[Add Unit ID - 1 byte]:::wrap
    P --> TCP[Add MBAP - 7 bytes]:::wrap
    RTU --> CRC[Compute CRC-16]:::crc
    CRC --> FRTU[Modbus RTU ADU]:::final
    TCP --> FTCP[Modbus TCP ADU]:::final
    classDef app fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef pdu fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    classDef wrap fill:#F5DEB3,stroke:#D2691E,color:#000
    classDef crc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the role of the Unit Identifier in a Modbus RTU frame. What value indicates a broadcast message?**

**Model Answer:**
The **Unit Identifier (UID)** is the first byte of every Modbus RTU frame and identifies the slave device the master is addressing. Valid range: **1–247**. UID = **0** denotes a **broadcast** message — the master sends it to all slaves simultaneously; no slave responds. Slaves process the PDU but never acknowledge a broadcast, so it is used only for write operations (FCs 05, 06, 0F, 10).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Modbus RTU and Modbus TCP error-checking mechanisms.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Modbus RTU** uses a **16-bit Cyclic Redundancy Check (CRC-16)** based on polynomial $G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$ (reflected: **0xA001**). It is computed over all bytes of the ADU and appended Little-Endian.
- **Modbus TCP** has **no application-layer CRC**, because **TCP itself** provides reliable delivery and a 16-bit Internet checksum. The MBAP header replaces framing/error-check functions.
- Thus Modbus RTU must perform end-to-end integrity checking; Modbus TCP relies on the transport layer.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat diagram, describe the **Modbus protocol stack** and explain the difference between **PDU** and **ADU**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A master sends the following Modbus RTU request to Slave ID **0x0A** to read **3 holding registers starting at address 0x0020**:

```
0A  03  00 20  00 03  ?? ??
```

Construct the complete RTU frame by computing the **CRC-16**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Modbus sits at the **Application Layer (Layer 7)** of the OSI model. It uses a simple **master–slave** request–response mechanism.

**PDU vs ADU:**
- **PDU (Protocol Data Unit):** Inner core consisting of **Function Code + Data**. Identical for RTU, ASCII, and TCP. Max **253 bytes**.
- **ADU (Application Data Unit):** PDU plus addressing and error-check fields, which differ per variant:
  - RTU → `Unit ID + PDU + CRC-16`
  - ASCII → `:` + hex-encoded PDU + LRC-8 + CRLF
  - TCP → MBAP (7 bytes) + PDU

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph LR
    APP[Application - SCADA / HMI] --> PDU[PDU - FC plus Data - 253 bytes max]:::pdu
    PDU --> RTU_A[RTU ADU - UnitID plus PDU plus CRC-16]:::rtu
    PDU --> ASCII_A[ASCII ADU - colon plus hex plus LRC plus CRLF]:::asc
    PDU --> TCP_A[TCP ADU - MBAP plus PDU]:::tcp
    classDef pdu fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,color:#000
    classDef rtu fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef asc fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef tcp fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,color:#000
```

**(b) CRC-16 Computation:**

Message bytes (before CRC):
$$ M = [0x0A,\ 0x03,\ 0x00,\ 0x20,\ 0x00,\ 0x03] $$

Initialize $\text{CRC} = 0xFFFF$. Apply the Modbus CRC-16 algorithm byte-by-byte:

| Step | Byte | CRC In | Operation | CRC Out |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0A | FFFF | XOR → FFF5, LSB=1, ROT+0xA001 | EFE0 |
| 2 | 03 | EFE0 | XOR → EFE3, LSB=1, ROT+0xA001 | C7C5 |
| 3 | 00 | C7C5 | XOR → C7C5, LSB=1, ROT+0xA001 | 878B |
| 4 | 20 | 878B | XOR → 87AB, LSB=1, ROT+0xA001 | 1757 |
| 5 | 00 | 1757 | XOR → 1757, LSB=1, ROT+0xA001 | 2EAE |
| 6 | 03 | 2EAE | XOR → 2EAD, LSB=1, ROT+0xA001 | 5D5C |

**Final CRC = 0x5D5C → transmitted Lo, Hi → `0x5C 0x5D`**

**Complete RTU Frame:**

$$ \boxed{[\,0x0A,\ 0x03,\ 0x00,\ 0x20,\ 0x00,\ 0x03,\ 0x5C,\ 0x5D\,]} $$

---

### Question B [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **four data tables** in Modbus with their address ranges, access types, and the function codes used to access them. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A SCADA master writes the value **0x00FF** to a single holding register at address **0x0005** of slave **0x07** using FC 06. Construct the Modbus RTU request frame and the expected normal response frame (include CRC). **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a)** Modbus standardizes four logical tables per device:

| Table | Address Range | Size | Access | FCs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coils | 00001 – 09999 | 1 bit | R / W | 01, 05, 15 |
| Discrete Inputs | 10001 – 19999 | 1 bit | Read-only | 02 |
| Input Registers | 30001 – 39999 | 16 bit | Read-only | 04 |
| Holding Registers | 40001 – 49999 | 16 bit | R / W | 03, 06, 16, 23 |

The leading digit (0, 1, 3, 4) is a **table prefix**. The actual on-wire address is the **lower 4 digits** (zero-based), e.g., register **40019** → on-wire address `0x0013`. FC 03 reads holding registers; FC 06 writes a single holding register; FC 16 writes multiple.

**(b) Frame Construction:**

**Request — Write Single Register (FC 06):**

- Slave ID: `0x07`
- FC: `0x06`
- Register Address Hi/Lo: `0x00 0x05`
- Register Value Hi/Lo: `0x00 0xFF`

Message before CRC:

$$ M = [0x07,\ 0x06,\ 0x00,\ 0x05,\ 0x00,\ 0xFF] $$

**Compute CRC-16:**

| Step | Byte | CRC In | CRC Out |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 07 | FFFF | C0C1 |
| 2 | 06 | C0C1 | 80C1 |
| 3 | 00 | 80C1 | 0180 |
| 4 | 05 | 0180 | 0C81 |
| 5 | 00 | 0C81 | 1902 |
| 6 | FF | 1902 | 2F80 |

**Final CRC = 0x2F80 → transmitted as `0x80 0x2F`**

**Master → Slave Request Frame:**

$$ \boxed{[\,0x07,\ 0x06,\ 0x00,\ 0x05,\ 0x00,\ 0xFF,\ 0x80,\ 0x2F\,]} $$

**Expected Normal Response (echoes the request):**

Modbus FC 06 returns the *exact same bytes* on success:

$$ \boxed{[\,0x07,\ 0x06,\ 0x00,\ 0x05,\ 0x00,\ 0xFF,\ 0x80,\ 0x2F\,]} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. **Address base confusion** — Modbus documentation uses **1-based** addresses (40001), but the **on-wire PDU uses 0-based** (0x0000). Always subtract 1. Marks lost here are the **#1 reason** students fail this question.
> 2. **CRC byte order** — Many students forget Modbus transmits CRC **Little-Endian** (Lo byte first). Swap the bytes or you lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Function code for exception** — If a request fails, the slave returns `FC | 0x80`, **not** the original FC. Students writing FC = 03 instead of 0x83 in exception examples lose 1 mark.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ Modbus is a **1979 Modicon** open protocol, sitting at **OSI Layer 7**, deployable over RS-232, RS-485, or TCP/IP.
- ✅ Three flavours: **RTU (binary+CRC)**, **ASCII (hex+LRC)**, **TCP (MBAP, port 502)**.
- ✅ Strict **Master–Slave** model: slaves never initiate communication. UID **0 = broadcast**.
- ✅ Four data tables: **Coils (RW bit), Discrete Inputs (R bit), Input Registers (R 16-bit), Holding Registers (RW 16-bit)**.
- ✅ Key function codes: **01, 02, 03, 04, 05, 06, 15 (0F), 16 (10)**.
- ✅ PDU max = **253 bytes**; registers = **16-bit**; float = two registers (Big/Little-Endian, device-dependent).
- ✅ CRC-16 polynomial = **0xA001 (reflected)**; transmitted **Little-Endian**; residue on success = **0x0000**.
- ✅ LRC-8 = two's complement of sum of bytes, appended as 1 byte.
- ✅ Modbus RTU requires **3.5-character silent interval** between frames.
- ✅ Exception response format: `FC + 0x80`, followed by exception code (01/02/03/...).
- ✅ Standard TCP port: **502**; MBAP header is **7 bytes** (TxID + Protocol ID + Length + UnitID).
