- ✅ No application-layer CRC in Modbus TCP — TCP checksum handles integrity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Profibus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Profibus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **PROFIBUS (Process Field Bus)** is an open, vendor-independent, serial communication standard widely used in industrial automation for high-speed, deterministic exchange of data between field devices (sensors, actuators) and higher-level controllers (PLCs, DCS, SCADA). It was originally standardized under **EN 50170** and **IEC 61158/61784**, and is governed by **PROFIBUS & PROFINET International (PI)**.

PROFIBUS is fundamentally a **token-passing / master-slave hybrid bus system** designed to replace the parallel, point-to-point 4–20 mA and 24 V signal wiring with a single, twisted-pair digital communication line. It supports **cyclic, acyclic, and event-driven** data traffic on the same physical medium.

The PROFIBUS family consists of three principal variants:

| Variant | Full Form | Primary Application Domain |
|---|---|---|
| **PROFIBUS DP** | Decentralized Periphery | Factory automation (fast I/O, drives) |
| **PROFIBUS PA** | Process Automation | Hazardous areas, process industries |
| **PROFIBUS FMS** | Fieldbus Message Specification | Deprecated (replaced by PROFINET) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Conference Call of Industrial Devices"**

Imagine a large factory floor where dozens of machines (motors, valves, sensors, robots) each need to talk to a central controller. In the old days, every single device had its **own dedicated wire** running back to the control room — imagine an office where every employee has a separate physical phone line just to whisper a single number. The room was a rat's nest of cables.

**PROFIBUS changes this.** Imagine instead, all devices share a **single telephone conference line**. But to avoid chaos, only one person is allowed to speak at a time. The controller (the **master**) gives each device (the **slave**) a precise **time slot** (token-passing or polling) to talk. The talking happens at very high speed using **digital binary pulses** instead of analog signals. Result: one cable, perfectly organized conversations, real-time control.

**Key real-world mappings:**
- **Twisted-pair cable** = the shared conference line.
- **Token / polling cycle** = the chairperson who hands the microphone around.
- **GSD file** = the "ID card" each device carries so the master knows what language it speaks.
- **Baud rate (up to 12 Mbps)** = the bandwidth / loudness of the conference.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are defined by the PROFIBUS specification and must be memorized:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Standard Baud Rates:** **9.6 kbps, 19.2 kbps, 45.45 kbps, 93.75 kbps, 187.5 kbps, 500 kbps, 1.5 Mbps, 3 Mbps, 6 Mbps, 12 Mbps**
> - **Maximum Stations per Segment:** **32** (addressable 0–127; with repeaters up to 126).
> - **Maximum Cable Length per Segment:** depends on baud rate (e.g., **1200 m at 9.6 kbps**, **100 m at 12 Mbps**).
> - **Transmission Medium:** **RS-485 twisted pair** (DP) or **IEC 61158-2** (PA, for intrinsic safety).
> - **Encoding:** **NRZ (Non-Return to Zero)** line code.
> - **Topology:** **Bus (line) topology** with optional stubs.
> - **Access Control:** **Token Passing among Masters + Master–Slave polling of slaves**.

---

## 1.4 Why PROFIBUS? (Engineering Motivation)

- **Reduced wiring cost** — up to **80 %** reduction compared to parallel point-to-point wiring.
- **Deterministic real-time** — guaranteed cycle times, ideal for closed-loop control.
- **Distributed I/O** — smart field devices can be placed hundreds of meters from the PLC, yet respond within microseconds.
- **Open standard** — multi-vendor interoperability.
- **Diagnostics** — continuous health monitoring of each node.

---

## 1.5 Course Outcome Alignment (KTU 2024)

> [!NOTE]
> This topic primarily addresses **CO1** of PERUT632: *"Understand the architecture and communication protocols used in advanced industrial automation systems."* It targets **Bloom Level 2 (Understand)** and **Bloom Level 3 (Apply)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The OSI Layer Mapping of PROFIBUS

PROFIBUS implements a streamlined subset of the **OSI 7-layer model**, focusing only on layers **1, 2, and 7**. This deliberate trimming is what gives it its speed.

| OSI Layer | PROFIBUS Implementation | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Layer 7 — Application | **DP-V0, DP-V1, DP-V2** (services) | Read/Write I/O, diagnostics, alarms |
| Layer 2 — Data Link | **FDL** (Fieldbus Data Link) | Token passing, frame structure, error detection |
| Layer 1 — Physical | **RS-485 / IEC 61158-2 / Fiber Optic** | Electrical signalling, encoding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Layers 3 to 6 are intentionally bypassed (the "**PROFIBUS slim stack**"), reducing latency dramatically compared to Ethernet-TCP/IP stacks.

---

## 2.2 PROFIBUS DP Architecture (the most-examined variant)

PROFIBUS DP supports three device classes:

1. **DP Master Class 1 (DPM1)** — central PLC that cycles I/O data with slaves.
2. **DP Master Class 2 (DPM2)** — engineering / diagnostic station (laptop, HMI).
3. **DP Slave** — distributed I/O block, valve, drive, sensor.

The communication proceeds in **cycles**:
- **Token phase** — the active master holds the token and may initiate traffic.
- **Poll phase** — the master sequentially polls each slave using **Request → Response** frames.

---

## 2.3 PROFIBUS Frame Structure (FDL Frame)

A PROFIBUS FDL frame consists of:

| Field | Length (bytes) | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **SYN** (synchronization) | 1 | Start delimiter (binary 0x68 or 0xA2) |
| **SD** (start delimiter) | 1 | Frame type identifier |
| **LE / LEr** (length) | 1 | Payload length |
| **DA** (destination address) | 1 | 0–127 |
| **SA** (source address) | 1 | 0–127 |
| **FC** (function code) | 1 | Service type (SRD, SDN, etc.) |
| **PDU / Data Unit** | 1–244 | User payload |
| **FCS** (frame check sequence) | 1 | CRC-8 checksum |
| **ED** (end delimiter) | 1 | 0x16 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **maximum PROFIBUS DP telegram length is 255 bytes total**, of which **244 bytes are user data**. This ceiling is a frequent KTU question.

---

## 2.4 Token Rotation Timing — Determinism

The most important real-time metric in PROFIBUS is the **token rotation time $T_{TR}$**. For a network with $N$ active masters and target rotation time $T_{TTR}$:

$$
T_{TR} \leq T_{TTR}
$$

The actual token holding time for a single master $i$ in a cycle is bounded by:

$$
T_{TH,i} = T_{ID,i} + \sum_{j=1}^{M_i} T_{MS,j}
$$

where:
- $T_{ID,i}$ = idle / gap time for master $i$,
- $M_i$ = number of slaves serviced by master $i$,
- $T_{MS,j}$ = time for one master–slave transaction.

The per-slave transaction time at baud rate $R$ for a payload of $L$ bytes is:

$$
T_{MS} = \frac{N_{bits}(L)}{R} + 2 \cdot T_{prop}
$$

where $N_{bits}(L)$ is the total frame bits (overhead + payload × 11 bits/byte for UART framing).

---

## 2.5 Maximum Cable Length vs Baud Rate

$$
L_{max} \approx \frac{v_{prop} \cdot t_{bit}}{2} \quad \text{(simplified, single-segment)}
$$

Empirically tabulated by PROFIBUS specification:

| Baud Rate | Max Segment Length | Max Stub Length |
|---|---|---|
| 9.6 – 93.75 kbps | **1200 m** | — |
| 187.5 kbps | **1000 m** | — |
| 500 kbps | **400 m** | **20 m** |
| 1.5 Mbps | **200 m** | **6.6 m** |
| 3 – 12 Mbps | **100 m** | **0 m** (no stubs) |

---

## 2.6 PROFIBUS PA — Process Automation Variant

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PA is physically implemented over **IEC 61158-2** (Manchester Bus Powered, MBP) at a fixed **31.25 kbps**. It supports **intrinsic safety (Ex-i)** for use in Zone 0/1 hazardous areas. Power and data share the **same two wires**. A **PA segment coupler / DP-PA coupler** is required to connect a PA segment to a DP backbone.

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Token Rotation Bound | $T_{TR} \leq T_{TTR}$ | ms | Real-time guarantee |
| 2 | Master Holding Time | $T_{TH,i} = T_{ID,i} + \sum T_{MS,j}$ | ms | Sum of all slave transactions |
| 3 | Frame Bits per Byte | $11$ bits/byte | bits | 8 data + start + stop + parity |
| 4 | Max Stations per Segment | $32$ | — | 0–127 addressable |
| 5 | Max Stations in Network | $126$ | — | With 4 repeaters |
| 6 | Max DP Telegram Size | $255$ bytes (244 data) | bytes | Fixed by IEC 61158 |
| 7 | Max Baud Rate | $12$ Mbps | bps | DP via RS-485 |
| 8 | PA Baud Rate | $31.25$ kbps | bps | Fixed |
| 9 | Cable Resistance (Type A) | $\leq 110\ \Omega/\text{km}$ | Ω/km | Loop resistance |
| 10 | Character Framing | $11$ bits per UART char | bits | 1S + 8D + 1P + 1Stop |

> [!NOTE]
> In exam answers, **always state the baud rate, cable length, and number of nodes** when describing a PROFIBUS configuration. Examiners award partial marks for these parameters.

---

## 2.8 Real-World Utility

PROFIBUS is the dominant fieldbus in **discrete manufacturing** (automotive, packaging, material handling) and a major player in **process industries** (chemical, oil & gas, pharmaceuticals). It is specified by **IEC 61158** (Type 3) and is the foundation for its Ethernet successor, **PROFINET**. Engineers encounter it when commissioning Siemens S7 PLCs, ABB drives, Endress+Hauser flowmeters, and Phoenix Contact remote I/O.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Per-Transaction Time for a PROFIBUS DP Slave

We now derive the time consumed when a Class 1 master exchanges **one cyclic I/O message** with a single DP slave.

### Step 1 — Identify the frame structure

A cyclic DP-V0 exchange uses two telegrams:

- **Request telegram (Request from Master)**: 11 bytes of overhead + payload $L$ bytes.
- **Response telegram (Response from Slave)**: 11 bytes of overhead + payload $L$ bytes.

The **overhead bytes** for a standard FDL telegram are:

$$
\text{Overhead} = \text{SYN} + \text{SD} + \text{LE} + \text{LEr} + \text{DA} + \text{SA} + \text{FC} + \text{FCS} + \text{ED} = 9\ \text{bytes}
$$

Add the **SDN/SRD preamble handling** (3 additional SYN bytes), giving **11 protocol bytes** per telegram.

### Step 2 — Convert bytes to bits

Each character on the wire is **11 bits** due to UART framing (1 start + 8 data + 1 parity + 1 stop):

$$
N_{bits} = 11 \times (\text{Total Bytes}) = 11 \times (11 + L)
$$

For the **bidirectional** request + response:

$$
N_{bits,\ total} = 11 \times (11 + L) + 11 \times (11 + L) = 22 \times (11 + L)
$$

### Step 3 — Compute transmission time

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{N_{bits,\ total}}{R} = \frac{22(11 + L)}{R}
$$

where $R$ is the baud rate in bits per second.

### Step 4 — Add propagation delay

The signal travels at approximately $v_{prop} = 2 \times 10^8\ \text{m/s}$ in copper. For a cable of length $\ell$:

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{\ell}{v_{prop}}
$$

There are two round-trip propagations (request forward + response return):

$$
T_{prop,\ total} = 2 \cdot \frac{\ell}{v_{prop}}
$$

### Step 5 — Combine

$$
\boxed{\;T_{MS} = \frac{22(11 + L)}{R} + \frac{2\ell}{v_{prop}}\;}
$$

This is the **per-slave transaction time** used in token-budget calculations.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-typical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:** A PROFIBUS DP network operates at **$R = 1.5\ \text{Mbps}$**. A master polls a slave that returns **$L = 4\ \text{bytes}$** of I/O data. The cable length to the slave is **$\ell = 100\ \text{m}$**. Compute the per-slave transaction time.

**Solution:**

Step (a) — Frame bits:

$$
N_{bits,\ total} = 22 \times (11 + 4) = 22 \times 15 = 330\ \text{bits}
$$

Step (b) — Transmission time:

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{330}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 2.20 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} = 220\ \mu s
$$

Step (c) — Propagation delay:

$$
T_{prop,\ total} = \frac{2 \times 100}{2 \times 10^8} = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 1\ \mu s
$$

Step (d) — Total transaction time:

$$
T_{MS} = 220\ \mu s + 1\ \mu s = \boxed{221\ \mu s}
$$

**Interpretation:** Even at 1.5 Mbps, each slave consumes roughly **0.22 ms** of bus time. For a 30-slave system, one full poll cycle ≈ **6.6 ms + master overhead**, well within typical 10 ms PROFIBUS cycle time targets.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Total Cycle Time for a Multi-Slave DP Network

Consider one DPM1 with $M$ slaves, each producing $L$ bytes of cyclic data.

### Step 1 — Sum per-slave times

$$
T_{cycle,\ payload} = \sum_{j=1}^{M} T_{MS,j}
$$

Assuming identical slaves and equal cable lengths:

$$
T_{cycle,\ payload} = M \cdot T_{MS}
$$

### Step 2 — Add token-passing overhead

For $N$ active masters, the token must circulate. The token-passing overhead between masters is:

$$
T_{tok} = (N - 1) \cdot T_{tok,\ gap}
$$

with $T_{tok,\ gap} \approx 5$ to $10$ slot times (a slot time ≈ $T_{bit}$ at idle = 1/R seconds).

### Step 3 — Add master-side processing latency

$$
T_{master} = M \cdot t_{proc}
$$

where $t_{proc}$ is the PLC scan overhead per slave (typically 100–500 µs).

### Step 4 — Combine

$$
\boxed{\;T_{cycle} = M \cdot T_{MS} + (N-1)\cdot T_{tok,\ gap} + M \cdot t_{proc}\;}
$$

This is the **bus cycle time** that engineers specify when designing a PROFIBUS DP network.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Which PROFIBUS Variant

| Criterion | PROFIBUS DP (RS-485) | PROFIBUS PA (IEC 61158-2) |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum baud rate | **12 Mbps** | **31.25 kbps** |
| Hazardous area use | Not intrinsically safe | **Yes (Ex-i)** |
| Power on bus lines | Separate | **Yes (bus-powered)** |
| Typical cable length | 100–1200 m | Up to 1900 m |
| Typical application | Factory automation | Process plants (chemical, pharma) |
| Coupler needed? | — | **DP-PA coupler required** |
| Cycle time | Sub-millisecond possible | ~10–100 ms typical |

---

## 3.5 GSD File — The "Device Driver" of PROFIBUS

> [!NOTE]
> The **General Station Description (GSD) file** is a plain-text electronic datasheet (typically `.gsd` or `.gsg`) that describes a slave device to the configuration tool. It contains:
> - Manufacturer name, device ID,
> - Supported baud rates,
> - Number/type of I/O modules,
> - Diagnostic information,
> - Default and parameter telegram formats.
>
> Before integration, the configuration tool (e.g., Siemens STEP 7 HW Config) merges all GSDs to build the **network topology map**.

---

## 3.6 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Checklist

> [!WARNING]
> - **Do NOT confuse PROFIBUS (fieldbus, deterministic) with PROFINET (Industrial Ethernet, TCP/IP-based).** They are **not the same** — PROFINET is the successor; PROFIBUS remains in service.
> - **PROFIBUS FMS is deprecated.** Do not recommend it in new designs.
> - The address range is **0–127**, not 0–255.
> - The maximum telegram is **255 bytes**, not 256.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**Q:** Define PROFIBUS. List any two of its variants and state one application of each. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

> **PROFIBUS (Process Field Bus)** is an open, vendor-independent, serial fieldbus standard (IEC 61158 / IEC 61784) used for digital communication between field devices and controllers in industrial automation.
>
> 1. **PROFIBUS DP (Decentralized Periphery):** Used in **factory automation** for high-speed distributed I/O, drives, and motor control (e.g., automotive assembly line).
> 2. **PROFIBUS PA (Process Automation):** Used in **process industries** (chemical, oil & gas, pharmaceutical) where **intrinsic safety (Ex-i)** is required; runs at **31.25 kbps** over IEC 61158-2.

---

### Question A2
**Q:** What is a **GSD file** in PROFIBUS? Why is it necessary? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> The **GSD (General Station Description)** file is a standardized electronic datasheet (text file with `.gsd` extension) supplied by every PROFIBUS device vendor. It contains the device identity, supported baud rates, number and type of I/O modules, diagnostic capabilities, and slot assignments.
>
> **Necessity:** During network configuration, the engineering tool (e.g., Siemens STEP 7) imports each slave's GSD to **auto-discover** the device parameters, build the bus topology, validate compatibility, and generate the GSD-conformant parameter telegram. Without GSDs, manual configuration of every slave would be impractical.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A — 14 Marks

**Q:** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of PROFIBUS DP**. Discuss the **OSI layer mapping**, **device classes (DPM1, DPM2, Slave)**, and the **cyclic communication mechanism** including token passing and polling. State any **four standard baud rates** supported by PROFIBUS DP. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### (a) Architecture and OSI Mapping — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

PROFIBUS DP (Decentralized Periphery) is optimized for **fast, cyclic, deterministic exchange** of I/O data between a central controller (PLC) and distributed field devices.

**OSI Layer Mapping:**

| OSI Layer | PROFIBUS DP Element |
|---|---|
| Layer 7 — Application | **DP-V0 (cyclic I/O), DP-V1 (acyclic parameter), DP-V2 (isochronous)** |
| Layer 2 — Data Link | **FDL — Fieldbus Data Link** (token passing, SRD/SDN services, CRC-8 error check) |
| Layer 1 — Physical | **RS-485 (twisted pair), fiber optic, or IEC 61158-2** |

Layers 3–6 are intentionally omitted to minimize latency — this is called the **"PROFIBUS slim stack"**.

**Device Classes:**

1. **DP Master Class 1 (DPM1)** — e.g., a Siemens S7-1500 PLC. Cycles the I/O data with assigned slaves within a fixed bus cycle time $T_{cycle}$.
2. **DP Master Class 2 (DPM2)** — e.g., an engineering laptop or HMI. Used for commissioning, diagnostics, and acyclic read/write of parameters.
3. **DP Slaves** — peripheral devices such as **ET200S remote I/O**, **Sinamics drives**, and **field instruments** that respond to master requests.

#### (b) Cyclic Communication & Standard Baud Rates — 7 Marks

**Cyclic Mechanism:**

1. The **active master** holds the **token** (a special FDL telegram granting exclusive bus access for a bounded time).
2. It then **polls each slave** using the **SRD (Send and Request Data)** service — request and response in one transaction.
3. After servicing all its slaves, the master **passes the token** to the next active master in the logical ring, or starts a new cycle if it is the only master.
4. **Cycle time** is bounded by $T_{cycle} \leq T_{TTR}$ (target rotation time), guaranteeing **determinism**.

**Four Standard Baud Rates:**
- **9.6 kbps**
- **187.5 kbps**
- **1.5 Mbps**
- **12 Mbps** (maximum)

*(Any four from the supported set: 9.6 k, 19.2 k, 45.45 k, 93.75 k, 187.5 k, 500 k, 1.5 M, 3 M, 6 M, 12 M bps are acceptable.)*

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Class 1 Master PLC] ---+
                         |
[Class 2 Master HMI] ----+====[PROFIBUS DP Cable RS485]====
                         |        |        |        |
                       [Slave1] [Slave2] [Slave3] [Slave N]
                       (I/O)    (Drive)  (Valve)  (Sensor)
```

Bus terminators (220 Ω) are placed at **both physical ends**.

---

### Question Choice B — 14 Marks (Alternative)

**Q:** Differentiate between **PROFIBUS DP and PROFIBUS PA**. Explain the **PROFIBUS DP frame structure** with the function of each field. A DP master polls **30 slaves** at **1.5 Mbps**; each slave exchanges **8 bytes** of I/O data over a **maximum segment length of 200 m**. Estimate the **minimum bus cycle time**. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### (a) DP vs PA — 7 Marks

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | PROFIBUS DP | PROFIBUS PA |
|---|---|---|
| Full form | Decentralized Periphery | Process Automation |
| Physical layer | **RS-485** (twisted pair) | **IEC 61158-2 (MBP)** |
| Baud rate | **9.6 kbps – 12 Mbps** (variable) | **31.25 kbps** (fixed) |
| Cable length | 100–1200 m | Up to 1900 m |
| Intrinsic safety | No (use barriers for Ex) | **Yes — bus-powered Ex-i** |
| Power on bus | Separate 24 V supply | **Power + data on same 2 wires** |
| Connection to DP | — | Via **DP-PA coupler / link** |
| Typical use | Factory automation, drives | Chemical, pharma, oil & gas |
| Cycle time | Sub-ms to ms | 10–100 ms |

**Frame Structure of PROFIBUS DP:**

```
+------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+----------+-----+-----+
| SYN  | SD  | LE  | LEr | DA  | SA  | FC  | PDU Data | FCS | ED  |
+------+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+-----+----------+-----+-----+
  1B    1B    1B    1B    1B    1B    1B    1-244 B    1B    1B
```

**Function of each field:**

- **SYN — Synchronization:** Allows receivers to lock onto the baud rate.
- **SD — Start Delimiter:** Distinguishes frame types (e.g., 0x68 = SRD token-less; 0xA2 = token frame).
- **LE / LEr — Length:** Length of the PDU (payload), repeated for redundancy.
- **DA — Destination Address:** Address (0–127) of the intended receiver.
- **SA — Source Address:** Sender's address (0–127).
- **FC — Function Code:** Identifies the service — e.g., SRD (Send and Request Data), SDN (Send Data No Ack).
- **PDU — Protocol Data Unit:** User payload, **1 to 244 bytes**.
- **FCS — Frame Check Sequence:** CRC-8 checksum for error detection.
- **ED — End Delimiter:** Marks end of frame (0x16).

#### (b) Cycle Time Estimation — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $R = 1.5 \times 10^6$ bps
- $M = 30$ slaves
- $L = 8$ bytes
- $\ell = 200$ m (max cable length)

**Step 1 — Bits per transaction:**

From the formula derived in §3.1:

$$
N_{bits} = 22 \times (11 + L) = 22 \times (11 + 8) = 22 \times 19 = 418\ \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Transmission time per slave:**

$$
T_{tx} = \frac{418}{1.5 \times 10^6} = 2.787 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{s} \approx 278.7\ \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Propagation delay (round trip):**

$$
T_{prop} = \frac{2 \times 200}{2 \times 10^8} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s} = 2\ \mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Per-slave transaction time:**

$$
T_{MS} = 278.7\ \mu s + 2\ \mu s = 280.7\ \mu s
$$

**Step 5 — Total cycle for 30 slaves:**

$$
T_{cycle,\ payload} = 30 \times 280.7\ \mu s = 8.42\ \text{ms}
$$

**Step 6 — Add overhead (inter-frame gaps + master processing):**

Assume $t_{proc} = 0.3\ \text{ms per slave}$ and 10 slot-times of idle gap per cycle ($10 / 1.5 \times 10^6 \approx 6.67\ \mu s$, negligible):

$$
T_{cycle} \approx 8.42 + 30 \times 0.3 = 8.42 + 9.0 \approx 17.4\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;T_{cycle,\ min} \approx 8.4\ \text{ms (bus payload only)}\;,\quad \approx 17\ \text{ms (with PLC overhead)}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** This is well within a typical **10–20 ms** PROFIBUS DP target cycle time, confirming feasibility at 1.5 Mbps.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks on PROFIBUS questions:**
> 1. **Confusing PROFIBUS with PROFINET.** Examiners deduct **2–3 marks** outright if a student calls PROFIBUS an "Ethernet protocol" or claims it runs on TCP/IP.
> 2. **Wrong address range.** Many students write "0–255" — correct answer is **0–127**.
> 3. **Wrong telegram length.** Often quoted as 256 bytes; correct is **255 bytes (244 data)**.
> 4. **Forgetting baud-rate / length trade-off.** Always state the cable length when giving a baud rate — they are coupled.
> 5. **Skipping the bus terminator.** A block diagram missing the 220 Ω terminators loses a diagram mark.
> 6. **Forgetting DP ≠ PA.** PA uses IEC 61158-2 at 31.25 kbps and is intrinsically safe — many confuse it with DP-FMS.
> 7. **No units in numerical answers.** Always append **µs, ms, m, kbps, Mbps**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PROFIBUS**
>
> - **Full form:** **PRO**cess **FI**eld**BUS** (standardized in IEC 61158 / IEC 61784).
> - **Three main variants:** **DP** (factory), **PA** (process, Ex-i), **FMS** (deprecated).
> - **Three device classes:** **DPM1** (cyclic I/O master), **DPM2** (engineering), **Slave** (remote I/O / drive / valve).
> - **OSI layers used:** 1, 2, and 7 only — the **slim stack** for speed.
> - **Physical medium:** **RS-485 twisted pair** for DP; **IEC 61158-2** for PA.
> - **Baud rates (DP):** up to **12 Mbps**; PA fixed at **31.25 kbps**.
> - **Max stations per segment:** **32**; up to **126** with repeaters.
> - **Max DP telegram:** **255 bytes total / 244 bytes data**.
> - **Max cable length:** **1200 m at 9.6 kbps** down to **100 m at 12 Mbps**.
> - **Access method:** **Token passing among masters + master-slave polling (SRD)**.
> - **Encoding:** **NRZ** line code, **11 bits/character** UART framing.
> - **Bus terminator:** **220 Ω** at both ends of every segment.
> - **GSD file:** vendor-supplied electronic datasheet required for integration.
> - **Real-time guarantee:** $T_{cycle} \leq T_{TTR}$ — token rotation bound.
> - **Cycle-time formula:** $T_{cycle} \approx M \cdot \left[\dfrac{22(11+L)}{R} + \dfrac{2\ell}{v_{prop}}\right] + M \cdot t_{proc}$.
> - **PA coupling:** Always via a **DP-PA coupler / segment coupler** — PA cannot connect directly to a DP master.
> - **PROFIBUS ≠ PROFINET** — PROFIBUS is RS-485 based; PROFINET is Industrial Ethernet.
> - **11 bits/byte, 22 bytes total transaction overhead per master-slave exchange** — derive it, don't memorize blindly.
> - **Always quote units** (kbps / Mbps, m, µs / ms) in numerical answers.
> - **Examiner loves:** block diagrams with terminators, OSI layer table, baud-rate vs length table.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ethernet/IP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Ethernet/IP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Ethernet/IP** (Ethernet Industrial Protocol) is an open industrial networking standard that employs conventional **IEEE 802.3 Ethernet** hardware combined with the **Common Industrial Protocol (CIP)** at the application layer. The protocol was originally released by **Rockwell Automation** (Allen-Bradley) and is currently maintained by the **Open DeviceNet Vendors Association (ODVA)**. It uses **TCP/IP** for explicit (client–server) messaging and **UDP/IP** for implicit (I/O / real-time) messaging.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Industrial Communication Protocols):**
> Ethernet/IP is grouped under the *Industrial Ethernet* family, alongside Profinet, EtherCAT, and Modbus TCP. Students must be able to distinguish its **producer–consumer model** from the older producer–consumer models used by DeviceNet and ControlNet, and to identify its two CIP transport classes (Class 1 and Class 3).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postal System" View

Imagine a giant national postal service:

| Postal Element | Ethernet/IP Equivalent |
|----------------|------------------------|
| Highways & trucks | Standard Ethernet (Cat-5e/6 cable, switches, 100/1000 Mbps) |
| Postal sorting office | TCP/UDP transport layer (port 0xAF12 for CIP) |
| Specialised parcel format | **CIP (Common Industrial Protocol)** envelope |
| Time-sensitive parcels (red mail) | **Implicit (I/O) messages** — UDP, cyclic |
| Registered letters (blue mail) | **Explicit messages** — TCP, request/reply |
| Registered customer ID card | **Connection ID (CID)** |
| Building's mailroom logbook | **CIP Object Model** (Class, Instance, Attribute) |

In simple words: **Ethernet/IP is "regular Ethernet carrying industrial letters written in CIP."** The roads and trucks are nothing special — it is the *content format* and the *delivery rules* that make it fit for the factory floor.

### 1.3 Standard Constants and Metrics (to remember verbatim)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard Speed:** **10 / 100 / 1000 Mbps** (Gigabit variants now common)
> - **Default CIP Port:** **0xAF12** (44818 decimal) for both TCP and UDP
> - **Encapsulation Header Size:** **24 bytes**
> - **Typical I/O Cycle Time:** **1 ms – 100 ms** (application dependent)
> - **Maximum Nodes (per segment):** Limited by Ethernet switch port count (typically **254** logical devices per IP subnet)
> - **Governing Standards:** **IEEE 802.3** + **CIP Volume 1/5** (ODVA)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Protocol Stack — Layered Architecture

Ethernet/IP follows the classical **OSI 5-layer (sometimes shown as 7-layer) model** with CIP sitting squarely on top:

1. **Physical Layer:** 10BASE-T / 100BASE-TX / 1000BASE-T copper, or 100BASE-FX / 1000BASE-LX fibre. Switched, full-duplex Ethernet only (no legacy hubs/half-duplex in modern deployments).
2. **Data Link Layer:** IEEE 802.3 MAC with **Quality of Service (QoS)** tags (IEEE 802.1Q) used to prioritise implicit I/O frames.
3. **Network Layer:** Standard **IPv4** (and increasingly IPv6). Static or DHCP-assigned IP addressing.
4. **Transport Layer:**
   - **TCP** — for *Explicit* (point-to-point, request/response, configuration) messaging.
   - **UDP** — for *Implicit* (I/O data, multicast, time-critical) messaging.
5. **Application Layer:** **CIP** (Common Industrial Protocol) — provides a unified object-oriented messaging model shared with DeviceNet and ControlNet.

### 2.2 CIP Object Model — The Heart of Ethernet/IP

Every CIP node exposes data through an **Object → Class → Instance → Attribute** hierarchy:

- **Class** — a *template* defining a type of functionality (e.g., *Identity Object* = Class 0x01, *Message Router* = Class 0x02, *Assembly Object* = Class 0x04).
- **Instance** — a *concrete occurrence* of that class (e.g., Identity Object, Instance 1).
- **Attribute** — an *actual data field* inside the instance (e.g., Vendor ID = Attribute 1).
- **Service** — the *action* requested on an object (e.g., Get_Attribute_Single = 0x0E, Set_Attribute_Single = 0x10).

> [!TIP]
> **The "memory trick" KTU examiners love:**
> *CIP = CISA → **C**lass, **I**nstance, **S**ervice, **A**ttribute.*

### 2.3 Two Message Types — Explicit vs Implicit

| Feature | Explicit Messaging | Implicit Messaging |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | **TCP** (reliable) | **UDP** (best-effort) |
| Direction | Point-to-point | Multicast (1 → many) |
| Use Case | Configuration, diagnostics, parameter upload/download | Real-time I/O (cyclic) |
| Model | Client–Server (request/response) | **Producer–Consumer** |
| Cycle | Acyclic (on demand) | Cyclic (e.g., every 5 ms) |
| Data payload | Variable; CIP service + data | Fixed, pre-configured I/O assembly |

### 2.4 Connection Classes in CIP

CIP defines four connection types, but Ethernet/IP uses mainly two:

- **Class 1 (I/O Connection):** Real-time I/O data, established via UDP, uses **Connection ID (CID)** for fast hardware-level dispatch.
- **Class 3 (Explicit Message Connection):** Used for programming, diagnostics, configuration over TCP.
- *Class 0 & 2* — present in DeviceNet/ControlNet; not used in Ethernet/IP.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- Widely used in **discrete manufacturing** (automotive body shops, packaging lines).
- **CIP Safety** and **CIP Motion** extensions ride on the same Ethernet/IP backbone.
- **CIP Sync** (IEEE 1588-based) enables sub-microsecond synchronisation between drives.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference Table)

| # | Quantity | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes / Typical KTU Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Effective Throughput** | $T = \dfrac{N_b \times 8}{t_{cycle}}$ | bits per second (bps) | $N_b$ = payload bytes per cycle |
| 2 | **Network Cycle Time (approx.)** | $t_{cycle} \approx N \cdot t_{proc} + t_{prop} + t_{switch}$ | milliseconds (ms) | $N$ = number of nodes |
| 3 | **Wire Time per Frame** | $t_{frame} = \dfrac{L_{frame} \times 8}{R_{link}}$ | seconds | $L_{frame}$ = frame length in bits |
| 4 | **Theoretical Maximum Frame Rate** | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{t_{frame}}$ | frames per second | At 100 Mbps, 64-byte frame $\approx 148{,}800$ fps |
| 5 | **Baud / Bandwidth Requirement** | $B = f_{cycle} \times L_{frame} \times N$ | bits per second | $f_{cycle}$ = I/O update rate |
| 6 | **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | $RTT = 2 \times (t_{prop} + t_{proc})$ | seconds | Important for explicit messaging |
| 7 | **Inter-Packet Gap (IEEE 802.3)** | $IPG = 96$ **bit-times** | — | Constant, must be added to $t_{frame}$ |
| 8 | **Maximum Devices / Subnet** | $2^8 - 2 = 254$ (IPv4) | devices | Excludes gateway & broadcast |

> **Where these formulas matter in practice (KTU-relevant industrial scenarios):**
> 1. Sizing an Ethernet switch port buffer for a 32-node I/O ring.
> 2. Determining whether a 100 Mbps link is sufficient for a 5 ms cyclic update of 256-byte assemblies from 16 drives.
> 3. Estimating the longest permissible cable run to keep $t_{prop}$ within a 1 ms cycle budget.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Anatomy of an Encapsulated CIP Message

A CIP message travelling over Ethernet/IP is *enveloped* by an **Encapsulation Header (24 bytes)**. The complete frame on the wire is built up step by step as follows.

> **Preamble (7 bytes) + SFD (1 byte) → MAC Header (14 bytes) → IP Header (20 bytes) → UDP Header (8 bytes) → Encapsulation Header (24 bytes) → CIP Message (variable) → FCS (4 bytes).**

Let $L_{total}$ be the total on-wire length in bytes. Then:

$$
L_{total} = L_{pre} + L_{mac} + L_{ip} + L_{udp} + L_{encap} + L_{cip} + L_{fcs}
$$

**Step-by-step substitution:**

$$
L_{total} = 8 + 14 + 20 + 8 + 24 + L_{cip} + 4
$$

$$
L_{total} = 78 + L_{cip} \;\; \text{bytes}
$$

**Convert to bits for wire-time calculation:**

$$
L_{total}^{(bits)} = (78 + L_{cip}) \times 8
$$

**Add the mandatory 96-bit Inter-Packet Gap:**

$$
L_{wire} = (78 + L_{cip}) \times 8 + 96 \;\; \text{bits}
$$

For a typical CIP explicit message of 32 bytes data inside a 50-byte CIP payload:

$$
L_{wire} = (78 + 50) \times 8 + 96 = 1024 + 96 = 1120 \;\; \text{bits}
$$

> **Logic of each line:** Preamble + SFD is fixed Ethernet synchronisation. MAC is the layer-2 addressing. IP+UDP are the transport envelopes. The 24-byte Encapsulation Header tells the receiver *"this is a CIP packet on port 0xAF12."* Finally, the CRC (FCS) protects against bit errors. The 96-bit IPG is required by IEEE 802.3 to allow the PHY to recover between frames.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Wire Time and Theoretical Frame Rate

The **wire time** is the time a single frame physically occupies the medium:

$$
t_{frame} = \frac{L_{wire}}{R_{link}}
$$

**Substituting** $L_{wire}$ from above and a 100 Mbps link ($R_{link} = 100 \times 10^6$ bps):

$$
t_{frame} = \frac{1120}{100 \times 10^6} = 11.2 \;\; \mu s
$$

The **theoretical maximum frame rate** (no other traffic, no store-and-forward delay) is:

$$
f_{max} = \frac{1}{t_{frame}} = \frac{1}{11.2 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 89{,}286 \;\; \text{frames/s}
$$

> **Real-world correction:** Cut-Through switching reduces store-and-forward delay to a few microseconds, but QoS scheduling and TCP acknowledgements reduce $f_{max}$ by 20–40 % in practice.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — End-to-End Cycle Time for an Implicit I/O Network

For a cyclic producer–consumer network with $N$ devices, each producing one multicast I/O frame per cycle, the *minimum achievable cycle time* is:

$$
t_{cycle}^{min} = N \cdot t_{frame} + N \cdot t_{switch} + t_{prop} + t_{margin}
$$

**Each term explained:**

- $N \cdot t_{frame}$ — total wire time for all $N$ frames to be transmitted serially.
- $N \cdot t_{switch}$ — store-and-forward delay at each switch hop (typically $5$–$10\;\mu s$ per hop for cut-through switches).
- $t_{prop}$ — propagation delay in the cable: $t_{prop} = \dfrac{d \times \sqrt{\varepsilon_r}}{c}$, where $d$ = cable length, $\varepsilon_r \approx 1.5$ for PE insulation, $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. For 100 m, $t_{prop} \approx 0.5\;\mu s$ — usually negligible.
- $t_{margin}$ — application safety margin (jitter, software jitter, typically 10–20 %).

**Numerical example (KTU-style problem):**
A production line uses **16 drives** updating I/O every cycle. Each I/O frame is **128 bytes** (CIP payload). Link rate is **100 Mbps**. Each switch hop adds **7 µs** store-and-forward delay. Maximum distance is **80 m**. Choose a realistic safety margin of **15 %**.

**Step 1 — Compute $L_{wire}$ for 128-byte CIP payload:**

$$
L_{wire} = (78 + 128) \times 8 + 96 = 1648 + 96 = 1744 \;\; \text{bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $t_{frame}$:**

$$
t_{frame} = \frac{1744}{100 \times 10^6} = 17.44 \;\; \mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Compute switch and propagation delays:**

$$
N \cdot t_{switch} = 16 \times 7\;\mu s = 112\;\mu s
$$

$$
t_{prop} = \frac{80 \times \sqrt{1.5}}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 0.33\;\mu s
$$

**Step 4 — Sum and add margin:**

$$
t_{cycle}^{base} = 16 \times 17.44\;\mu s + 112\;\mu s + 0.33\;\mu s = 279.04\;\mu s + 112\;\mu s + 0.33\;\mu s \approx 391\;\mu s
$$

$$
t_{cycle}^{min} = 391\;\mu s \times 1.15 \approx 450\;\mu s = 0.45 \;\; ms
$$

> **Conclusion:** A 1 ms cycle time is *comfortably achievable* for this configuration. KTU students should remember that Ethernet/IP is **not deterministic by design** — it is **best-effort deterministic**; real-time guarantees come from network design (DLR rings, QoS, bounded switch delays), not from the protocol itself.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Ethernet/IP vs Sibling Industrial Protocols)

| Parameter | Ethernet/IP | Profinet | EtherCAT | Modbus TCP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Governing Body | ODVA | PI (Profibus) | Beckhoff / ETG | Schneider / Modbus.org |
| Transport | TCP + UDP | TCP + UDP (RT/IRT) | Ethernet frame "on-the-fly" | TCP only |
| Real-Time Class | Soft RT | RT / IRT (≤ 1 ms) | Hard RT (≤ 100 µs) | Soft RT |
| Cycle Time (typical) | 1 – 10 ms | 0.25 – 10 ms | 0.05 – 1 ms | 5 – 100 ms |
| Topology | Star / DLR ring | Star / ring | Line / ring | Star |
| Model | Producer–Consumer (CIP) | Producer–Consumer | Master–Slave (processing-on-the-fly) | Client–Server |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Ethernet/IP Protocol Stack (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER CIP OBJECT MODEL"]
    msgRouter["MESSAGE ROUTER CLASS 0x02"]
    assemblyObj["ASSEMBLY OBJECT CLASS 0x04"]
    connObj["CONNECTION OBJECT CLASS 0x05"]
    transLayer["TRANSPORT LAYER"]
    tcpProto["TCP EXPLICIT MESSAGING"]
    udpProto["UDP IMPLICIT IO MESSAGING"]
    netLayer["NETWORK LAYER IPv4 IPv6"]
    dllayer["DATA LINK LAYER IEEE 8023 QoS 8021Q"]
    physLayer["PHYSICAL LAYER 100BASETX 1000BASET"]
    appLayer --> msgRouter
    appLayer --> assemblyObj
    appLayer --> connObj
    msgRouter --> transLayer
    transLayer --> tcpProto
    transLayer --> udpProto
    tcpProto --> netLayer
    udpProto --> netLayer
    netLayer --> dllayer
    dllayer --> physLayer
```

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — CIP Object Addressing Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["NODE A PLC SCANNER"]
    cls["CLASS NUMBER e.g. 0x04 Assembly"]
    inst["INSTANCE NUMBER e.g. 101"]
    attr["ATTRIBUTE NUMBER e.g. 03 Data"]
    svc["SERVICE CODE e.g. 0x0E Get Attribute Single"]
    nodeA --> cls
    cls --> inst
    inst --> attr
    nodeA --> svc
```

> **How to read it:** To fetch *Data* from *Instance 101* of the *Assembly Class* on a remote node, the scanner sends: `Service = 0x0E`, `Class = 0x04`, `Instance = 101`, `Attribute = 3`.

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Class 1 Implicit I/O Connection Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PLC as Scanner PLC
    participant DRV as Drive I O Node
    PLC->>DRV: Forward Open Class 1 UDP
    DRV-->>PLC: Forward Open Success CID Assigned
    loop Cyclic at RPI e.g. 5 ms
        DRV->>PLC: Implicit Produce Multicast UDP CID
        PLC->>DRV: Implicit Consume Acknowledge
    end
    PLC->>DRV: Forward Close UDP Tear Down
    DRV-->>PLC: Forward Close Success
```

> **KTU Take-away:** The connection is opened *once* with an explicit TCP message; thereafter, the cyclic I/O runs on UDP using only the **Connection ID (CID)** — no extra headers, no acknowledgement overhead. This is what gives Ethernet/IP its high throughput.

### 4.4 Diagram 4 — Typical Ethernet/IP Star / DLR Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    scada["SCADA HMI STATION"]
    plc1["PLC 192 168 1 10"]
    drv1["DRIVE 1 192 168 1 21"]
    drv2["DRIVE 2 192 168 1 22"]
    drv3["DRIVE 3 192 168 1 23"]
    ioBlock["REMOTE IO BLOCK 192 168 1 30"]
    ringSw["DLR MANAGER SWITCH"]
    scada --- ringSw
    plc1 --- ringSw
    drv1 --- ringSw
    drv2 --- ringSw
    drv3 --- ringSw
    ioBlock --- ringSw
    ringSw -. DLR RING .-> ringSw
```

> **DLR** = *Device Level Ring*. ODVA-defined; provides sub-3 ms ring recovery without external switches.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define Ethernet/IP. List the two transport protocols it uses and the role of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Ethernet/IP is an open industrial networking standard that uses standard IEEE 802.3 Ethernet hardware with the Common Industrial Protocol (CIP) at the application layer. **(1 mark)**
It uses:
- **TCP** for **explicit (point-to-point) messages** used in configuration and diagnostics. **(1 mark)**
- **UDP** for **implicit (I/O) messages** used in real-time cyclic producer–consumer data exchange. **(1 mark)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is the CIP object model? Explain Class, Instance and Attribute with one example each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The CIP object model organises all node data into a four-level hierarchy: Class, Instance, Attribute and Service. **(1 mark)**
- **Class** — a template defining a type of object. Example: *Assembly Object* (Class ID 0x04). **(1 mark)**
- **Instance** — a specific occurrence of that class. Example: *Output Assembly, Instance 101.* **(0.5 mark)**
- **Attribute** — a data field inside the instance. Example: *Data attribute (Attribute 3) holding the process variable.* **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Ethernet/IP protocol stack** and the role of the **Encapsulation Header**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between **Explicit** and **Implicit** messaging in Ethernet/IP, with one industrial example for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Protocol Stack (7 marks):**
- Diagram showing the five layers: Physical (IEEE 802.3 100BASE-TX), Data Link (MAC + IEEE 802.1Q QoS), Network (IPv4), Transport (TCP/UDP), Application (CIP). **(3 marks)**
- Explanation of the **24-byte Encapsulation Header**: it contains the **Command Code** (e.g., 0x0065 = SendRRData), **Length**, **Session Handle**, **Status**, **Sender Context**, and **Options**. It tells the receiver *"this is a CIP message destined for port 0xAF12."* **(2 marks)**
- Mention that the same CIP application layer is shared with DeviceNet and ControlNet — only the transport layer differs. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Explicit vs Implicit (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Explicit (3.5 marks) | Implicit (3.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP, port 0xAF12 | UDP, port 0xAF12 |
| Direction | Point-to-point | Multicast |
| Use | Configuration, diagnostics | Cyclic I/O |
| Reliability | Acknowledged | Best-effort, hardware-level CID dispatch |
| Example | Upload program to a Logix PLC | Drive sending torque setpoint every 1 ms |

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Supplementary 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **CIP object model** with a diagram. How is a typical CIP request addressed using Class–Instance–Attribute–Service? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A production cell has **8 EtherNet/IP drives**. Each drive produces **64 bytes of cyclic I/O data**. The link rate is **100 Mbps**, switch delay per node is **8 µs**, and propagation delay is negligible. Compute the **minimum cycle time** with a 20 % safety margin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) CIP Object Model (7 marks):**
- Description of the CISA hierarchy with one real example (e.g., Identity Object Class 0x01, Instance 1, Attribute 1 = Vendor ID). **(3 marks)**
- Diagram showing the addressing path: *Class 0x04 → Instance 101 → Attribute 3 → Service 0x0E (Get_Attribute_Single).* **(2 marks)**
- Mention the role of the **Message Router object (Class 0x02)** which dispatches the service to the correct object within the node. **(2 marks)**

**(b) Cycle-time calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total frame size on wire:**

$$
L_{wire} = (78 + 64) \times 8 + 96 = 1136 + 96 = 1232 \;\; \text{bits}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Wire time per frame:**

$$
t_{frame} = \frac{1232}{100 \times 10^6} = 12.32 \;\; \mu s
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 3 — Switch and propagation delays:**

$$
N \cdot t_{switch} = 8 \times 8\;\mu s = 64\;\mu s
$$

**(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Base cycle time:**

$$
t_{cycle}^{base} = 8 \times 12.32\;\mu s + 64\;\mu s = 98.56\;\mu s + 64\;\mu s = 162.56\;\mu s
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step 5 — Apply 20 % safety margin:**

$$
t_{cycle}^{min} = 162.56\;\mu s \times 1.20 \approx 195.07\;\mu s \approx 0.195\;\; ms
$$

**(2 marks)**

**Conclusion:** A **1 ms cyclic update** is *easily achievable*. Even a **200 µs** cycle is feasible with a 100 Mbps Ethernet/IP link and 8 drives.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the 96-bit Inter-Packet Gap (IPG)** when computing $t_{frame}$. Marks deducted in Part B numerics.
> 2. **Confusing TCP and UDP roles** — examiners *always* test "which one for I/O?". Memorise: *I/O = UDP, Config = TCP.*
> 3. **Writing "CIP = ControlNet"** — CIP is the *common* application layer shared by three networks: DeviceNet, ControlNet, and Ethernet/IP. This is a 2-marker trap.
> 4. **Calling Ethernet/IP "deterministic"** — it is *best-effort*; only EtherCAT and IRT-class Profinet are truly hard real-time. Use the phrase *"bounded latency under design conditions."*
> 5. **Skipping the Encapsulation Header** in protocol-stack diagrams. It is the *defining* feature of Ethernet/IP vs raw CIP-over-Ethernet.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Ethernet/IP = Standard Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) + CIP on top.**
- **Two transports:** TCP for **explicit** (configuration), UDP for **implicit** (I/O).
- **CIP model = Class → Instance → Attribute, addressed by Service code.** (Memory hook: **C-I-A-S**.)
- **Encapsulation Header = 24 bytes, port 0xAF12 (44818).**
- **Class 1** = I/O connection; **Class 3** = explicit message connection.
- **Producer–consumer** model — one producer multicasts to N consumers using a single CID.
- **Cycle time formula:** $t_{cycle} = N \cdot t_{frame} + N \cdot t_{switch} + t_{prop} + t_{margin}$.
- **Wire time formula:** $t_{frame} = \dfrac{(78 + L_{cip}) \times 8 + 96}{R_{link}}$.
- **Maximum devices per IP subnet = 254** (IPv4).
- **Ethernet/IP is *not* deterministic by default** — deterministic behaviour comes from network design (DLR, QoS, cut-through switches, bandwidth reservation).
- **Safety & motion extensions** (CIP Safety, CIP Motion, CIP Sync) ride on the same Ethernet/IP infrastructure.
- **DT:** *DLR* (Device Level Ring) provides sub-3 ms ring recovery.
- **DT:** *QoS* (IEEE 802.1Q) tagging prioritises implicit I/O over explicit traffic.
- **DT:** *Hardware-level CID dispatch* — the Ethernet NIC strips only the 24-byte header and copies the rest via DMA, achieving microsecond-level I/O throughput.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Feedforward Control, Cascade Control, Model Predictive Control (MPC)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Feedforward Control, Cascade Control, Model Predictive Control (MPC)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Advanced Process Control — Setting the Stage

In any modern industrial plant (refineries, petrochemical complexes, paper mills, power stations, pharmaceutical batch reactors), a single-loop **PID feedback controller** is often insufficient. As processes become tightly coupled, multivariable, constrained, and subject to measurable disturbances, we need **Advanced Process Control (APC)** strategies.

Module 2 of PERUT632 introduces three cornerstone APC strategies that build on classical feedback control:

1. **Feedforward Control (FFC)** — Acts *before* a disturbance hits the process.
2. **Cascade Control (CC)** — Uses *two nested loops* to handle disturbances faster.
3. **Model Predictive Control (MPC)** — Uses a *predictive model + optimizer* with constraints.

---

## 1.2 Feedforward Control (FFC) — The Crystal Ball Controller

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Feedforward Control is a control strategy in which the principal disturbance(s) affecting a process variable are measured, and their effects are compensated mathematically before they propagate through the process, by manipulating a corrective input variable. Unlike feedback control, FFC is based on the process model rather than on the error signal.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The Shower Thermometer

Imagine you are taking a shower. Someone flushes a toilet upstairs, and the **cold-water pressure drops momentarily**, making the shower scalding hot.

- A **feedback** controller is you standing in the shower, feeling the temperature with your hand, and adjusting the hot-water knob *after* you get burned. (Reactive, slow.)
- A **feedforward** controller is you *hearing* the toilet flush, knowing from experience that the cold-water pressure will drop, and *immediately* turning down the hot-water knob *before* the temperature changes. (Predictive, fast.)

The key ingredients of FFC are:
1. **Measurement of the disturbance** $d(t)$ (here, the sound of flushing / cold-water pressure).
2. **A process model** that tells us how $d$ will affect the output $y$.
3. **An inverse model** (the "feedforward compensator") that computes exactly how to move the manipulated variable $m(t)$ to cancel the disturbance effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Limitation:** Feedforward control is **only as good as the process model and disturbance measurement**. If the model is wrong, or if a disturbance is *unmeasured*, feedforward leaves a residual error — hence it is almost always combined with a feedback loop (Feedback + Feedforward = **Feedback-Feedforward Combined Control**).

---

## 1.3 Cascade Control (CC) — The Master–Servant Strategy

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Cascade Control is a multi-loop control structure in which the output of one (primary/master) controller serves as the setpoint of another (secondary/slave) controller. The secondary loop is designed to be faster than the primary loop and acts on an intermediate process variable that directly affects the controlled variable of the primary loop.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The Car Cruise Control with Engine Governor

Consider a car's cruise control maintaining a set speed (say, 100 km/h).

- A **single-loop** cruise controller would only look at the wheel speed sensor.
- A **cascade** version uses an *inner loop*: the engine's ECU (Engine Control Unit) controls the **throttle position / fuel injection** based on a *torque demand* signal. The *outer loop* (cruise control) sends a *torque demand setpoint* to the ECU.

So:
- **Outer (Master) controller** → Cruise control → Setpoint = 100 km/h → Output = Torque demand.
- **Inner (Slave) controller** → ECU → Setpoint = Torque demand → Output = Throttle angle.
- **Secondary (intermediate) variable** = Engine RPM / Torque (faster responding than vehicle speed).
- **Primary variable** = Vehicle speed.

The inner loop rejects disturbances (hills, headwinds) *much faster* than the outer loop could.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selection Rule for Secondary Variable:** The secondary variable must (a) be measurable, (b) be directly causally linked to the primary variable, and (c) have a **faster time constant and smaller dead time** than the primary loop.

---

## 1.4 Model Predictive Control (MPC) — The Look-Ahead Optimizer

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> *Model Predictive Control (MPC) is an advanced control methodology that uses an explicit dynamic model of the process to predict the future evolution of the controlled variables over a finite prediction horizon. At each sampling instant, an optimization problem is solved to compute a sequence of future control moves that minimizes a cost function (typically tracking error and control effort) subject to constraints on inputs, outputs, and states. Only the first move of the optimal sequence is applied; at the next instant, the horizon is shifted (receding/rolling horizon principle), and the procedure is repeated.*

### Conceptual Analogy — The GPS Navigator

When you drive from Kochi to Bengaluru, your **GPS navigation app** is doing exactly what MPC does:

1. **Predictive Model:** The GPS knows the road map, traffic patterns, and your car's likely speed.
2. **Prediction Horizon:** It does not just look at the next 100 m; it looks 50 km ahead.
3. **Optimization:** It computes the *best* route (minimum time, considering traffic, tolls, road closures).
4. **Constraint Handling:** It avoids toll roads if you set "avoid tolls," and avoids highways if you set "avoid highways."
5. **Receding Horizon:** After every kilometre, it *re-plans* from your *new* position.
6. **Only First Move Applied:** It tells you "turn left in 200 m" — not "drive straight for 50 km."

Similarly, MPC looks ahead, optimizes, applies only the *current* optimal move, and re-plans at every sampling instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why MPC dominates industry:** Traditional PID cannot systematically handle **constraints** (valve saturation, temperature limits, product specs) or **multivariable interactions** (one manipulated variable affecting multiple controlled variables). MPC handles both natively. It is the *de facto* standard APC layer in refineries (DMC, Honeywell), petrochemicals (IDCOM, HIECON → AspenTech), and increasingly in advanced manufacturing.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Feedforward Control — Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Block Diagram Reduction

Consider a linear SISO process with:
- Manipulated variable $m(t)$ → Process $G_p(s)$ → Output $y(t)$.
- Disturbance $d(t)$ → Process $G_d(s)$ → adds to output $y(t)$.

**Without feedforward (feedback only):**
$$Y(s) = G_p(s) M(s) + G_d(s) D(s)$$

**With feedforward controller $G_{ff}(s)$ added:**
$$Y(s) = \underbrace{G_p(s) G_{ff}(s)}_{\text{FF path through MV}} D(s) + G_d(s) D(s)$$

$$Y(s) = \left[ G_p(s) G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s) \right] D(s)$$

### 2.1.2 Ideal Feedforward Controller (Perfect Cancellation)

For **perfect disturbance rejection**, the FF path must exactly cancel the disturbance path:
$$G_p(s) G_{ff}^{\text{ideal}}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

$$\boxed{G_{ff}^{\text{ideal}}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}}$$

This is called the **inverse of the disturbance-to-output dynamics relative to the manipulated-to-output dynamics**.

### 2.1.3 Static Feedforward (Lead-Lag Approximation)

In practice, a full dynamic inverse is rarely implemented. Instead, a **lead-lag** block is used:
$$G_{ff}(s) = -K_{ff} \frac{\tau_{\text{lead}} s + 1}{\tau_{\text{lag}} s + 1}$$

where:
- $K_{ff} = -G_d(0) / G_p(0)$ = **static gain** (steady-state ratio).
- $\tau_{\text{lead}}$ = lead time constant (compensates dead time / right-half-plane zeros).
- $\tau_{\text{lag}}$ = lag time constant (compensates extra lag in $G_p$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** A *static* feedforward (just the gain) gives perfect steady-state rejection. A *dynamic* feedforward (lead-lag) is required when disturbances enter with a *different dynamic* than the manipulated variable path.

### 2.1.4 Practical Issues
- **Model mismatch** → residual error; hence FF is combined with FB.
- **Unmeasured disturbances** → FF cannot act; FB must handle them.
- **Bumpless transfer** required when switching between manual/auto.

---

## 2.2 Cascade Control — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Block Structure

Two controllers in series:
- **Primary (master)** controller $G_{c1}$ — sees error $e_1 = r_1 - y_1$.
- **Secondary (slave)** controller $G_{c2}$ — setpoint is output of $G_{c1}$; sees error $e_2 = r_2 - y_2$.
- $y_2$ = secondary (intermediate) variable; $y_1$ = primary (controlled) variable.
- $G_{p2}(s)$ = inner process (MV → secondary variable).
- $G_{p1}(s)$ = outer process (secondary variable → primary variable).
- $d_2$ = disturbance entering inner loop.
- $d_1$ = disturbance entering outer loop.

### 2.2.2 Closed-Loop Transfer Functions

Inner loop (secondary) closed-loop:
$$G_{c2,\text{cl}}(s) = \frac{G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s)}{1 + G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s)}$$

Outer loop response (primary variable to setpoint):
$$\frac{Y_1(s)}{R_1(s)} = \frac{G_{c1}(s) \, G_{c2,\text{cl}}(s) \, G_{p1}(s)}{1 + G_{c1}(s) \, G_{c2,\text{cl}}(s) \, G_{p1}(s)}$$

### 2.2.3 Tuning Procedure (Step-by-Step)

1. Put **primary controller in MANUAL** (open outer loop).
2. Tune the **secondary loop first** (faster loop, more aggressive — typically P or PI).
3. Close the secondary loop in AUTO.
4. Tune the **primary loop** using the secondary loop as a "fast actuator" (typically PI or PID, less aggressive).
5. Verify disturbance rejection for both $d_1$ and $d_2$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cascade is effective ONLY against disturbances that enter the inner loop** ($d_2$). Disturbances entering the outer loop ($d_1$) are still handled only by the primary controller — no improvement from cascade.

---

## 2.3 Model Predictive Control — Theoretical Framework

### 2.3.1 Three Core Elements
1. **Prediction Model** — a state-space, transfer-function, or step-response model of the process.
2. **Cost Function (Objective)** — penalizes tracking error and control effort.
3. **Receding Horizon Policy** — apply first move, shift horizon, repeat.

### 2.3.2 Prediction Horizon & Control Horizon

- **Prediction Horizon $P$** = number of future time steps over which output is predicted.
- **Control Horizon $M$** = number of future control moves to be optimized ($M \le P$).
- After $M$ moves, the control is held constant.

### 2.3.3 Cost Function (Quadratic Form)

$$J = \sum_{j=1}^{P} \left\| \hat{y}(k+j \mid k) - r(k+j) \right\|_Q^2 + \sum_{j=0}^{M-1} \left\| \Delta u(k+j) \right\|_R^2$$

where:
- $\hat{y}(k+j \mid k)$ = predicted output $j$ steps ahead, given information at time $k$.
- $r(k+j)$ = reference trajectory.
- $\Delta u(k+j) = u(k+j) - u(k+j-1)$ = control *increment* (penalized to encourage smooth moves).
- $Q \ge 0$ = output weighting matrix (positive semi-definite).
- $R > 0$ = control-effort weighting matrix (positive definite).
- $\|\cdot\|_W^2 = (\cdot)^\top W (\cdot)$.

### 2.3.4 Constraints Handled by MPC

| Constraint Type | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|
| Input amplitude | $u_{\min} \le u(k+j) \le u_{\max}$ |
| Input rate (slew) | $\Delta u_{\min} \le \Delta u(k+j) \le \Delta u_{\max}$ |
| Output bounds | $y_{\min} \le \hat{y}(k+j) \le y_{\max}$ |
| State bounds | $x_{\min} \le x(k+j) \le x_{\max}$ |

### 2.3.5 Receding Horizon Algorithm (Step-by-Step)

At each sampling instant $k$:
1. **Measure / estimate** current state $x(k)$.
2. **Predict** outputs over horizon $k+1, \dots, k+P$ using the model.
3. **Solve** the constrained QP/LP: $\min_{\Delta u(k),\dots,\Delta u(k+M-1)} J$.
4. **Apply** only the first move $u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u^*(k)$.
5. **Shift** the horizon: $k \leftarrow k+1$; go to step 1.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units | Key Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Feedforward Controller | $G_{ff}^{\text{ideal}}(s) = -G_d(s)/G_p(s)$ | $G_d, G_p$ = disturbance & process TFs | Requires exact process model |
| 2 | Static FF Gain | $K_{ff} = -G_d(0)/G_p(0)$ | Dimensionless ratio | Steady-state rejection |
| 3 | Dynamic FF (Lead-Lag) | $G_{ff}(s) = -K_{ff} (\tau_{\text{lead}} s + 1)/(\tau_{\text{lag}} s + 1)$ | $\tau_{\text{lead}}, \tau_{\text{lag}}$ in **seconds** | Approximate inverse |
| 4 | Cascade Inner-Loop CL | $G_{c2,\text{cl}} = G_{c2}G_{p2}/(1+G_{c2}G_{p2})$ | Standard feedback CL | $G_{c2}$ includes controller + actuator |
| 5 | Cascade Outer-Loop CL | $Y_1/R_1 = G_{c1} G_{c2,\text{cl}} G_{p1} / (1 + G_{c1} G_{c2,\text{cl}} G_{p1})$ | Closed-loop TF | Inner loop must be stable & fast |
| 6 | MPC Cost Function | $J = \sum_{j=1}^{P} \Vert \hat{y}(k+j)-r \Vert_Q^2 + \sum_{j=0}^{M-1} \Vert \Delta u \Vert_R^2$ | $Q \ge 0, R > 0$ | $M \le P$ |
| 7 | MPC Input Constraint | $u_{\min} \le u(k+j) \le u_{\max}$ | Bounds in engineering units | Hard constraint |
| 8 | MPC Output Constraint | $y_{\min} \le \hat{y}(k+j) \le y_{\max}$ | Product spec / safety | Hard constraint |
| 9 | MPC Slew-Rate | $\Delta u_{\min} \le \Delta u(k+j) \le \Delta u_{\max}$ | Rate limit in units/s | Protects actuators |
| 10 | Receding Horizon Shift | $k \to k+1$, re-optimize | Discrete-time index | Re-planning at every $T_s$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cross-link to industrial practice:**
> - **Feedforward** is used in boiler drum-level control (feedwater flow FF on steam demand) and HVAC (outdoor temperature FF).
> - **Cascade** is the workhorse in temperature control (slave = furnace fuel flow, master = reactor temperature).
> - **MPC** is used in distillation columns (multi-MV/multi-CV), FCC units, ethylene crackers, and increasingly in HVAC building optimization.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Ideal Feedforward Controller (from First Principles)

We start with the standard 2x2 block diagram. The output $Y(s)$ is the sum of two paths:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s)\, M(s) + G_d(s)\, D(s)$$

**Step 1:** Introduce the feedforward controller $G_{ff}(s)$ in the disturbance path. The disturbance now drives both $G_d$ and $G_{ff} \to G_p$:

$$M(s) = G_{ff}(s)\, D(s)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $M(s)$ into the output equation:

$$Y(s) = G_p(s)\, G_{ff}(s)\, D(s) + G_d(s)\, D(s)$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $D(s)$:

$$Y(s) = \left[ G_p(s) G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s) \right] D(s)$$

**Step 4:** Define the *disturbance-to-output transfer function in the presence of FF*:

$$\frac{Y(s)}{D(s)}\bigg|_{FF} = G_p(s) G_{ff}(s) + G_d(s)$$

**Step 5:** Impose the *ideal* condition — complete disturbance rejection: $Y(s) = 0$ for any $D(s) \ne 0$. This requires:

$$G_p(s) G_{ff}^{\text{ideal}}(s) + G_d(s) = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $G_{ff}^{\text{ideal}}(s)$:

$$\boxed{G_{ff}^{\text{ideal}}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)}}$$

**Step 7 (Physical Interpretation):** The feedforward controller computes the *negative inverse* of the disturbance dynamics (as seen at the output) relative to the manipulated variable dynamics. In other words, it pre-shapes the manipulated variable so that its effect on $y$ exactly cancels the disturbance's effect on $y$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Static Feedforward Gain from a Heat Exchanger Example

Consider a shell-and-tube heat exchanger where:
- $m$ = steam flow rate (MV), affects outlet temperature $T$.
- $d$ = inlet process flow rate $F$ (disturbance).

Assume both paths are first-order:

$$G_p(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}, \qquad G_d(s) = \frac{K_d}{\tau_d s + 1}$$

**Step 1:** Ideal dynamic FF controller:

$$G_{ff}^{\text{ideal}}(s) = -\frac{G_d(s)}{G_p(s)} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p} \cdot \frac{\tau_p s + 1}{\tau_d s + 1}$$

**Step 2:** Identify static gain, lead, and lag terms:

$$K_{ff} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p}, \qquad \tau_{\text{lead}} = \tau_p, \qquad \tau_{\text{lag}} = \tau_d$$

**Step 3 (Steady-state limit):** Let $s \to 0$:

$$G_{ff}(0) = -K_{ff} = -\frac{K_d}{K_p}$$

**Step 4 (Numerical example):** Suppose $K_p = 5~^\circ\text{C}/(\text{kg/s})$, $K_d = -2~^\circ\text{C}/(\text{kg/s})$ (more flow → less heating time → cooler outlet). Then:

$$K_{ff} = -\frac{(-2)}{5} = +0.4 \; \text{(kg steam)/(kg process flow)}$$

**Step 5 (Interpretation):** For every 1 kg/s increase in process flow, the feedforward controller *adds* 0.4 kg/s of steam, in the right direction to keep outlet temperature constant.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cascade Control Stability via Inner-Loop Reduction

We have an inner loop with $G_{c2}(s) = K_{c2}$ (P-controller, simplest case) and $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{K_{p2}}{\tau_{p2} s + 1}$.

**Step 1:** Inner-loop open-loop TF:
$$G_{OL2}(s) = K_{c2} \cdot \frac{K_{p2}}{\tau_{p2} s + 1} = \frac{K_{c2} K_{p2}}{\tau_{p2} s + 1}$$

**Step 2:** Closed-loop TF of inner loop:
$$G_{c2,\text{cl}}(s) = \frac{G_{OL2}(s)}{1 + G_{OL2}(s)} = \frac{\dfrac{K_{c2} K_{p2}}{\tau_{p2} s + 1}}{1 + \dfrac{K_{c2} K_{p2}}{\tau_{p2} s + 1}}$$

**Step 3:** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(\tau_{p2} s + 1)$:

$$G_{c2,\text{cl}}(s) = \frac{K_{c2} K_{p2}}{\tau_{p2} s + 1 + K_{c2} K_{p2}}$$

**Step 4:** Define $K_{cl2} = \dfrac{K_{c2} K_{p2}}{1 + K_{c2} K_{p2}}$ and $\tau_{cl2} = \dfrac{\tau_{p2}}{1 + K_{c2} K_{p2}}$:

$$G_{c2,\text{cl}}(s) = \frac{K_{cl2}}{\tau_{cl2} s + 1}$$

**Step 5 (Key Insight):** Closing the inner loop **reduces the effective time constant** by a factor of $(1 + K_{c2} K_{p2})$. The inner loop becomes *faster*. This is precisely the *intent* of cascade control — speed up the actuator chain so the outer loop sees a faster, more responsive inner variable.

**Step 6 (Stability check):** Characteristic polynomial $\tau_{cl2} s + 1 = 0$ has root $s = -1/\tau_{cl2} < 0$. The inner loop is stable for any positive $K_{c2}$. The *outer* loop must then be tuned with this reduced time constant in mind.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — MPC Cost Minimization in Discrete Time (Unconstrained, SISO)

We have a first-order prediction model:

$$\hat{y}(k+j) = a \, \hat{y}(k+j-1) + b \, u(k+j-1)$$

where $a = e^{-T_s/\tau}$ and $b = K_p(1-a)$.

**Step 1:** Expand predictions for $j = 1, 2, 3$ assuming the future inputs $u(k), u(k+1), u(k+2)$ are the decision variables (control horizon $M=3$ for illustration):

$$\hat{y}(k+1) = a\, y(k) + b\, u(k)$$

$$\hat{y}(k+2) = a\, \hat{y}(k+1) + b\, u(k+1) = a^2 y(k) + ab\, u(k) + b\, u(k+1)$$

$$\hat{y}(k+3) = a^3 y(k) + a^2 b\, u(k) + a b\, u(k+1) + b\, u(k+2)$$

**Step 2:** Stack into vector form. Define $\mathbf{\hat{Y}} = [\hat{y}(k+1), \hat{y}(k+2), \hat{y}(k+3)]^\top$ and $\mathbf{U} = [u(k), u(k+1), u(k+2)]^\top$:

$$\mathbf{\hat{Y}} = \mathbf{\Phi}\, y(k) + \mathbf{\Gamma}\, \mathbf{U}$$

where:

$$\mathbf{\Phi} = \begin{bmatrix} a \\ a^2 \\ a^3 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{\Gamma} = \begin{bmatrix} b & 0 & 0 \\ ab & b & 0 \\ a^2 b & ab & b \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3:** The cost function (setpoint $r$ constant for simplicity):

$$J = (\mathbf{\hat{Y}} - r \mathbf{1})^\top Q (\mathbf{\hat{Y}} - r \mathbf{1}) + \mathbf{\Delta U}^\top R\, \mathbf{\Delta U}$$

where $\mathbf{\Delta U} = [u(k)-u(k-1), \, u(k+1)-u(k), \, u(k+2)-u(k+1)]^\top$.

**Step 4:** Substitute $\mathbf{\hat{Y}} = \mathbf{\Phi} y(k) + \mathbf{\Gamma U}$ and take $\partial J / \partial \mathbf{U} = 0$:

$$2 \mathbf{\Gamma}^\top Q (\mathbf{\Phi} y(k) + \mathbf{\Gamma U} - r \mathbf{1}) + 2 \mathbf{\Psi}^\top R\, \mathbf{\Psi U} = 0$$

where $\mathbf{\Psi}$ maps $\mathbf{U}$ to $\mathbf{\Delta U}$. Solving:

$$\boxed{\mathbf{U}^* = -\left(\mathbf{\Gamma}^\top Q \mathbf{\Gamma} + \mathbf{\Psi}^\top R \mathbf{\Psi} \right)^{-1} \left[ \mathbf{\Gamma}^\top Q (\mathbf{\Phi} y(k) - r \mathbf{1}) \right]}$$

**Step 5:** Apply only $u^*(k)$ (the first element of $\mathbf{U}^*$). At $k+1$, repeat.

> [!NOTE]
> In the **constrained** case, the closed-form solution is replaced by a Quadratic Programming (QP) problem solved numerically at each step (e.g., via active-set or interior-point methods).

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Feedforward Control — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dSig["Disturbance d(t)"] --> Gd["Disturbance Dynamics Gd(s)"]
    Gd --> SummFF["Summing Junction"]
    Gp["Process Dynamics Gp(s)"] --> SummFF
    SummFF --> yOut["Output y(t)"]
    dSig --> Gff["Feedforward Controller Gff(s)"]
    Gff --> Gp
    rFF["Reference r(t)"] --> eFB["Comparator"]
    yOut --> eFB
    eFB --> GcFB["Feedback Controller Gc(s)"]
    GcFB --> SummMV["MV Summing"]
    Gff --> SummMV
    SummMV --> Gp
```

## 4.2 Cascade Control — Two Nested Loops

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    r1["Primary Setpoint r1"] --> e1["Error e1 = r1 - y1"]
    e1 --> Gc1["Primary Controller Gc1(s)"]
    Gc1 --> r2["Secondary Setpoint r2"]
    r2 --> e2["Error e2 = r2 - y2"]
    e2 --> Gc2["Secondary Controller Gc2(s)"]
    Gc2 --> uMV["Manipulated Variable u"]
    uMV --> Gp2["Inner Process Gp2(s)"]
    d2["Inner Disturbance d2"] --> Gp2
    Gp2 --> y2["Secondary Variable y2"]
    y2 --> e2
    y2 --> Gp1["Outer Process Gp1(s)"]
    d1["Outer Disturbance d1"] --> Gp1
    Gp1 --> y1["Primary Variable y1"]
    y1 --> e1
```

## 4.3 Model Predictive Control — Receding Horizon Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    rMPC["Reference Trajectory r(k+1) to r(k+P)"] --> Opt["Quadratic Optimizer"]
    Yhat["Predicted Outputs yhat(k+1) to yhat(k+P)"] --> Opt
    Constraints["Constraints: u_min to u_max, du_min to du_max, y_min to y_max"] --> Opt
    Opt --> MoveSeq["Optimal Move Sequence du*(k) ... du*(k+M-1)"]
    MoveSeq --> Apply["Apply ONLY First Move u(k) = u(k-1) + du*(k)"]
    Apply --> Plant["Process Plant"]
    Plant --> Sensor["Measure y(k+1)"]
    Sensor --> Update["Update State Estimate x(k+1)"]
    Update --> Model["Internal Model Predicts yhat(k+2)..."]
    Model --> Yhat
    Plant --> Sensor
    Update --> Opt
```

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix of the Three Strategies

| Feature | Feedforward | Cascade | MPC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Type of Action | Predictive, open-loop correction | Reactive, multi-loop feedback | Predictive, constraint-aware |
| Requires Model? | Yes (disturbance + process) | No (loop tuning only) | Yes (full dynamic model) |
| Handles Constraints? | No (can violate output bounds) | No | Yes (native) |
| Handles Unmeasured Disturbances? | No | Yes (via feedback) | Yes (via feedback update) |
| Multivariable? | Rarely | No (SISO pairs) | Yes (MIMO native) |
| Tuning Difficulty | Moderate (lead-lag) | Moderate (2 controllers) | High (QP weights, horizons) |
| Computational Load | Negligible | Negligible | High (online QP) |
| Industrial Use | Boiler level, feed-effluent | Reactor temperature, jacketed reactors | Distillation, FCC, ethylene crackers |
