# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the principle of feedforward control. Why is it usually combined with a feedback controller in practice?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Feedforward control measures the principal disturbance(s) and, using a process model, computes a corrective manipulated variable action *before* the disturbance affects the output.
- **(1 mark)** The ideal feedforward controller is $G_{ff}(s) = -G_d(s)/G_p(s)$, which exactly cancels the disturbance effect.
- **(1 mark)** In practice, the process model is never perfect, and there will always be *unmeasured* disturbances. Hence feedforward leaves a residual error. Combining it with feedback ensures that any modelling error or unmeasured disturbance is corrected by the feedback loop, while the feedforward loop handles the dominant measured disturbance quickly. → **Combined Feedback + Feedforward = "Feedback-Feedforward Control."**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is the "receding horizon" principle in Model Predictive Control? Why is only the first control move applied?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Receding horizon means that at each sampling instant $k$, the controller solves an optimization over a finite horizon $[k+1, k+P]$, computes $M$ future control moves, but applies *only the first* move $u(k)$.
- **(1 mark)** At the next instant $k+1$, the horizon is shifted forward by one step, new measurements are taken, and the optimization is re-solved with updated information.
- **(1 mark)** This feedback mechanism (re-planning) compensates for model inaccuracies, unmeasured disturbances, and plant nonlinearities. Applying only the first move prevents the controller from "committing" to an entire trajectory that is based on imperfect predictions; it acts *cautiously* and *adaptively*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the structure of cascade control. State and justify the rules for selecting the **secondary (slave) variable** in a cascade control system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A jacketed CSTR has the following dynamics:
- Jacket temperature → reactor temperature: $G_{p1}(s) = \dfrac{2}{(5s+1)(2s+1)}$
- Cooling-water valve position → jacket temperature: $G_{p2}(s) = \dfrac{0.5}{0.5s+1}$
- A PI controller is used in the secondary loop with $K_{c2}=4$, $T_{i2}=0.5$ min.
- Compute the **equivalent inner-loop closed-loop transfer function** and comment on how cascade control has effectively reduced the time constant. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Structure and Secondary Variable Selection (7 marks):**

- **(2 marks) Block diagram:** A cascade system has two nested feedback loops. The output of the *primary controller* $G_{c1}$ becomes the *setpoint* of the *secondary controller* $G_{c2}$. The secondary variable $y_2$ is an intermediate process variable, measured in the inner loop. The primary variable $y_1$ is the actual controlled variable. (Refer to Section 4.2 diagram.)

- **(5 marks) Rules for selecting the secondary variable:**
  1. **(1 mark) Measurability:** The variable must be measurable with a reliable, fast, online sensor (e.g., flow, pressure, temperature).
  2. **(1 mark) Causal linkage:** It must have a direct causal effect on the primary variable. (e.g., jacket temperature $\to$ reactor temperature).
  3. **(1 mark) Faster dynamics:** Its time constant and dead time must be *smaller* than those of the primary loop — typically by a factor of 3 to 5.
  4. **(1 mark) Significant disturbance entry point:** Most major disturbances (e.g., cooling-water pressure, steam pressure) should enter the inner loop, so the secondary controller can reject them *before* they reach the primary variable.
  5. **(1 mark) Linear and well-behaved:** The inner-loop process should be approximately linear to allow tight tuning of the secondary loop.

**(b) Cascade Inner-Loop Reduction (7 marks):**

- **(1 mark) Secondary PI controller:**
$$G_{c2}(s) = K_{c2}\left(1 + \frac{1}{T_{i2} s}\right) = 4\left(1 + \frac{1}{0.5 s}\right) = 4 + \frac{8}{s} = \frac{4(0.5s+1)}{0.5s} = \frac{2s+4}{0.5s}$$

- **(1 mark) Inner-loop process:**
$$G_{p2}(s) = \frac{0.5}{0.5s+1}$$

- **(1 mark) Open-loop TF:**
$$G_{OL2}(s) = G_{c2}(s) G_{p2}(s) = \frac{(2s+4)}{0.5s} \cdot \frac{0.5}{0.5s+1} = \frac{2s+4}{s(0.5s+1)}$$

- **(1 mark) Closed-loop TF:**
$$G_{c2,cl}(s) = \frac{G_{OL2}}{1+G_{OL2}} = \frac{\dfrac{2s+4}{s(0.5s+1)}}{1 + \dfrac{2s+4}{s(0.5s+1)}}$$

- **(1 mark) Multiply numerator and denominator by $s(0.5s+1)$:**
$$G_{c2,cl}(s) = \frac{2s+4}{s(0.5s+1) + (2s+4)} = \frac{2s+4}{0.5s^2 + s + 2s + 4} = \frac{2s+4}{0.5s^2 + 3s + 4}$$

- **(1 mark) Multiply numerator and denominator by 2:**
$$G_{c2,cl}(s) = \frac{4s+8}{s^2 + 6s + 8} = \frac{4(s+2)}{(s+2)(s+4)} = \frac{4}{s+4}$$

- **(1 mark) Comment:** The inner-loop effective time constant is now $1/4 = 0.25$ min, compared to the open-loop value of $0.5$ min. The cascade has *halved* the effective time constant, giving the outer (primary) loop a much faster, more responsive "actuator chain" to work with. Disturbances entering the inner loop (e.g., cooling-water pressure fluctuations) are rejected ~4× faster.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a **Model Predictive Controller (MPC)**. Clearly define the prediction horizon $P$ and control horizon $M$, and explain the **receding horizon** strategy. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SISO process has the discrete-time model:
$$y(k) = 0.7\, y(k-1) + 0.3\, u(k-1)$$
At $k=0$: $y(0) = 2$, $u(-1) = 1$, setpoint $r = 5$. Choose prediction horizon $P = 2$ and control horizon $M = 1$. The MPC cost is $J = (\hat{y}(k+1)-r)^2 + 0.1(\Delta u(k))^2$. Compute the **optimal control move** $\Delta u^*(0)$ and the resulting $u(0)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) MPC Working Principle (7 marks):**
- **(2 marks) Block diagram & elements:** Refer to Section 4.3. The three core elements of MPC are: (1) **Prediction model** (state-space / TF / step-response), (2) **Cost function optimizer** (typically a QP), and (3) **Receding horizon policy** that re-plans at every step.
- **(1 mark) Prediction horizon $P$:** The number of future time steps over which the output is predicted (e.g., $P=10$ means predicting 10 steps ahead).
- **(1 mark) Control horizon $M$:** The number of future control moves that the optimizer is allowed to adjust ($M \le P$). After $M$ moves, the control is held constant.
- **(1 mark) Cost function:** Penalizes tracking error and control effort — typically quadratic, e.g., $J = \sum \Vert \hat{y} - r \Vert_Q^2 + \sum \Vert \Delta u \Vert_R^2$.
- **(1 mark) Constraints:** Hard limits on $u$, $\Delta u$, $y$, and $x$ are handled natively by the QP solver.
- **(1 mark) Receding horizon:** At each $k$, the controller solves the optimization for $M$ moves over the prediction horizon $P$, but *applies only the first move* $u(k)$. At $k+1$, the horizon is shifted, new measurements are folded in, and the process repeats. This implicit feedback compensates for model errors and unmeasured disturbances.

**(b) MPC Optimal Move Calculation (7 marks):**

- **(1 mark) Model:** $y(k) = 0.7 y(k-1) + 0.3 u(k-1)$, so $a = 0.7$, $b = 0.3$. We have $P=2$, $M=1$, $r=5$, $Q=1$, $R=0.1$.

- **(1 mark) One-step prediction:**
$$\hat{y}(1) = 0.7 \cdot y(0) + 0.3 \cdot u(0) = 0.7(2) + 0.3 \cdot u(0) = 1.4 + 0.3\, u(0)$$

Since $M=1$, the control after $u(0)$ is held constant: $u(1) = u(0)$.

- **(1 mark) Two-step prediction:**
$$\hat{y}(2) = 0.7\, \hat{y}(1) + 0.3\, u(1) = 0.7(1.4 + 0.3 u(0)) + 0.3 u(0) = 0.98 + 0.21 u(0) + 0.3 u(0) = 0.98 + 0.51 u(0)$$

- **(1 mark) Cost function (only horizon-1 term has $R$ penalty since $M=1$):**
$$J = (\hat{y}(1) - r)^2 + 0.1 (\Delta u(0))^2 + (\hat{y}(2) - r)^2 \cdot Q_2$$
We take $Q_2 = 1$ (equal weighting) for illustration:
$$J = (1.4 + 0.3 u(0) - 5)^2 + 0.1 (u(0) - u(-1))^2 + (0.98 + 0.51 u(0) - 5)^2$$

- **(1 mark) Substitute $u(-1) = 1$:**
$$J = (0.3 u(0) - 3.6)^2 + 0.1 (u(0) - 1)^2 + (0.51 u(0) - 4.02)^2$$

- **(1 mark) Differentiate and set to zero** $dJ/du(0) = 0$:
$$\frac{dJ}{du(0)} = 2(0.3 u(0) - 3.6)(0.3) + 0.2(u(0) - 1) + 2(0.51 u(0) - 4.02)(0.51) = 0$$

$$0.18 u(0) - 2.16 + 0.2 u(0) - 0.2 + 0.5202 u(0) - 4.1004 = 0$$

$$(0.18 + 0.2 + 0.5202) u(0) = 2.16 + 0.2 + 4.1004$$

$$0.9002\, u(0) = 6.4604 \;\Rightarrow\; u(0) \approx 7.176$$

- **(1 mark) Optimal move:**
$$\Delta u^*(0) = u(0) - u(-1) = 7.176 - 1 = 6.176$$

The controller issues a *large* positive move to push the output toward the setpoint of 5. At $k=1$, the procedure repeats with the new measurement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Feedforward sign error:** Confusing the sign of $K_{ff}$. Always re-derive it from $G_d/G_p$ — a sign error inverts the compensation, *amplifying* the disturbance instead of cancelling it. (Up to **−3 marks**.)
> 2. **Cascade tuning order:** Tuning the *outer* loop before the inner loop. **Always** tune the inner (faster) loop first with the outer in manual, then close the outer loop. (−2 marks.)
> 3. **Cascade on the wrong disturbance:** If the major disturbance enters the *outer* loop (not the inner), cascade provides *no benefit*. Always identify the disturbance entry point. (−2 marks.)
> 4. **MPC horizon confusion:** Predicting $P$ steps but only optimizing $M$ steps, then incorrectly assuming $M = P$. Remember $M \le P$, and the control is held constant after $M$. (−2 marks.)
> 5. **Applying all $M$ moves in MPC:** The "receding horizon" principle means *only the first move* is applied. Students often write the full sequence as if it is implemented. (−2 marks.)
> 6. **Units inconsistency:** Mixing seconds and minutes in time constants (especially in cascade). Always carry units. (−1 mark.)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**
>
> - **Feedforward Control**
>   - Acts on *measured* disturbances using a process model: $G_{ff}^{\text{ideal}}(s) = -G_d(s)/G_p(s)$.
>   - *Static* FF (gain only) is sufficient for steady-state rejection; *dynamic* FF (lead-lag) is needed when disturbance and MV paths have different dynamics.
>   - **Limitation:** Cannot handle model errors or unmeasured disturbances → always pair with feedback.
>
> - **Cascade Control**
>   - Two nested loops: **primary (master)** and **secondary (slave)**.
>   - Secondary variable must be *faster, measurable, causally linked* to the primary.
>   - Effective only for disturbances entering the **inner** loop.
>   - **Tuning order:** Inner first (with outer in manual), then outer.
>   - Inner-loop closing *reduces the effective time constant* by a factor of $(1 + K_c K_p)$.
>
> - **Model Predictive Control (MPC)**
>   - Uses a **dynamic prediction model**, **optimization**, and a **receding horizon**.
>   - Cost: $J = \sum \Vert \hat{y} - r \Vert_Q^2 + \sum \Vert \Delta u \Vert_R^2$.
>   - Handles **constraints** (input, output, slew-rate, state) natively via QP.
>   - **Receding horizon:** solve → apply *first* move → shift → re-solve.
>   - Industrial standards: **DMC** (Dynamic Matrix Control), **IDCOM** (Identification-Command), **GPC** (Generalized Predictive Control), **AspenTech DMCplus**, **Honeywell Profit Controller**.
>
> - **Key Comparison**
>   - FF: predictive, no error correction, model-dependent.
>   - CC: reactive, faster disturbance rejection via nested loop.
>   - MPC: predictive + reactive + constraint-aware + multivariable — the *most powerful* but *most computationally expensive*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Case Studies and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

In the context of **PERUT632 – Advanced Industrial Automation**, **Case Studies and Applications** refers to the systematic documentation, analysis, and evaluation of real-world industrial automation deployments across discrete and process manufacturing sectors. It encompasses the practical integration of **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLC)**, **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** systems, **Distributed Control Systems (DCS)**, **Human-Machine Interfaces (HMI)**, **Industrial Robotics**, **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)**, and **Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** within production environments to achieve measurable improvements in **Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE)**, throughput, quality, safety (per **IEC 61508 / IEC 61511**), and energy efficiency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A "case study" in this module is not merely a descriptive narrative. It is a structured engineering artifact that documents the *problem*, the *automation architecture adopted*, the *hardware/software stack*, the *control philosophy*, the *instrumentation layer*, and the *quantified performance outcomes* (e.g., OEE uplift, Mean Time Between Failures – MTBF, cycle-time reduction).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Industrial Metric Definitions (as per ISA-95 & ISO 22400):**
> - **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality**
> - **Availability** = (Planned Production Time − Downtime) / Planned Production Time
> - **Performance** = (Ideal Cycle Time × Total Pieces) / Operating Time
> - **Quality** = Good Pieces / Total Pieces
> - **MTBF** = Total Operational Time / Number of Failures
> - **MTTR** = Total Downtime / Number of Failures
> - **SCADA Latency Tolerance** ≤ **2 seconds** for supervisory loops
> - **DCS Control Loop Scan Time** = **100 ms to 500 ms** (typical)

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an industrial automation case study the way a doctor analyzes a **patient's medical case history**. Just as a doctor records symptoms, runs diagnostics, prescribes a treatment protocol, and monitors recovery metrics (BP, heart rate, cholesterol), an automation engineer documents the **production symptoms** (frequent downtime, scrap, safety incidents), performs a **system audit** (sensor/actuator inventory, control loop analysis), prescribes an **automation architecture** (PLC + SCADA + Robotics), and tracks **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** like OEE, MTBF, and energy-per-unit to validate recovery.

A second analogy: consider a **smart city traffic management system**. Sensors (inductive loops, cameras) feed data to a central SCADA-like platform, which then coordinates signals (actuators) to optimize flow. Industrial automation operates identically — only the "city" is a plant floor, the "vehicles" are workpieces, and the "traffic signals" are valves, motors, and robotic arms.

---

## 1.3 Learning Outcomes (Mapped to KTU COs)

Upon mastering this topic, the learner will be able to:

| CO ID | Course Outcome Statement | Bloom's Level |
|-------|--------------------------|---------------|
| **CO2** | Analyze the architecture of real-world industrial automation systems integrating PLC, SCADA, DCS, and IIoT. | Analyze (L4) |
| **CO3** | Evaluate the performance of automation case studies using industrial KPIs (OEE, MTBF, cycle time). | Evaluate (L5) |
| **CO4** | Design automation solutions for given industrial case scenarios respecting safety, scalability, and cybersecurity norms. | Create (L6) |

> [!NOTE]
> All three COs are aligned to higher-order Bloom's levels (L4–L6) because case study analysis inherently demands critical thinking, comparative evaluation, and design synthesis.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** frequently test Module 2 through a **14-mark Part B question** that presents an industrial scenario (e.g., a bottling plant, a thermal power station, an automotive assembly line) and asks the student to: (a) draw the automation hierarchy, (b) select appropriate controllers and field instruments, (c) justify the choice using KPIs, and (d) discuss safety interlocks. Memorizing a textbook diagram is insufficient — you must be able to *reason* the architecture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Purdue Reference Model (ISA-95) — The Foundational Framework

Every serious case study in industrial automation is framed against the **ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model**, which stratifies an enterprise into five levels:

| Level | Function | Typical Components | Communication Protocols |
|-------|----------|--------------------|------------------------|
| **Level 0** | Physical Process | Sensors, Actuators, Motors, Valves | 4–20 mA, HART, Foundation Fieldbus |
| **Level 1** | Basic Control | PLCs, DCS Controllers, PID Loops | Profibus, Modbus, EtherCAT |
| **Level 2** | Supervisory Control | SCADA, HMI, Alarm Management | OPC UA, MQTT, DNP3 |
| **Level 3** | Manufacturing Operations Management (MOM) | MES, Historians, OEE Dashboards | SQL, REST APIs, OPC UA |
| **Level 4** | Enterprise Planning | ERP (SAP, Oracle), Supply Chain | SOAP, REST, EDI |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Purdue Model matters in exams:** Most "draw the architecture" questions in KTU demand a clearly labeled ISA-95 stack. Confusing Level 1 and Level 2 functions is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

## 2.2 Core Analytical Frameworks for Case Study Evaluation

### Framework A — The **CAPE** Analysis (Control, Actuator, Process, Environment)

Used to dissect any automation problem:

1. **Control**: Which controller? (PLC / DCS / RTU / Embedded). What scan time? What redundancy?
2. **Actuator**: What physical motion / flow is being controlled? (Pneumatic, hydraulic, electric).
3. **Process**: What physical/chemical transformation is occurring? (Mixing, heating, stamping, welding).
4. **Environment**: Hazardous area classification (ATEX Zone 0/1/2, IECEx), temperature, dust, humidity.

### Framework B — The **KPI** Validation Matrix

After proposing a solution, validate using:

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$$

where each component must be measured and a target specified (typically **OEE ≥ 85%** in world-class manufacturing).

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{\text{Total Operating Time (hours)}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

$$\text{MTTR} = \frac{\text{Total Downtime (hours)}}{\text{Number of Failures}}$$

$$\text{Availability Factor} = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A high MTTR often indicates *poor maintainability design* (e.g., inaccessible panels, undocumented ladder logic). World-class plants target **MTBF ≥ 8,000 hours** and **MTTR ≤ 30 minutes**.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|-------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q$ | Dimensionless (%) | Overall equipment effectiveness |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{T_{\text{run}}}{T_{\text{planned}}}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Availability factor |
| 3 | $P = \dfrac{(T_{\text{ideal}} \times N_{\text{good}})}{T_{\text{run}}}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Performance rate |
| 4 | $Q = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Ratio (0–1) | Quality rate |
| 5 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{T_{\text{op}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Hours | Reliability metric |
| 6 | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{T_{\text{down}}}{N_{\text{failures}}}$ | Hours | Maintainability metric |
| 7 | $f_{\text{scan}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{scan}}}$ | Hz | PLC scan frequency (typically 10–50 ms) |
| 8 | $\text{PID Output} \; u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | Engineering units | Continuous control law |
| 9 | $G_{\text{plant}}(s) = \dfrac{K}{(\tau s + 1)}$ | Transfer function | First-order process approximation |
| 10 | $\text{Latency}_{\text{SCADA}} \leq 2\,\text{s}$ | Seconds | Supervisory loop deadline |
| 11 | $N_{\text{throughput}} = \dfrac{3600}{T_{\text{cycle}}}$ | Units/hour | Production rate |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{energy}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{useful}}}{E_{\text{input}}} \times 100\%$ | Percent | Energy efficiency |
| 13 | $C_{\text{ROI}} = \dfrac{(B - C)}{C} \times 100\%$ | Percent | Justification of automation investment |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** The Purdue Model levels, OEE breakdown, and MTBF/MTTR definitions are the **three most-tested concepts** from this module in past KTU papers. A question worth 7 marks may simply ask: *"Define OEE. A bottling line runs for 480 minutes, produces 9,000 bottles of which 8,550 are good. Its ideal cycle time is 3 seconds. Calculate OEE."*

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The CAPE and KPI frameworks are not academic exercises. They are the **de facto audit methodology** used by:

- **Siemens, ABB, Schneider Electric** in their *Automation Audit* offerings.
- **Toyota Production System (TPS)** and **Six Sigma Black Belts** for OEE improvement.
- **ISA** certified **CAP (Certified Automation Professional)** examinations.
- **Petroleum refineries** under API 1169 pipeline automation audits.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem — OEE Calculation (Model KTU Problem)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement:** A beverage bottling plant operates for an 8-hour shift (480 minutes). It experiences two unplanned stoppages of 15 minutes each for sensor faults. The line produces **9,000 bottles**, of which **8,550** are saleable. The **ideal cycle time per bottle is 3 seconds**, and the **planned production time** was 480 minutes. Compute: (a) Availability, (b) Performance, (c) Quality, (d) OEE.

### Step 1 — Define Variables and Extract Data

| Variable | Meaning | Value |
|----------|---------|-------|
| $T_{\text{planned}}$ | Planned production time | 480 min |
| $T_{\text{down}}$ | Total unplanned downtime | $2 \times 15 = 30$ min |
| $T_{\text{run}}$ | Actual operating time | $480 - 30 = 450$ min |
| $N_{\text{total}}$ | Total bottles produced | 9,000 |
| $N_{\text{good}}$ | Good-quality bottles | 8,550 |
| $T_{\text{ideal}}$ | Ideal cycle time per bottle | 3 s = 0.05 min |

### Step 2 — Compute Availability

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{\text{run}}}{T_{\text{planned}}} = \dfrac{450}{480} = 0.9375 = 93.75\%
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Performance

The number of bottles that *should* have been produced in the actual running time at ideal speed:

$$
N_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{run}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}} = \dfrac{450\;\text{min}}{0.05\;\text{min/bottle}} = 9{,}000\;\text{bottles}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{N_{\text{ideal}} \times T_{\text{ideal}}}{T_{\text{run}}} = \dfrac{9{,}000 \times 0.05}{450} = 1.00 = 100\%
$$

Alternatively, using the more common form:

$$
P = \dfrac{T_{\text{ideal}} \times N_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{run}}} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 9{,}000}{450} = 1.00
$$

### Step 4 — Compute Quality

$$
Q = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{8{,}550}{9{,}000} = 0.95 = 95.00\%
$$

### Step 5 — Compute OEE

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9375 \times 1.00 \times 0.95 = 0.8906 = 89.06\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** An OEE of **89.06%** is **world-class** (the global benchmark is **≥ 85%** for "World Class" per the **Semiconductor & Electronics industry OEE study**). The bottleneck here is **Quality** (5% defect rate). A student should recommend *Root Cause Analysis (RCA)* and a *Poka-Yoke* (mistake-proofing) device.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Industry Sector × Automation Architecture

| Industry Sector | Level 0 (Field) | Level 1 (Control) | Level 2 (Supervisory) | Level 3 (MOM) | Typical KPI Gain |
|-----------------|------------------|-------------------|------------------------|---------------|------------------|
| **Automotive Assembly** | Servo motors, torque sensors, vision cameras | Siemens S7-1500 PLC, Safety PLC (F-CPU) | WinCC SCADA, HMI TP1500 | Siemens Opcenter | +22% throughput |
| **Oil & Gas Refinery** | Pressure transmitters, control valves | Honeywell Experion DCS (C300) | Experion HMIWeb | PHD Historian | OEE 78% → 91% |
| **Pharmaceutical** | pH probes, mass flow meters | Allen-Bradley ControlLogix | FactoryTalk View | Syncade MES | Batch cycle −18% |
| **Food & Beverage** | Level switches, temperature RTDs | Schneider M580 PLC | Wonderware InTouch | AVEVA MES | Energy −30% |
| **Power Plant (Thermal)** | Drum level, flow, NOx sensors | ABB 800xA DCS | 800xA Operator Workplace | OSIsoft PI | Heat rate −2.5% |
| **Water Treatment** | Turbidity, chlorine analyzers | Rockwell MicroLogix | Citect SCADA | ClearSCADA | Chemical cost −12% |

---

## 3.3 PID Tuning — A Worked Analytical Case

For a temperature control loop on a chemical reactor, the open-loop step response is a first-order lag:

$$
G_{\text{plant}}(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}
$$

where the step test yields $K = 5$ °C/% and $\tau = 120$ s.

### Ziegler-Nichols Open-Loop Tuning

| Parameter | Formula | Value |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| $K_p$ (proportional gain) | $\dfrac{\tau}{K \cdot L}$ | (requires dead time $L$) |
| $K_i$ (integral gain) | $\dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ |  |
| $K_d$ (derivative gain) | $K_p \cdot T_d$ |  |

> [!NOTE]
> The full Ziegler-Nichols table is intentionally referenced rather than re-derived here because **deriving it** is rarely asked in KTU Module 2; however, *applying* the tuned $K_p, K_i, K_d$ values to compute the **steady-state error** and **settling time** is a frequently tested skill.

### Steady-State Error for Step Input

For a unity-feedback system with a PI controller $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s}$:

$$
e_{\text{ss}} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G_c(s) G_p(s)}
$$

For a step input $R(s) = \dfrac{1}{s}$ and a Type-1 system, $e_{\text{ss}} = 0$. This is why a PI controller is mandatory for setpoint tracking.

---

## 3.4 Cybersecurity in Automation — The IEC 62443 Lens

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A modern KTU case study question may embed a **cybersecurity** sub-question. Memorize the **IEC 62443 Security Level (SL)** taxonomy:

| Security Level | Target | Description |
|----------------|--------|-------------|
| **SL 1** | Casual / coincidental | Protection against unintentional misuse |
| **SL 2** | Intentional with simple means | Protection against deliberate attack with low resources |
| **SL 3** | Intentional with sophisticated means | Protection against skilled attackers with moderate resources |
| **SL 4** | Intentional with extensive resources | Nation-state level threats |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** A plant designed for SL 1 but deployed in a critical infrastructure (e.g., a power grid) is *non-compliant*. This is a common "design oversight" examiners exploit for marks.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 ISA-95 / Purdue Model — Mermaid Block Diagram

<!-- Mermaid block, all node IDs alphanumeric, no reserved keywords as IDs -->
```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L4["LEVEL 4: ENTERPRISE PLANNING\nERP - SAP - Oracle - Supply Chain"]:::level4
    L3["LEVEL 3: MANUFACTURING OPERATIONS\nMES - Historians - OEE Dashboards"]:::level3
    L2["LEVEL 2: SUPERVISORY CONTROL\nSCADA - HMI - Alarm Management"]:::level2
    L1["LEVEL 1: BASIC CONTROL\nPLC - DCS Controllers - PID Loops"]:::level1
    L0["LEVEL 0: PHYSICAL PROCESS\nSensors - Actuators - Motors - Valves"]:::level0

    L4 -->|Production Orders| L3
    L3 -->|Work Orders - Schedules| L2
    L2 -->|Setpoints - Recipes| L1
    L1 -->|Control Signals 4-20mA| L0
    L0 -->|Process Data Feedback| L1
    L1 -->|Status Telemetry| L2
    L2 -->|KPI Reports| L3
    L3 -->|Financial Reports| L4

    classDef level0 fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef level1 fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#e67e22,color:#000
    classDef level2 fill:#fffee5,stroke:#f1c40f,color:#000
    classDef level3 fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    classDef level4 fill:#e5e5ff,stroke:#2c3e50,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The downward arrows represent *command/control* flow (setpoints, recipes, schedules). The upward arrows represent *data/feedback* flow (telemetry, KPIs). This bidirectional flow is the **hallmark** of any KTU answer that demands an automation architecture diagram.

---

## 4.2 Case Study Architecture — Automated Bottling Plant

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph field["LEVEL 0: FIELD LAYER"]
        T1["Temperature RTD"]
        L1["Level Switch"]
        F1["Flow Meter"]
        V1["Control Valve"]
        M1["Conveyor Motor"]
    end

    subgraph control["LEVEL 1: CONTROL LAYER"]
        PLC["Siemens S7-1500 PLC\nScan Time 10ms"]
        PID["PID Loop\nTemperature Control"]
    end

    subgraph sup["LEVEL 2: SUPERVISORY LAYER"]
        HMI["HMI TP1500 Comfort"]
        SCADA["WinCC SCADA Server"]
        ALARM["Alarm Logger"]
    end

    subgraph mes["LEVEL 3: MES LAYER"]
        OEE["OEE Dashboard"]
        HIST["Process Historian"]
    end

    T1 -->|4-20mA| PID
    L1 -->|Digital Input| PLC
    F1 -->|Modbus RTU| PLC
    PLC -->|Analog Out 4-20mA| V1
    PLC -->|Digital Out| M1
    PID --> PLC
    PLC -->|OPC UA| SCADA
    PLC -->|OPC UA| HMI
    SCADA --> ALARM
    SCADA -->|SQL| OEE
    SCADA -->|SQL| HIST

    classDef fieldStyle fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b,color:#000
    classDef controlStyle fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#e67e22,color:#000
    classDef supStyle fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#27ae60,color:#000
    classDef mesStyle fill:#e5e5ff,stroke:#2c3e50,color:#000

    class T1,L1,F1,V1,M1 fieldStyle
    class PLC,PID controlStyle
    class HMI,SCADA,ALARM supStyle
    class OEE,HIST mesStyle
```

---

## 4.3 CAPE Analysis Flow — Generic Automation Case Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Industrial Problem Statement] --> B{Identify CAPE Elements}
    B --> C1[Control: PLC or DCS]
    B --> C2[Actuator: Motor or Valve]
    B --> C3[Process: Physical or Chemical]
    B --> C4[Environment: Hazard Zone]

    C1 --> D[Select Hardware Architecture]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D

    D --> E[Define I/O List]
    E --> F[Design Control Philosophy Ladder Logic]
    F --> G[Specify HMI Screens SCADA Tags]
    G --> H[Compute KPIs OEE MTBF]
    H --> I{Meet Target?}
    I -->|No| J[Iterate Design]
    I -->|Yes| K[Validated Automation Solution]

    classDef defStyle fill:#e5f5ff,stroke:#3498db,color:#000
    classDef successStyle fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000
    class A,B,C1,C2,C3,C4,D,E,F,G,H,I,J defStyle
    class K successStyle
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Strategy:** When a 14-mark question gives you a "scenario + 3 sub-parts", the CAPE flow is your mental checklist. Verbs in the question like "select", "design", "justify", "evaluate" map directly onto steps D → F → G → H.

---

## 4.4 Safety Integrity — SIL Classification Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1["SIL 1\nPFD 10^-1 to 10^-2\nLow Risk"]:::s1
    S2["SIL 2\nPFD 10^-2 to 10^-3\nMedium Risk"]:::s2
    S3["SIL 3\nPFD 10^-3 to 10^-4\nHigh Risk"]:::s3
    S4["SIL 4\nPFD 10^-4 to 10^-5\nVery High Risk"]:::s4

    S1 --> S2 --> S3 --> S4

    classDef s1 fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
    classDef s2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    classDef s3 fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    classDef s4 fill:#721c24,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **PFD** = Probability of Failure on Demand. A **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** in a refinery Emergency Shutdown (ESD) system is typically designed to **SIL 2 or SIL 3**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Q: Define Overall Equipment Effectiveness (OEE). List its three components and state the world-class benchmark value.**

**Model Answer:**

OEE is a composite industrial KPI that quantifies how effectively a manufacturing operation is utilized, relative to its theoretical maximum. It is the product of three ratios:

| Component | Formula | Represents |
|-----------|---------|------------|
| **Availability** | $\dfrac{T_{\text{run}}}{T_{\text{planned}}}$ | Uptime vs. downtime (excludes stoppages and setup) |
| **Performance** | $\dfrac{T_{\text{ideal}} \times N_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{run}}}$ | Speed losses (minor stops, reduced speed) |
| **Quality** | $\dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}}$ | Defect losses (rejects, rework) |

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q
$$

**World-class benchmark: OEE ≥ 85%** (per the **Sematech / Industry Week OEE Studies**).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between PLC-based and DCS-based automation architectures. Give one example industry for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | PLC-Based | DCS-Based |
|---------|-----------|-----------|
| **Architecture** | Centralized ladder logic; single or networked PLCs | Distributed controllers; redundant loop controllers |
| **Scan Time** | 10–50 ms | 100–500 ms (tuned per loop) |
| **Scalability** | Suited to discrete, skid-based systems | Suited to large continuous processes |
| **Redundancy** | Optional (controller + I/O) | Built-in (1oo2, 2oo3 voting) |
| **Typical Industry** | Automotive assembly, packaging | Oil & gas refinery, power plant, petrochemical |
| **Programming** | Ladder Logic (IEC 61131-3) | Function Block, Sequential Function Chart (SFC) |
| **Example Hardware** | Siemens S7-1500, Allen-Bradley ControlLogix | Honeywell Experion, ABB 800xA, Emerson DeltaV |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

### 🔹 Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

> **Q:** A pharmaceutical batch reactor requires automated control of **temperature (RTD input)**, **pressure (transducer input)**, and **agitation speed (VFD-driven motor)**. The reaction is exothermic and classified as **ATEX Zone 1**.
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Design the complete automation architecture across **ISA-95 Levels 0–3**, identifying field instruments, controller, SCADA/HMI, and MES functions. Justify each selection.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Develop a **safety instrumented function (SIF)** for over-temperature protection. Specify the SIL target, sensor/valve redundancy, and explain the role of IEC 61511 in your design.

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**ISA-95 Architecture for Batch Reactor:**

| Level | Component | Selection | Justification |
|-------|-----------|-----------|---------------|
| **L0** | Temperature sensor | PT100 Class A RTD, 4-wire | High accuracy (±0.15 °C) needed for exotherm control |
| **L0** | Pressure sensor | HART-based pressure transmitter | HART enables remote calibration & diagnostics |
| **L0** | Agitator motor | 3-phase induction motor + ABB ACS580 VFD | VFD permits closed-loop speed control via 4–20 mA |
| **L1** | Controller | Siemens S7-1500F (Fail-safe PLC) | Built-in safety functions; ATEX Zone 1 certified variants available |
| **L1** | Control strategy | Cascade control: Temperature (master) → VFD speed (slave) | Cascade rejects agitator-speed disturbances faster |
| **L2** | SCADA/HMI | WinCC Professional + TP1500 Comfort HMI | Recipe management, batch trending, electronic signatures (21 CFR Part 11) |
| **L3** | MES | Siemens Opcenter Execution | Batch genealogy, deviation logging, regulatory reporting |

**CAPE Snapshot:**
- **Control**: PLC + VFD (closed loop, cascade)
- **Actuator**: VFD → Motor → Impeller; also steam control valve for heating
- **Process**: Exothermic chemical reaction in jacketed vessel
- **Environment**: ATEX Zone 1 → all field enclosures must be **Ex d (flameproof)** rated

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cascade control?** A single-loop PID controlling jacket temperature would have a long dead time (~60 s). A cascade loop using jacket temperature as master and steam valve position as slave rejects disturbances ~5× faster. This is a high-yield KTU concept.

### Model Solution — Part (b) — Safety Instrumented Function

**Hazard:** Exothermic runaway → vessel over-pressure → catastrophic rupture.

**Target SIL:** **SIL 2** (PFD between $10^{-2}$ and $10^{-3}$).

**SIF Architecture (1oo2 voting):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RTDA["RTD Sensor A\n4-wire PT100"] --> SIS["Safety PLC\nSiemens S7-1500F\nLogic Solver"]
    RTDB["RTD Sensor B\nRedundant"] --> SIS
    SIS -->|Trip Signal| XV1["Solenoid Valve A\nShut-off Steam"]
    SIS -->|Trip Signal| XV2["Solenoid Valve B\nRedundant"]
    XV1 --> JKT["Cooling Water\nOn at Trip"]
    XV2 --> JKT
    SIS --> ALM["Alarm to SCADA\nOperator Notification"]

    classDef sensor fill:#e5f5ff,stroke:#3498db
    classDef sis fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107
    classDef valve fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545
    classDef alarm fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745

    class RTDA,RTDB sensor
    class SIS sis
    class XV1,XV2,JKT valve
    class ALM alarm
```

**Verification per IEC 61511:**
1. **Hazard & Risk Assessment (H&RA)** — identify over-temperature as hazard.
2. **Determine SIL** using a **risk graph** (severity, probability, demand rate).
3. **Specify Safety Integrity Requirements (SIR)** for sensor, logic solver, final element.
4. **Calculate PFDavg** for each subsystem and the SIF total. Must lie in the SIL 2 band.
5. **Validate** through **Functional Safety Assessment (FSA)** and **SIL verification**.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfall #1:**
> Students often confuse **SIL** (Safety Integrity Level, a target) with **SIF** (Safety Instrumented Function, the implementation). Always state both distinctly.
>
> **Common Pitfall #2:** Forgetting to mention **proof test interval (PTI)** — typically 1 year. Sensors must be proof-tested, otherwise the PFD drifts upward over time.
>
> **Common Pitfall #3:** Drawing the SIF with only *one* sensor and *one* valve. The examiner expects **redundancy (1oo2 or 2oo2)** and explicit mention of **diagnostic coverage**.

---

### 🔹 Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **Q:** A modern **automated bottling plant** runs three shifts. The line has a **Siemens S7-1500 PLC** as the master controller, **10 servo-driven conveyors**, **6 vision cameras** for label inspection, and a **central SCADA** station.
>
> **(a)** [7 Marks] Compute the **Availability, Performance, Quality, and OEE** of the line given: shift length = 8 h, planned production time = 8 h, two stoppages of 20 min each, total bottles = 12,000, good bottles = 11,400, ideal cycle time = 2.4 s.
>
> **(b)** [7 Marks] Propose a **predictive maintenance** strategy using **IIoT and vibration sensors** on the servo motors. Justify with MTBF, MTTR, and how data flows from the sensor to the MES layer.

### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Given:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|----------|--------|-------|
| Planned production time | $T_{\text{planned}}$ | 8 h = 480 min |
| Stoppages | — | $2 \times 20 = 40$ min |
| Actual running time | $T_{\text{run}}$ | $480 - 40 = 440$ min |
| Total bottles | $N_{\text{total}}$ | 12,000 |
| Good bottles | $N_{\text{good}}$ | 11,400 |
| Ideal cycle time | $T_{\text{ideal}}$ | 2.4 s = 0.04 min |

**Step 1 — Availability:**

$$
A = \dfrac{T_{\text{run}}}{T_{\text{planned}}} = \dfrac{440}{480} = 0.9167 = 91.67\%
$$

**Step 2 — Performance:**

The number of bottles that *should* have been produced at the ideal rate in 440 minutes:

$$
N_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{run}}}{T_{\text{ideal}}} = \dfrac{440}{0.04} = 11{,}000 \;\text{bottles}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{T_{\text{ideal}} \times N_{\text{total}}}{T_{\text{run}}} = \dfrac{0.04 \times 12{,}000}{440} = \dfrac{480}{440} = 1.0909
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Performance > 1.00 is physically impossible in standard OEE definition.** This indicates an *inconsistency* in the problem data — either the line ran faster than ideal (rare) or the stated total exceeds the physically possible count. In an exam, you should: **(i)** compute P as shown, **(ii)** cap it at 1.00 for OEE, **or (iii)** flag the anomaly. Examiners reward the *flagging*.

**Corrected form (capped):**

$$
P_{\text{capped}} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{0.04 \times 12{,}000}{440},\; 1.00\right) = 1.00
$$

**Step 3 — Quality:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{N_{\text{good}}}{N_{\text{total}}} = \dfrac{11{,}400}{12{,}000} = 0.95 = 95.00\%
$$

**Step 4 — OEE:**

$$
\text{OEE} = A \times P_{\text{capped}} \times Q = 0.9167 \times 1.00 \times 0.95 = 0.8708 = 87.08\%
$$

**Interpretation:** OEE ≈ **87.08%** is **world-class**. The bottleneck is **Availability** (downtime losses). Recommendation: invest in **predictive maintenance** on the 10 servo conveyors.

---

### Model Solution — Part (b) — Predictive Maintenance via IIoT

**Architecture:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph field["Field Layer"]
        VB["Vibration Sensor\nIEPE / MEMS"]
        TC["Temperature Sensor\nRTD"]
        CS["Current Sensor\nHall-effect"]
    end

    subgraph edge["Edge Layer"]
        IOTG["IIoT Gateway\nSiemens IOT2050"]
    end

    subgraph plant["Plant Network"]
        PLC["S7-1500 PLC\nProfinet"]
        SCADA["WinCC SCADA"]
    end

    subgraph cloud["Cloud / MES Layer"]
        ANALYTICS["Predictive Analytics\nPython / AWS IoT"]
        MES["MES — OEE Dashboard"]
        ALERT["Maintenance Alert System"]
    end

    VB -->|4-20mA / IO-Link| IOTG
    TC --> IOTG
    CS --> IOTG
    IOTG -->|MQTT over TLS| PLC
    IOTG -->|OPC UA| SCADA
    PLC --> SCADA
    SCADA -->|SQL / REST| MES
    IOTG -->|MQTT| ANALYTICS
    ANALYTICS -->|Threshold Breach| ALERT
    ALERT --> MES

    classDef fieldStyle fill:#ffe5e5,stroke:#c0392b
    classDef edgeStyle fill:#fff4e5,stroke:#e67e22
    classDef plantStyle fill:#e5ffe5,stroke:#27ae60
    classDef cloudStyle fill:#e5e5ff,stroke:#2c3e50

    class VB,TC,CS fieldStyle
    class IOTG edgeStyle
    class PLC,SCADA plantStyle
    class ANALYTICS,MES,ALERT cloudStyle
```

**Predictive Strategy — Justification:**

| Metric | Reactive Maintenance | Predictive (Proposed) |
|--------|----------------------|----------------------|
| MTBF | 2,000 h (reactive, run-to-failure) | **5,500 h** (early detection) |
| MTTR | 4 h (diagnosis delay) | **45 min** (pre-staged spare) |
| Availability | 91.7% (current) | **96.5%** (target) |

**Vibration Analysis Techniques Used:**

- **Time Domain:** RMS velocity, crest factor, kurtosis
- **Frequency Domain:** FFT spectrum — peak at 1×, 2× rotating frequency indicates imbalance, looseness, or bearing defect
- **Envelope Analysis:** Demodulated high-frequency resonance reveals early bearing-race pitting

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
>
> 1. **Never** claim that IIoT predictive maintenance is "fully accurate". State that it has a **false-positive rate of ~5–10%** depending on training data quality.
> 2. You **must** mention that the vibration sensor + gateway link must use **OPC UA over TLS** for cybersecurity per **IEC 62443**.
> 3. A common mark-loss: students forget to map the predictive **data flow** to the **MES layer** — the answer should clearly state *which database* and *which dashboard* receives the alert.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔴 High-Priority Revision Bullets — Print This Section**

- ✅ **OEE = Availability × Performance × Quality** — World-class threshold is **≥ 85%**.
- ✅ **ISA-95 / Purdue Model** has **5 levels** (L0–L4). Know at least **3 components per level**.
- ✅ **PLC** → discrete, fast, ladder logic. **DCS** → continuous, distributed, redundant.
- ✅ **SCADA** = supervisory monitoring & control, not real-time control. **Latency ≤ 2 s**.
- ✅ **MTBF** (reliability) ↑ good. **MTTR** (maintainability) ↓ good.
- ✅ **ATEX Zones:** Zone 0 (continuous), Zone 1 (occasional), Zone 2 (rare) — equipment must be **Ex d / Ex e / Ex i** rated accordingly.
- ✅ **SIL levels** (1–4) determined per **IEC 61511**. Pharmaceutical reactors and refinery ESDs typically target **SIL 2 or 3**.
- ✅ **PFD** (Probability of Failure on Demand) decreases by **one order of magnitude per SIL level**.
- ✅ **PID** output: $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \frac{de}{dt}$. PI eliminates steady-state error; PD improves transient response.
- ✅ **Cascade control** is the de facto architecture for **exothermic reactors**, **distillation columns**, and **furnaces**.
- ✅ **Fieldbus protocols:** Profinet (Siemens), EtherNet/IP (Rockwell), Modbus TCP, Foundation Fieldbus (process), HART (legacy 4–20 mA enhancement).
- ✅ **IIoT data flow** is **Field → Edge Gateway → PLC/SCADA → MES → Cloud Analytics**. Use **MQTT** or **OPC UA** as the transport.
- ✅ **Cybersecurity** is governed by **IEC 62443** with **Security Levels SL 1–4**. Critical infrastructure demands **SL 3 minimum**.
- ✅ **Predictive maintenance** uses **vibration (FFT, envelope), thermography, oil analysis, and motor current signature analysis (MCSA)**.
- ✅ **Vision system cycle time** for label inspection must be **≤ conveyor dwell time** — calculate from line speed: $T_{\text{vision}} \leq \frac{L_{\text{label zone}}}{v_{\text{conveyor}}}$.
- ✅ For a **14-mark KTU question**, always include: **(i)** an ISA-95 diagram, **(ii)** I/O list, **(iii)** control strategy with at least one P&ID symbol or ladder snippet, **(iv)** KPI calculation, **(v)** safety/cybersecurity mention.

> [!TIP]
> **🎯 Last-Minute Exam Strategy:**
> If you have 7 minutes left and a 14-mark case study question is incomplete, draw the **ISA-95 block diagram** (worth 3–4 marks) and **state the OEE formula with your computed value** (worth 2 marks). These two artifacts alone can rescue ~50% of the mark.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advanced PLC Programming Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advanced PLC Programming Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Advanced PLC Programming Techniques** refer to a sophisticated suite of programming methodologies, instruction sets, and structured design philosophies that go beyond elementary ladder logic (LAD) to solve complex industrial automation problems. As defined in the IEC 61131-3 international standard governing PLC programming, these techniques encompass **Sequential Function Charts (SFC)**, **Function Block Diagrams (FBD)**, **Structured Text (ST)**, **Instruction Lists (IL)**, and advanced ladder constructs such as program organization using subroutines, interrupt-driven routines, data manipulation, and closed-loop control algorithms.

In the KTU 2024 scheme context for PERUT632, *Advanced PLC Programming Techniques* specifically emphasize:

- **Structured programming** using subroutines, user-defined function blocks (UDFBs), and programs
- **Data handling** using data blocks (DBs), arrays, and structured data types
- **Analog I/O scaling and processing** for continuous process variables
- **PID control** for closed-loop regulation
- **Sequential control** using step-bit sequencers and SFC
- **Communication programming** for distributed I/O and networked PLCs

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic is mapped to **CO3 (Module 3)** with the cognitive level *Apply / Analyze*. Students must be able to *develop, analyze, and debug* advanced PLC programs for real-world automation scenarios.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine a basic PLC program as a **single cook** following one recipe from start to finish — a linear sequence of "if hot, then stir; if done, plate it." This works for simple dishes.

An **Advanced PLC Program** is like a **professional kitchen brigade**:
- The **head chef (Main Program / OB1)** coordinates overall service
- **Sous chefs (Subroutines / SBRs)** handle specific tasks (sauces, grills, desserts)
- **Specialist stations (Function Blocks)** perform repeated tasks like "deep-fry" or "boil-water" identically each time
- **Sensors (Inputs)** are like waiters reporting customer requests and food temperature
- **Actuators (Outputs)** are like the serving staff plating and delivering food
- **The recipe book (Data Blocks / DBs)** stores the exact measurements, temperatures, and timing for each dish

The head chef doesn't need to know *how* to deep-fry — they just call the deep-fry specialist station. This is the essence of **structured, modular programming**: reusability, scalability, and maintainability.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics in PLC Programming

> [!NOTE]
> The following engineering parameters and standard metrics are central to this topic:
> - **Scan Cycle Time (T_scan)**: Typically **5–20 ms** for modern PLCs; the time taken to read inputs, execute logic, and update outputs.
> - **I/O Update Rate**: Measured in **Hertz (Hz)**, critical for high-speed applications.
> - **Resolution of Analog I/O**: **12-bit, 14-bit, or 16-bit** corresponding to **4096, 16384, or 65536** discrete steps.
> - **Engineering Units Scaling**: Conversion between raw counts (e.g., **0–27648** for Siemens, **0–32767** for Allen-Bradley) and real-world values (e.g., 4–20 mA → 0–100 °C).
> - **PID Tuning Constants**: Kc (proportional gain), Ti (integral time in seconds), Td (derivative time in seconds).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of Advanced PLC Programming

Advanced PLC programming is built upon the following hierarchical layers:

### 2.1.1 Program Organization Units (POUs)
- **Organization Blocks (OBs)**: Main cyclic, time-based, or event-triggered entry points
- **Functions (FCs)**: Stateless subroutines that return a single value
- **Function Blocks (FBs)**: Stateful code blocks with associated **Instance Data Blocks (DBs)** for memory persistence
- **Data Blocks (DBs)**: Dedicated memory areas storing variables, parameters, and recipes

### 2.1.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Concept

| Concept | Why It Exists | How It Works |
|---------|---------------|--------------|
| **Subroutines (SBR)** | Avoid code duplication | Code is written once and called from multiple locations, reducing program scan time and easing maintenance |
| **User-Defined Function Blocks (UDFB)** | Reusable control algorithms (e.g., motor starter, valve controller) | Each instance has its own data block, allowing the same logic to control multiple physical devices |
| **Sequential Function Charts (SFC)** | Visualize complex step-by-step processes | Uses **Steps (states)**, **Transitions (conditions)**, and **Actions (outputs)** to model state machines |
| **Data Blocks (DB)** | Centralize process data and recipes | Stores setpoints, tuning constants, alarm limits — easily modified without changing logic |
| **Analog Scaling** | Bridge raw ADC counts to engineering units | Linear equation mapping raw range to physical range |
| **PID Control** | Regulate continuous processes (temperature, pressure, flow) | Three-term controller minimizing error between setpoint and process variable |
| **High-Speed Counters (HSC)** | Count pulses from encoders beyond normal scan rate | Hardware counters independent of scan cycle, accessed via dedicated instructions |
| **Interrupt Routines** | Time-critical response to events | Triggered by hardware events, time intervals, or process conditions, preempting the main scan |

### 2.1.3 The IEC 61131-3 Programming Languages

The international standard **IEC 61131-3** defines **five (5)** programming languages for PLCs, categorized as:

**Textual Languages:**
- **Instruction List (IL)** — low-level, assembly-like (largely deprecated)
- **Structured Text (ST)** — high-level, Pascal-like (used for complex math, loops, conditional logic)

**Graphical Languages:**
- **Ladder Diagram (LD)** — relay-logic inspired (most common in industry)
- **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** — signal-flow based, excellent for continuous control
- **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** — state-transition based, ideal for batch processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "list the IEC 61131-3 languages," always state all **5** and briefly mention each — partial answers lose marks.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\vert` for absolute value to preserve markdown table integrity. The following table contains all high-yield formulas for this topic.**

| Concept | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application Context |
|---------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| **Analog Input Scaling (Linear)** | $E = \left( \dfrac{R - R_{low}}{R_{high} - R_{low}} \right) \cdot (E_{high} - E_{low}) + E_{low}$ | $E$ = Engineering value; $R$ = Raw count; $R_{low},R_{high}$ = Raw min/max; $E_{low},E_{high}$ = Eng. min/max | Converting 0–10V or 4–20mA signals to °C, bar, L/min |
| **Analog Output Scaling** | $R = \left( \dfrac{E - E_{low}}{E_{high} - E_{low}} \right) \cdot (R_{high} - R_{low}) + R_{low}$ | Same as above, inverted | Driving valves, VFDs from setpoint values |
| **PID Control Output** | $u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$ | $u(t)$ = controller output; $e(t)$ = error = SP − PV; $K_c$ = gain; $T_i$ = integral time (s); $T_d$ = derivative time (s) | Closed-loop regulation of temperature, pressure |
| **Discrete PID (Velocity Form)** | $\Delta u_n = K_c \left[ (e_n - e_{n-1}) + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i} e_n + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2}) \right]$ | $T_s$ = sample time (s); subscripts denote sampling instants | PLC-implementable PID avoiding integral windup |
| **High-Speed Counter Frequency** | $f = \dfrac{N_{pulses}}{\Delta t}$ | $f$ = frequency (Hz); $N$ = pulse count; $\Delta t$ = measurement window (s) | Speed measurement from incremental encoders |
| **Encoder Resolution** | $\theta = \dfrac{360°}{PPR \cdot 4}$ | $\theta$ = angular resolution (deg); $PPR$ = pulses per revolution; factor 4 for quadrature decoding | Position control accuracy |
| **Scan Cycle Time Estimate** | $T_{scan} \approx T_{input} + T_{logic} + T_{output} + T_{comm}$ | Each term in milliseconds | System responsiveness |
| **Bumpless Transfer** | $\Delta u_{manual \to auto} = u_{last,manual} - u_{PID}(e_{last})$ | Difference applied at handover | Smooth transition from manual to auto mode |
| **Anti-Windup (Back-Calculation)** | $u_{sat} = K_c \cdot e + K_c \cdot T_d \cdot \dot{e} + K_{track}(u - u_{sat})$ | $K_{track} = \dfrac{1}{T_t}$; $T_t$ = tracking time constant | Prevents integral saturation when output is clamped |
| **Signal Conditioning (RTD)** | $R_{RTD}(T) = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^2 \right]$ | $R_0$ = resistance at 0°C; $\alpha,\beta$ = Callendar–Van Dusen coefficients | Platinum PT100/PT1000 linearization |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Assembly Lines**: Sequential Function Charts coordinate robotic welding, painting, and assembly stations
- **Power Plants**: PID loops regulate boiler drum level, steam temperature, and turbine speed
- **Water Treatment Plants**: Analog I/O scaling for pH, turbidity, and flow; PID for chemical dosing
- **Packaging Industries**: High-speed counters track conveyor speeds and product counts
- **Pharmaceutical Batch Processes**: SFC + Data Blocks enable **recipe-driven production** compliant with FDA 21 CFR Part 11

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Analog Input Scaling Equation

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU exams in this module. Students are expected to derive the linear scaling equation from first principles.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

A pressure transmitter outputs a **4–20 mA** signal corresponding to a process range of **0–10 bar**. The PLC analog input module is configured for **0–20 mA** with a raw digital range of **0–27648 counts** (Siemens S7 convention). Derive the equation to convert raw counts ($R$) into engineering units ($E$ in bar).

### 3.1.2 Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the known endpoints in raw domain.**

The current signal 4–20 mA maps to the raw count range used by the PLC. Since the analog input module receives 0–20 mA, we need to find the raw count corresponding to 4 mA.

The relationship between current and raw count is linear:

$$
\dfrac{R - 0}{27648 - 0} = \dfrac{I - 0}{20 - 0}
$$

where $I$ is the input current in mA. This gives:

$$
R = 27648 \cdot \dfrac{I}{20}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute raw counts at the two calibration points.**

For $I_{low} = 4$ mA:

$$
R_{low} = 27648 \cdot \dfrac{4}{20} = 27648 \cdot 0.20 = 5529.6
$$

For $I_{high} = 20$ mA:

$$
R_{high} = 27648 \cdot \dfrac{20}{20} = 27648
$$

**Step 3 — Write the linear mapping between raw and engineering domains.**

The general linear relationship between $R$ and $E$ takes the form:

$$
E = m \cdot R + c
$$

where $m$ is the slope and $c$ is the intercept.

**Step 4 — Apply the two-point form of a straight line.**

Using the two known mapping points $(R_{low}, E_{low}) = (5529.6, 0)$ and $(R_{high}, E_{high}) = (27648, 10)$:

$$
\dfrac{E - E_{low}}{E_{high} - E_{low}} = \dfrac{R - R_{low}}{R_{high} - R_{low}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $E$ in terms of $R$.**

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{R - R_{low}}{R_{high} - R_{low}} \right) \cdot (E_{high} - E_{low}) + E_{low}
$$

This is the **canonical scaling equation** used in virtually every PLC scaling subroutine.

**Step 6 — Substitute the numerical values.**

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{R - 5529.6}{27648 - 5529.6} \right) \cdot (10 - 0) + 0
$$

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{R - 5529.6}{22118.4} \right) \cdot 10
$$

**Step 7 — Verification.**

Check at $R = 5529.6$ (4 mA):

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{5529.6 - 5529.6}{22118.4} \right) \cdot 10 = 0 \text{ bar} \quad \checkmark
$$

Check at $R = 27648$ (20 mA):

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{27648 - 5529.6}{22118.4} \right) \cdot 10 = \left( \dfrac{22118.4}{22118.4} \right) \cdot 10 = 10 \text{ bar} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Scaling Equation:**
> $$\boxed{\,E = \left( \dfrac{R - 5529.6}{22118.4} \right) \cdot 10\,} \quad \text{[bar]}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Discrete-Time PID Algorithm

The continuous-time PID equation from Section 2.2 cannot be directly implemented in a PLC. The integral and derivative must be discretized.

### 3.2.1 Discretizing the Integral Term

The integral is approximated using the **rectangular (forward Euler)** method:

$$
\int_0^{t_n} e(\tau)\,d\tau \approx T_s \cdot \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k
$$

where $T_s$ is the sampling period and $e_k = e(kT_s)$.

### 3.2.2 Discretizing the Derivative Term

The derivative is approximated using the **backward difference**:

$$
\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg\vert_{t=t_n} \approx \dfrac{e_n - e_{n-1}}{T_s}
$$

### 3.2.3 Substituting into the PID Equation

$$
u_n = K_c \left[ e_n + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e_k + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - e_{n-1}) \right]
$$

**Step 1 — Form the velocity (incremental) output.**

To avoid accumulator windup, compute $\Delta u_n = u_n - u_{n-1}$:

$$
u_n - u_{n-1} = K_c \left[ (e_n - e_{n-1}) + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i} e_n + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2}) \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Final discrete velocity-form PID.**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta u_n = K_c \left[ (e_n - e_{n-1}) + \dfrac{T_s}{T_i} e_n + \dfrac{T_d}{T_s}(e_n - 2e_{n-1} + e_{n-2}) \right]\,}
$$

**Step 3 — The complete output update.**

$$
u_n = u_{n-1} + \Delta u_n
$$

This form is preferred in PLCs because:
- It uses **only past and present errors** — no integrator accumulator
- **Bumpless transfer** from manual to auto mode is automatic
- It is **immune to integral windup** when output is clamped

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Selecting the Right Technique

| Problem Characteristic | Recommended Technique | Justification |
|------------------------|------------------------|---------------|
| Repeating the same action on many devices (e.g., 50 motors) | **User-Defined Function Block (UDFB)** | Write logic once, instantiate 50 times with separate instance DBs |
| Batch process with distinct phases (charge → heat → hold → cool) | **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** | Graphical state machine naturally models step-based processes |
| Complex math, loops, string parsing, array processing | **Structured Text (ST)** | High-level constructs unavailable in ladder logic |
| Continuous control of temperature/pressure | **PID with anti-windup** | Industry-proven three-term controller |
| Recipe management with many setpoints | **Data Blocks (DB)** | Centralize parameters, change recipes without editing logic |
| Time-critical events (e.g., emergency stop) | **Hardware Interrupts** | Pre-empt scan cycle for sub-millisecond response |
| High-frequency pulse counting (e.g., encoder > 10 kHz) | **High-Speed Counter (HSC)** | Hardware counter independent of scan time |
| Coordinated motion of multiple axes | **Motion Control Blocks / PLCopen** | Standardized function blocks for servo/stepper control |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
List the **five (5) programming languages** defined by the IEC 61131-3 international standard for PLCs. Classify them as textual or graphical.

**Model Answer:**

The IEC 61131-3 standard defines five PLC programming languages:

1. **Instruction List (IL)** — *Textual*; low-level, assembly-like syntax
2. **Structured Text (ST)** — *Textual*; high-level, Pascal-like, used for complex math and loops
3. **Ladder Diagram (LD)** — *Graphical*; relay-logic inspired, most widely used
4. **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** — *Graphical*; signal-flow based, ideal for continuous control
5. **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** — *Graphical*; state-transition based, used for batch processes

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Note:** 1 mark for each correct language; 1 mark for the correct textual/graphical classification of any two.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define the term **"Function Block"** in the context of PLC programming. How does it differ from a regular **subroutine**?

**Model Answer:**

A **Function Block (FB)** is a reusable, stateful Program Organization Unit (POU) that encapsulates a control algorithm along with its persistent data. When a function block is "called," an **Instance Data Block (Instance DB)** is automatically created to hold its internal variables (memory), so the FB "remembers" its state between scans.

**Difference from a Subroutine:**

| Feature | Subroutine (SBR/FC) | Function Block (FB) |
|---------|----------------------|---------------------|
| State | Stateless (no memory) | Stateful (has associated instance DB) |
| Reuse | Single memory area | Each call gets its own instance DB |
| Typical use | One-time calculations | Repeated control of similar devices (e.g., multiple motors) |
| Data persistence | Lost between calls | Persists across scan cycles |

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Note:** 1 mark for FB definition, 1 mark for the key role of instance DB, 1 mark for at least two differentiating points.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Sub-Question Choice Set A

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**Question A (a)** [7 Marks]
Explain the **analog input scaling procedure** in a PLC with the help of the linear scaling equation. A level transmitter outputs **4–20 mA** for a tank level range of **0–5 meters**. The PLC analog input module is configured for **0–20 mA** with a raw count range of **0–27648**. Derive the equation to convert raw counts into level in meters and compute the level when the raw count is **13824**.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Concept of Analog Scaling (2 marks).**

Analog signals (4–20 mA or 0–10 V) are continuous process variables that the PLC converts into **raw digital counts** using an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). These raw counts must be mapped to **engineering units** (meters, °C, bar, etc.) using a linear scaling equation so that operators and control algorithms can use them.

**Step 2 — Identify Endpoints in the Raw Domain (1 mark).**

For a 4–20 mA signal on a 0–20 mA module with raw range 0–27648:

$$
R_{low} = 27648 \cdot \dfrac{4}{20} = 5529.6
$$

$$
R_{high} = 27648 \cdot \dfrac{20}{20} = 27648
$$

**Step 3 — Identify Endpoints in the Engineering Domain (1 mark).**

$$
E_{low} = 0 \text{ m}, \quad E_{high} = 5 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Write and Apply the Scaling Equation (2 marks).**

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{R - R_{low}}{R_{high} - R_{low}} \right) \cdot (E_{high} - E_{low}) + E_{low}
$$

Substituting:

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{R - 5529.6}{27648 - 5529.6} \right) \cdot 5 = \left( \dfrac{R - 5529.6}{22118.4} \right) \cdot 5
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Level at R = 13824 (1 mark).**

$$
E = \left( \dfrac{13824 - 5529.6}{22118.4} \right) \cdot 5 = \left( \dfrac{8294.4}{22118.4} \right) \cdot 5
$$

$$
E = 0.375 \cdot 5 = 1.875 \text{ m}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The level in the tank is 1.875 meters when the raw ADC count is 13824.**

---

**Question A (b)** [7 Marks]
With a neat block diagram, describe the **closed-loop PID control system** as implemented in a PLC. Explain the function of each block and state the standard PID equation with the meaning of each term.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Description (3 marks).**

The closed-loop PID control system consists of the following interconnected blocks:

1. **Setpoint (SP)**: Desired value of the process variable
2. **Summing Junction**: Computes the error $e(t) = SP - PV$
3. **PID Controller** (resides in PLC): Generates control output $u(t)$
4. **Actuator** (valve/heater/VFD): Manipulates the process
5. **Plant (Process)**: The physical system being controlled
6. **Sensor (Transmitter)**: Measures the actual process variable
7. **Analog Input Module + Scaling Block**: Brings feedback into engineering units

**Step 2 — Standard PID Equation (2 marks).**

$$
u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_i} \int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt} \right]
$$

**Step 3 — Meaning of Each Term (2 marks).**

| Term | Meaning | Effect |
|------|---------|--------|
| $K_c$ | Proportional gain | Produces output proportional to current error; reduces rise time |
| $T_i$ | Integral time (seconds) | Eliminates steady-state error; can cause overshoot |
| $T_d$ | Derivative time (seconds) | Anticipates future error; damps oscillations |
| $e(t)$ | Error = SP − PV | Deviation of process from setpoint |
| $u(t)$ | Controller output | Signal sent to actuator |

---

### Question B — Sub-Question Choice Set B

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 3 | 14 Marks]**

**Question B (a)** [7 Marks]
What is a **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)**? Explain its elements with a suitable example of a **bottle-filling plant** having the following steps: *Start → Fill → Cap → Label → Conveyor Advance → Repeat*.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Definition of SFC (2 marks).**

A **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** is a graphical programming language defined in IEC 61131-3 used to model **discrete, step-based processes** as state machines. An SFC consists of:

- **Steps (states)**: Represent phases of the process; each step has an associated **action**
- **Transitions**: Logical conditions that permit progression from one step to the next
- **Directed Links**: Connect steps through transitions
- **Actions**: Outputs executed while a step is active (Set, Reset, time-delayed, etc.)

**Step 2 — Bottle-Filling SFC Description (3 marks).**

The bottle-filling process can be modeled as:

1. **Step S0 (Initial/Start)**: System idle, conveyor stopped, awaiting Start pushbutton
   - Action: `Conveyor_Motor := OFF`
2. **Transition T1**: `Start_PB = TRUE` AND `Bottle_Detected = TRUE`
3. **Step S1 (Fill)**: Fill valve opens for a preset time or until level sensor trips
   - Action: `Fill_Valve := ON`; Timer `Fill_Timer` starts
4. **Transition T2**: `Fill_Timer.DN = TRUE`
5. **Step S2 (Cap)**: Capping head descends and applies cap
   - Action: `Cap_Cylinder := EXTEND`
6. **Transition T3**: `Cap_Done_Sensor = TRUE`
7. **Step S3 (Label)**: Label dispenser applies label
   - Action: `Label_Dispenser := ON`
8. **Transition T4**: `Label_Applied_Sensor = TRUE`
9. **Step S4 (Conveyor Advance)**: Conveyor moves bottle to next station
   - Action: `Conveyor_Motor := ON` for 2 seconds
10. **Transition T5**: `Conveyor_Timer.DN = TRUE`
11. **Return to Step S1 (or S0)** to repeat the cycle

**Step 3 — Advantages of SFC (2 marks).**

- Easy visualization of process flow
- Clear separation of logic into discrete states
- Simplified debugging — operator can see which step is active
- Direct mapping to **Grafcet** (the European predecessor) and **ISA-88** batch standards

---

**Question B (b)** [7 Marks]
Explain the concept of **structured programming in PLCs** using **Subroutines, Function Blocks, and Data Blocks**. State the benefits of structured programming over a single monolithic ladder program.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Subroutines (2 marks).**

A **subroutine** is a self-contained block of code that performs a specific task and is called from the main program (or another subroutine) using a subroutine call instruction. Subroutines are *stateless* — they do not retain variable values between calls. They are ideal for **one-time calculations, initialization routines, and conversion utilities** (e.g., a Fahrenheit-to-Celsius conversion subroutine).

**Step 2 — Function Blocks (2 marks).**

A **Function Block (FB)** is a *stateful* POU that encapsulates both code and persistent data. Each time a function block is called, a unique **Instance Data Block** is created to store its internal variables. This allows the same FB to control multiple similar devices independently. For example, a single `Motor_Starter` FB with instances for `Motor_1_DB`, `Motor_2_DB`, `Motor_3_DB` can independently start, stop, and protect 50 different motors with one piece of code.

**Step 3 — Data Blocks (1 mark).**

A **Data Block (DB)** is a dedicated memory region used to store variables such as setpoints, alarm thresholds, tuning parameters, and recipes. Global DBs are accessible from any POU; instance DBs are tied to specific FBs.

**Step 4 — Benefits of Structured Programming (2 marks).**

| Benefit | Explanation |
|---------|-------------|
| **Reusability** | Write once, use many times — reduces program size |
| **Maintainability** | Faults are localized; fixing one FB does not affect others |
| **Scalability** | Adding a new device = adding a new instance, not new code |
| **Readability** | Program structure mirrors the physical process |
| **Team Development** | Different engineers can develop different FBs in parallel |
| **Reduced Scan Time** | Conditional subroutine calls skip unused logic |

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 4 mA offset** in 4–20 mA scaling — students often use 0 as the lower raw count, leading to a **2-mark deduction** for the wrong $R_{low}$
> 2. **Mixing up $R_{low}$ and $E_{low}$** in the scaling formula — always double-check which variable belongs to the raw domain and which to the engineering domain
> 3. **Confusing Function Block with Subroutine** — a Function Block *must* have an instance DB; if you say "they are the same," expect a 2-mark penalty
> 4. **Forgetting to state all 5 IEC 61131-3 languages** in a 3-mark question — partial answers cap at 2 marks
> 5. **Writing the PID integral term as $K_i \cdot e(t)$ instead of $K_c \cdot \frac{1}{T_i} \int e\,dt$** — the standard form uses $K_c$ and $T_i$ together; both are required
> 6. **Not mentioning anti-windup** when discussing PID — the 2024 scheme CO3 explicitly requires "analyze" level responses, so a 2-mark sub-question often tests this
> 7. **Sketching SFC diagrams without transitions** — a "step" without a transition condition is incomplete and loses 1 mark

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Advanced PLC Programming Techniques**

### IEC 61131-3 Languages
- **5 languages**: IL, ST, LD, FBD, SFC
- **2 textual**: IL, ST | **3 graphical**: LD, FBD, SFC

### Program Organization Units (POUs)
- **OB (Organization Block)**: Main entry point; OB1 is the cyclic main
- **FC (Function)**: Stateless, returns one value
- **FB (Function Block)**: Stateful, has instance DB
- **DB (Data Block)**: Memory for variables and recipes

### Analog I/O Scaling
- **Input scaling**: $E = \dfrac{(R - R_{low})}{(R_{high} - R_{low})} \cdot (E_{high} - E_{low}) + E_{low}$
- **Output scaling**: inverse of the above
- **Standard 4–20 mA** → Siemens raw range is **0–27648** at **0–20 mA**; $R_{low} = 5529.6$

### PID Control
- **Equation**: $u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \dfrac{1}{T_i} \int e\,dt + T_d \dfrac{de}{dt} \right]$
- **Discrete form**: Velocity algorithm avoids integral windup
- **Anti-windup**: Back-calculation or clamping
- **Bumpless transfer**: Required when switching from Manual to Auto

### Sequential Function Chart (SFC)
- **Elements**: Steps, Transitions, Actions, Directed Links
- **Use case**: Batch processes, state machines
- **Standard**: IEC 61131-3, ISA-88, Grafcet

### High-Speed Counting & Interrupts
- **HSC**: Hardware counters for >10 kHz pulse trains
- **Quadrature decoding**: factor of 4 over base PPR
- **Interrupts**: Time-of-day, cyclic, hardware-triggered (e.g., emergency stop)

### Structured Programming Benefits
- Reusability, maintainability, scalability, readability, faster scan, team development

### Key Numerical Values to Memorize
- **27648** (Siemens 0–20 mA max raw count)
- **4–20 mA** industrial current loop standard
- **0–10 V** industrial voltage standard
- **0–65535** (16-bit unsigned range)
- **−32768 to +32767** (16-bit signed range)

### Common Real-World Sensors (for scaling problems)
- **PT100 RTD**: 100 Ω at 0°C, ~138.5 Ω at 100°C
- **Thermocouple Type K**: −200°C to +1370°C, ~41 μV/°C
- **Strain gauge load cell**: 1–3 mV/V excitation
- **Differential pressure (DP) transmitter**: for level/flow

### Exam Pattern Memory Aid
- **Part A (3 marks)**: Always expect direct concept definitions or short explanations
- **Part B (14 marks)**: 2 sub-parts of 7 marks each; expect **derivation + application** in (a) and **diagram + explanation** in (b)
- **Common CO3 verbs**: "Derive," "Implement," "Apply," "Analyze"

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Networking and Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PLC Networking and Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**PLC Networking and Communication** refers to the systematic interconnection of multiple Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs), Human-Machine Interfaces (HMIs), Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) stations, Variable Frequency Drives (VFDs), Remote I/O stations, sensors, and actuators through standardized industrial communication protocols. It enables real-time, deterministic exchange of process data, control commands, diagnostics, and configuration parameters across a manufacturing plant, forming the digital backbone of **Industrial Automation Pyramid Levels 0–3**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Automation Pyramid (ISA-95 / IEC 62264)**
> - **Level 0** — Field devices (sensors, actuators, motors)
> - **Level 1** — Control devices (PLCs, DCS controllers)
> - **Level 2** — Supervisory systems (SCADA, HMIs)
> - **Level 3** — Manufacturing Operations Management (MES / ERP)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a PLC network like a **bus system in a city**:
- Each **PLC** is a *bus stop* that has passengers (data) to drop and pick up.
- The **communication cable** (RS-485, Ethernet, Profibus) is the *road* connecting all stops.
- The **protocol** (Modbus, Profinet, EtherNet/IP) is the *traffic rule* that decides which bus goes first, who speaks when, and what language the conductor speaks.
- Without protocols, every device would speak simultaneously — total chaos. With protocols, the system runs predictably like a well-managed metro.

### 1.3 Why PLC Networking is Essential

| Industrial Need | How Networking Solves It |
|---|---|
| Distributed control across long distances | Replaces hardwiring with single communication bus |
| Centralized monitoring | SCADA/HMI polls multiple PLCs |
| Data logging | Historical data aggregated from PLCs to server |
| Inter-PLC coordination | Master PLC writes commands to Slave PLCs |
| Reduced wiring cost | Multi-drop bus replaces point-to-point |
| Remote diagnostics | Engineers access PLCs over LAN/WAN |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metrics to remember:**
> - **RS-485 maximum nodes per segment:** 32 (with repeaters: up to 256)
> - **Standard baud rates:** 9600, 19200, 38400, 115200 bps
> - **Maximum cable length (RS-485):** 1200 meters at low baud rates
> - **Industrial Ethernet speed:** 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 OSI Seven-Layer Model in Industrial Context

The **Open Systems Interconnection (OSI)** model is a 7-layer reference model. Industrial protocols typically operate over a subset of these layers.

| OSI Layer | Function | Industrial Examples |
|---|---|---|
| 7 — Application | User-level services | Modbus, Profinet, OPC UA |
| 6 — Presentation | Data formatting, encryption | OPC UA (binary encoding) |
| 5 — Session | Connection management | TCP sockets |
| 4 — Transport | End-to-end reliability | TCP (reliable), UDP (fast) |
| 3 — Network | Routing | IP addressing |
| 2 — Data Link | Framing, MAC addressing | Ethernet, RS-485 |
| 1 — Physical | Electrical signaling | Twisted pair, fiber |

> [!NOTE]
> Most fieldbus protocols like **Modbus RTU** operate only on Layers **1, 2, and 7** — these are called *"3-layer protocols"*. Industrial Ethernet protocols (Profinet, EtherNet/IP) use full Layer 1–7 TCP/IP stack.

### 2.2 Network Topologies

#### 2.2.1 Bus Topology (Most common in fieldbus)
- All devices connected to a single backbone cable.
- Terminator resistors (typically **120 Ω**) at both ends prevent signal reflection.
- Example: **Profibus DP**, **Modbus RTU over RS-485**.

#### 2.2.2 Star Topology
- Central hub/switch connects all devices.
- Failure of one node does not affect others.
- Example: **Industrial Ethernet with managed switches**.

#### 2.2.3 Ring Topology
- Devices connected in closed loop; data flows in one direction.
- Redundancy achieved via Dual Ring (e.g., **Media Redundancy Protocol — MRP**).

#### 2.2.4 Tree / Hierarchical
- Combination of star and bus. Common in large plants with cell-area networks.

#### 2.2.5 Mesh Topology
- Every node connects to multiple other nodes. Provides highest reliability.
- Used in **WirelessHART**, **ISA 100.11a**.

### 2.3 Serial Communication Standards

| Standard | Mode | Max Devices | Max Distance | Speed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RS-232** | Full duplex, point-to-point | 1 | 15 m | 20 kbps |
| **RS-422** | Full duplex, multi-drop | 10 | 1200 m | 10 Mbps |
| **RS-485** | Half/Full duplex, multi-drop | 32 (256 with repeaters) | 1200 m | 10 Mbps |

> [!IMPORTANT]
