> **RS-485 is the workhorse of industrial serial communication** because it uses **differential signaling** (D+/D− lines twisted together), which cancels out common-mode noise — ideal for electrically noisy factory floors.

### 2.4 Industrial Communication Protocols — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Protocol | Layer | Media | Topology | Speed | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Modbus RTU** | 1, 2, 7 | RS-485 | Bus | 115.2 kbps | Classic PLC-to-PLC |
| **Modbus TCP** | 1–7 | Ethernet | Star | 100 Mbps | Modern PLC integration |
| **Profibus DP** | 1, 2, 7 | RS-485 | Bus | 12 Mbps | Siemens factory floor |
| **Profinet** | 1–7 | Ethernet | Star | 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps | Siemens modern systems |
| **EtherNet/IP** | 1–7 | Ethernet | Star | 100 Mbps | Rockwell (Allen-Bradley) |
| **DeviceNet** | 1, 2, 7 | CAN bus | Bus/Drop | 500 kbps | Rockwell low-level devices |
| **CANopen** | 1, 2, 7 | CAN bus | Bus | 1 Mbps | Motion control, automotive |
| **Foundation Fieldbus** | 1, 2, 7 | Twisted pair | Bus | 31.25 kbps | Process industries |
| **OPC UA** | 3–7 | TCP/IP | Any | 100 Mbps+ | IIoT, cloud integration |

### 2.5 Communication Models

- **Master–Slave:** One master polls multiple slaves (Modbus RTU). Deterministic.
- **Client–Server:** Server holds data, client requests (Modbus TCP).
- **Producer–Consumer:** Devices publish data on event; subscribers consume (EtherNet/IP).
- **Peer-to-Peer:** All devices equal; cyclic broadcast (Profinet CBA).

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

PLC networking is foundational to:
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** — IIoT gateways
- **SCADA systems** — polling hundreds of PLCs
- **Process Industries** — Refineries, chemical plants (Foundation Fieldbus)
- **Discrete Manufacturing** — Automotive assembly lines (Profinet, EtherNet/IP)

### 2.7 KTU Formula / Quantity Sheet

| Quantity / Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Baud Rate** | Number of signal changes per second | bps (bits/sec) |
| **Maximum cable length (RS-485)** | $\frac{10^8}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ (approx.) | meters |
| **Cyclic Time (Profinet)** | $T_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{N_{\text{devices}} \times T_{\text{frame}}}{\text{Link Speed}}$ | seconds |
| **Propagation Delay (Fiber)** | $t_p = \frac{L}{v}$ where $v \approx 2 \times 10^8 \, m/s$ | seconds |
| **Throughput Efficiency** | $\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Data}}{\text{Total Bits}} \times 100\%$ | percent |
| **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$ | dB |
| **Maximum nodes on RS-485** | 32 standard; 256 with repeaters | nodes |
| **Termination Resistance** | $R_t = Z_0$ (characteristic impedance), typically **120 Ω** | ohms |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU board examiners frequently test the relationship between baud rate and cable length. As baud rate **increases**, maximum reliable cable length **decreases** due to attenuation and capacitance effects.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Problem 1: RS-485 Cable Length vs Baud Rate

**Problem:** Calculate the approximate maximum cable length for RS-485 at baud rate of **115200 bps**. Then compare with the length at **9600 bps**.

**Given:**
- Standard approximation rule for RS-485: $L_{\max} \approx \dfrac{10^8}{\text{Baud Rate}}$ meters

**Solution (Step-by-Step):**

At 115200 bps:

$$L_{\max} = \frac{10^8}{115200}$$

$$\text{Step 1: } 10^8 = 100{,}000{,}000$$

$$\text{Step 2: } L_{\max} = \frac{100{,}000{,}000}{115200}$$

$$\text{Step 3: } L_{\max} \approx 868.05 \text{ meters}$$

At 9600 bps:

$$L_{\max} = \frac{10^8}{9600}$$

$$\text{Step 1: } L_{\max} = \frac{100{,}000{,}000}{9600}$$

$$\text{Step 2: } L_{\max} \approx 10416.67 \text{ meters}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Theoretical } L_{\max} \approx 10.42 \text{ km}$$

**Comparison:**

| Baud Rate (bps) | Max Cable Length (m) | Practical Limit (m) |
|---|---|---|
| 9600 | 10416.67 | 1200 |
| 19200 | 5208.33 | 1200 |
| 38400 | 2604.17 | 1200 |
| 115200 | 868.05 | 800 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "practical limit" of **1200 m** comes from the RS-485 electrical specification. The formula gives the *attenuation-based* theoretical limit, while real-world cable capacitance, shielding, and EMI impose tighter practical bounds.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Problem 2: Modbus RTU Frame Structure and CRC

**Problem:** A Modbus RTU frame (Function Code 03 — Read Holding Registers) is to be transmitted over RS-485. Determine:
1. The total frame size in bytes.
2. The frame transmission time at 9600 bps.
3. The CRC-16 polynomial used.

**Given:**
- Slave Address = 1 byte
- Function Code = 1 byte
- Starting Address = 2 bytes
- Quantity of Registers = 2 bytes
- CRC-16 = 2 bytes
- 1 start bit + 1 stop bit (10 bits per byte)
- Baud rate = 9600 bps

**Solution:**

$$\text{Step 1: Total bytes} = 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 8 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Total bits} = 8 \text{ bytes} \times 10 \text{ bits/byte} = 80 \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Transmission time} = \frac{\text{Total bits}}{\text{Baud Rate}} = \frac{80}{9600}$$

$$\text{Step 4: } t_{\text{frame}} = 0.008333 \text{ seconds} = 8.33 \text{ ms}$$

**CRC-16 (Modbus) Polynomial:**

$$G(x) = x^{16} + x^{15} + x^2 + 1$$

Or in hexadecimal: **0xA001** (reverse representation of 0x8005).

> [!NOTE]
> Modbus RTU frames must be separated by a **silent interval of at least 3.5 character times** ($t_{\text{silent}} = 3.5 \times \frac{11}{9600} \approx 4.01$ ms). This is how receivers identify frame boundaries.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Problem 3: Cyclic Update Time in Profinet Network

**Problem:** A Profinet network has **10 I/O devices**, each transmitting **16 bytes of process data**. The link operates at **100 Mbps**. Calculate the cyclic update time if each frame takes **2 µs for propagation + processing** and Ethernet frame overhead is **38 bytes**.

**Given:**
- Number of devices, $N = 10$
- Process data per device = 16 bytes
- Frame overhead = 38 bytes
- Link speed = $100 \times 10^6$ bps
- Per-frame overhead time = 2 µs

**Solution:**

$$\text{Step 1: Total frame size} = (16 + 38) \text{ bytes} = 54 \text{ bytes}$$

$$\text{Step 2: Bits per frame} = 54 \times 8 = 432 \text{ bits}$$

$$\text{Step 3: Transmission time per frame} = \frac{432}{100 \times 10^6}$$

$$\text{Step 4: } T_{\text{frame}} = 4.32 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 4.32 \, \mu s$$

$$\text{Step 5: Total time for all devices} = N \times (T_{\text{frame}} + T_{\text{overhead}})$$

$$\text{Step 6: } T_{\text{total}} = 10 \times (4.32 + 2) \, \mu s$$

$$\text{Step 7: } T_{\text{total}} = 10 \times 6.32 = 63.2 \, \mu s$$

**Conclusion:** The cyclic update time is **63.2 µs** — well below the typical 1 ms Profinet real-time class 1 requirement.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Common Protocols

| Criteria | Modbus RTU | Profibus DP | Profinet | EtherNet/IP |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Physical Layer | RS-485 | RS-485 / Fiber | Ethernet | Ethernet |
| Determinism | Low (polling) | High | High (IRT: <1 ms) | Medium |
| Max Nodes | 247 | 126 | Unlimited (subnets) | Unlimited |
| Typical Cycle Time | 10–100 ms | 1–10 ms | 0.25–10 ms | 5–20 ms |
| Cable Length | 1200 m | 1200 m | 100 m (copper) | 100 m (copper) |
| Vendor | Open | Siemens | Siemens | Rockwell |
| Cost | Lowest | Medium | Higher | Higher |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Problem 4: SNR Calculation for Industrial Cable

**Problem:** A shielded twisted-pair cable carries **V_signal = 5 V** in an environment with **V_noise = 0.05 V** (50 mV). Compute the SNR in dB.

**Solution:**

$$\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}}\right)$$

$$\text{Step 1: } \frac{V_{\text{signal}}}{V_{\text{noise}}} = \frac{5}{0.05} = 100$$

$$\text{Step 2: } \text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}(100)$$

$$\text{Step 3: } \log_{10}(100) = 2$$

$$\text{Step 4: } \text{SNR} = 20 \times 2 = 40 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial standards require **SNR ≥ 20 dB** for reliable communication. Values below this lead to CRC errors and retries.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards (Strictly Followed)

The following Mermaid block adheres to the **Alpha Rule** for node IDs and contains no markdown formatting inside quoted labels.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Multi-Protocol PLC Network Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    SCADA["SCADA SERVER LEVEL 3"]
    MES["MES WORKSTATION"]
    PLCmaster["MASTER PLC LEVEL 2"]
    PLCslave1["SLAVE PLC 1 CELL A"]
    PLCslave2["SLAVE PLC 2 CELL B"]
    PLCslave3["SLAVE PLC 3 CELL C"]
    HMI1["HMI PANEL CELL A"]
    HMI2["HMI PANEL CELL B"]
    VFD1["VFD MOTOR DRIVE 1"]
    VFD2["VFD MOTOR DRIVE 2"]
    RIO1["REMOTE IO STATION 1"]
    RIO2["REMOTE IO STATION 2"]
    SW1["INDUSTRIAL ETHERNET SWITCH"]
    GW1["PROFINET TO MODBUS GATEWAY"]
    BUS485["RS 485 MULTI DROP BUS"]

    SCADA -->|ETHERNET TCP IP| SW1
    MES -->|ETHERNET TCP IP| SW1
    PLCmaster -->|PROFINET| SW1
    HMI1 -->|PROFINET| SW1
    HMI2 -->|PROFINET| SW1
    SW1 -->|ETHERNET| GW1
    PLCslave1 -->|MODBUS RTU RS 485| BUS485
    PLCslave2 -->|MODBUS RTU RS 485| BUS485
    PLCslave3 -->|MODBUS RTU RS 485| BUS485
    GW1 -->|CONVERTED MODBUS TCP| BUS485
    BUS485 --> RIO1
    BUS485 --> RIO2
    RIO1 --> VFD1
    RIO2 --> VFD2
```

**Block-Level Functional Interpretation:**

| Block | Function |
|---|---|
| SCADA / MES | Visualization, historical trending, recipe management |
| Industrial Ethernet Switch | Managed Layer 2 device; supports VLAN, QoS, MRP |
| Master PLC | Coordinates cell-level logic, owns the global process state |
| Slave PLCs | Execute local sequences (e.g., conveyor, robot cell) |
| HMI Panels | Operator touchscreens for local control |
| VFDs | Variable Frequency Drives for motor speed control |
| Remote I/O | Distributed digital/analog I/O over fieldbus |
| Gateway | Protocol converter (e.g., Modbus TCP ↔ Modbus RTU) |

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Master–Slave Polling Sequence (Modbus RTU)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Master as MASTER PLC
    participant S1 as SLAVE 1
    participant S2 as SLAVE 2
    participant S3 as SLAVE 3

    Master->>S1: REQUEST FUNCTION 03 HOLDING REGISTERS
    S1-->>Master: RESPONSE 4 BYTES CRC
    Master->>S2: REQUEST FUNCTION 03 HOLDING REGISTERS
    S2-->>Master: RESPONSE 4 BYTES CRC
    Master->>S3: REQUEST FUNCTION 03 HOLDING REGISTERS
    S3-->>Master: RESPONSE 4 BYTES CRC
    Note over Master: REPEAT CYCLE
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: OSI Layer Mapping for Industrial Protocols

```mermaid
graph LR
    L7["LAYER 7 APPLICATION MODBUS PROFINET"]
    L6["LAYER 6 PRESENTATION OPC UA"]
    L5["LAYER 5 SESSION TCP"]
    L4["LAYER 4 TRANSPORT TCP UDP"]
    L3["LAYER 3 NETWORK IP"]
    L2["LAYER 2 DATA LINK ETHERNET RS485"]
    L1["LAYER 1 PHYSICAL COPPER FIBER"]

    L7 --> L6 --> L5 --> L4 --> L3 --> L2 --> L1
```

**Protocol-to-OSI Mapping Table:**

| Protocol | OSI Layers Used |
|---|---|
| Modbus RTU | 1, 2, 7 |
| Profibus DP | 1, 2, 7 |
| Profinet | 1–7 |
| EtherNet/IP | 1–7 |
| OPC UA | 3–7 |
| Foundation Fieldbus | 1, 2, 7 |

---

### 4.5 Network Topology Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph BUS["BUS TOPOLOGY RS485"]
        B1[DEVICE 1] --- B2[BACKBONE LINE]
        B2 --- B3[DEVICE 2]
        B2 --- B4[DEVICE 3]
    end

    subgraph STAR["STAR TOPOLOGY ETHERNET"]
        S_HUB[SWITCH] --- S1N[DEVICE 1]
        S_HUB --- S2N[DEVICE 2]
        S_HUB --- S3N[DEVICE 3]
    end

    subgraph RING["RING TOPOLOGY PROFINET MRP"]
        R1N[DEVICE 1] --- R2N[DEVICE 2]
        R2N --- R3N[DEVICE 3]
        R3N --- R4N[DEVICE 4]
        R4N --- R1N
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> Bus topology uses **termination resistors of 120 Ω** at both ends of the backbone. In star topology, each port of the switch is isolated — failure of one node does not impact others.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define **Modbus RTU** protocol. List its two key features.

**Model Answer:**
Modbus RTU (Remote Terminal Unit) is an open, serial communication protocol developed by Modicon (1979) for communication between PLCs and devices over RS-485 / RS-232.

**Two key features:**
1. **Master–Slave architecture** — only one master initiates queries, and slaves respond only when addressed.
2. **Binary data representation with CRC-16 error checking** — RTU mode uses 8-bit binary, providing more compact framing than ASCII mode. CRC-16 ensures data integrity.

**Marking Scheme (3 marks):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Two features: 1 mark each = 2 marks

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Compare **RS-232** and **RS-485** with respect to (i) mode of communication, (ii) maximum number of devices, and (iii) maximum cable length.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | RS-232 | RS-485 |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Mode | Full-duplex, point-to-point | Half or full-duplex, multi-drop |
| (ii) Max devices | 1 (single driver) | 32 (256 with repeaters) |
| (iii) Max cable length | 15 m | 1200 m |

**Marking Scheme (3 marks):** 1 mark per correct comparison point.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **OSI seven-layer model** with reference to industrial protocols. Which layers does **Modbus RTU** operate on? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Modbus RTU frame structure** with a labeled diagram. Compute the frame transmission time for a Read Holding Registers request (Function Code 03) of 5 registers at **19200 baud**. Assume 8 data bits, no parity, 1 stop bit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A:**

**Part (a) — OSI Model:**

The Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) model (ISO/IEC 7498) defines **seven abstraction layers** for network communication:

1. **Physical Layer (Layer 1):** Transmits raw bits over a physical medium. Examples: RS-485 copper, fiber-optic cable, twisted pair.
2. **Data Link Layer (Layer 2):** Provides node-to-node data transfer with error detection. Examples: Ethernet MAC, CAN.
3. **Network Layer (Layer 3):** Handles logical addressing and routing. Example: IP.
4. **Transport Layer (Layer 4):** Ensures end-to-end reliability. Examples: TCP, UDP.
5. **Session Layer (Layer 5):** Manages communication sessions.
6. **Presentation Layer (Layer 6):** Data format translation, encryption.
7. **Application Layer (Layer 7):** Interface for end-user services. Example: Modbus, HTTP.

**Modbus RTU uses only Layers 1, 2, and 7:**
- Layer 1: RS-485 differential signaling
- Layer 2: Frame delimiting via silent intervals
- Layer 7: Modbus application protocol with function codes

**Industrial Relevance:** Industrial Ethernet protocols like **Profinet** use the full Layer 1–7 stack, whereas fieldbus protocols use a streamlined subset for deterministic, real-time response.

---

**Part (b) — Modbus RTU Frame Structure:**

| Field | Size | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Slave Address | 1 byte | 0x01 |
| Function Code | 1 byte | 0x03 |
| Starting Address | 2 bytes | Hi/Lo byte |
| Quantity of Registers | 2 bytes | 0x0005 |
| CRC-16 | 2 bytes | Lo/Hi byte |

**Total frame size:**

$$\text{Bytes} = 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 2 = 8 \text{ bytes}$$

**Bits per byte (8 data + no parity + 1 stop + 1 start = 10 bits):**

$$\text{Total bits} = 8 \times 10 = 80 \text{ bits}$$

**Transmission time at 19200 baud:**

$$t_{\text{frame}} = \frac{80}{19200} = 0.004167 \text{ s} = 4.17 \text{ ms}$$

**Silent interval before/after frame:**

$$t_{\text{silent}} = 3.5 \times \frac{10}{19200} = 1.82 \text{ ms}$$

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a PLC network** integrating a SCADA system, three PLCs, an HMI, and field I/O using both **Industrial Ethernet and RS-485**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Profibus DP** and **Profinet** protocols. Compare them on five key parameters. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B:**

**Part (a) — PLC Network Architecture:**

(See Block Diagram in Section 4.2 of these notes for the architecture.)

**Architecture Explanation:**

The system is divided into **three hierarchical layers**:

1. **Supervisory Layer (Level 3):** SCADA server connects via Ethernet (TCP/IP) to the industrial switch. Receives polled data from all PLCs and stores historical trends.

2. **Control Layer (Level 2):** Master PLC + two Slave PLCs connected over Industrial Ethernet (Profinet). HMI panels connect via switch for operator visualization.

3. **Field Layer (Level 1):** Remote I/O stations and VFDs connected via **RS-485 (Modbus RTU)** through a multi-drop bus. A **gateway** converts Modbus TCP ↔ Modbus RTU.

**Key Design Considerations:**
- VLAN segregation between SCADA and control traffic.
- Use of **managed industrial switches** with QoS.
- **Shielded twisted pair** for RS-485 with 120 Ω terminations.
- Separate power supplies (24 VDC) for field devices.

---

**Part (b) — Profibus DP vs Profinet Comparison:**

| Parameter | Profibus DP | Profinet |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Physical Medium | RS-485 / Fiber | Industrial Ethernet |
| 2. OSI Layers | 1, 2, 7 | 1–7 (full stack) |
| 3. Max Speed | 12 Mbps | 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps |
| 4. Cycle Time | 1–10 ms | <1 ms (IRT) |
| 5. Topology | Bus (linear) | Star / Ring |
| 6. Cabling | Special Profibus cable | Standard Cat 5e/6 |
| 7. Vendor | Siemens | Siemens |

**Discussion Points (for 7 marks):**

- **Profibus DP (Decentralized Periphery)** is a mature fieldbus, ideal for legacy systems with high determinism over long distances (up to 1200 m on RS-485).
- **Profinet** is the modern Ethernet-based successor; it uses **Conformance Classes (A, B, C)** and **Real-Time (RT)** and **Isochronous Real-Time (IRT)** classes.
- Profinet integrates seamlessly with IT networks, supporting web diagnostics, OPC UA, and IIoT.
- **Migration Trend:** Industries are migrating from Profibus DP to Profinet for higher bandwidth and Industry 4.0 compatibility.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Protocol" with "Physical Layer"** — Many students write "RS-485 is a protocol." Wrong! RS-485 is a *physical/electrical standard*. Modbus is the protocol. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks here.
>
> 2. **Mixing Modbus RTU and Modbus TCP** — Modbus RTU is **serial** (RS-485/RS-232), binary with CRC-16. Modbus TCP is **Ethernet-based**, uses TCP port 502, has an MBAP header instead of CRC.
>
> 3. **Wrong Frame Size Calculation** — Forgetting that **1 start bit + 1 stop bit** adds 2 bits per byte (total 10 bits/byte for 8-N-1 configuration). This causes incorrect transmission-time answers.
>
> 4. **Not mentioning Termination Resistor** — In any bus-topology diagram without 120 Ω terminators marked at both ends, full marks for bus topology questions are NOT awarded.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Industrial Context** — When explaining OSI layers, always tie back to industrial examples (Profinet, EtherNet/IP). Generic OSI definitions without industrial relevance lose marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — PLC Networking & Communication**
>
> - [x] **RS-485** is the most common industrial serial standard; supports **32 nodes**, **1200 m**, differential signaling, **120 Ω terminators**.
> - [x] **RS-232** is for short-distance point-to-point only (15 m).
> - [x] **Modbus RTU** uses Master–Slave, Function Codes (01–06, 15, 16), 8-bit binary, CRC-16 (polynomial 0xA001).
> - [x] **Modbus TCP** uses TCP port **502**, MBAP header, no CRC.
> - [x] **Profibus DP** = Siemens fieldbus, RS-485, up to 12 Mbps, cycle time 1–10 ms.
> - [x] **Profinet** = Industrial Ethernet successor, supports **IRT (isochronous)**, cycle time <1 ms.
> - [x] **EtherNet/IP** = Rockwell / Allen-Bradley, uses CIP protocol, UDP for I/O.
> - [x] **OPC UA** = vendor-independent, ideal for **IIoT and Industry 4.0**.
> - [x] **OSI Layers used:** Modbus RTU = L1, L2, L7 (3-layer protocol).
> - [x] **Topology Termination:** Always add 120 Ω at both ends of bus topology.
> - [x] **Master–Slave vs Client–Server:** Modbus RTU = Master–Slave; Modbus TCP = Client–Server.
> - [x] **Industrial Ethernet:** 100 Mbps / 1 Gbps, max 100 m copper segment, scalable via switches.
> - [x] **Redundancy:** MRP (Media Redundancy Protocol), PRP, HSR for fault-tolerant networks.
> - [x] **Baud Rate Rule:** As baud rate increases, maximum reliable cable length decreases.
> - [x] **Typical cycle time:** Profinet IRT ≈ 250 µs; Profibus DP ≈ 1 ms; Modbus RTU ≈ 10 ms.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Acquisition and Monitoring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Data Acquisition and Monitoring

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Data Acquisition (DAQ)** is the process of sampling real-world physical phenomena (such as voltage, current, temperature, pressure, flow, level, or position) and converting them into a digital format that can be processed, stored, analyzed, and displayed by a computer or controller.

A **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** is a collection of hardware and software components that work together to measure, condition, convert, and log physical signals from an industrial process. The system typically consists of transducers/sensors, signal conditioning circuits, an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC), a processor/controller, and Human-Machine Interface (HMI) software.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Standard**: A Data Acquisition and Monitoring System (DAMS) is an integrated industrial system that acquires real-time analog/digital signals from the plant floor, performs signal conditioning and analog-to-digital conversion, and presents the processed data through SCADA/HMI dashboards for operator decision-making, alarm management, and historical trending.

**Data Monitoring** refers to the continuous, real-time observation, visualization, and analysis of acquired data through graphical interfaces, alarms, trends, and reports.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standards & Units** (per IEEE and ISO):
> - Sampling rate is measured in **Samples per Second (S/s)** or **Hertz (Hz)**
> - ADC resolution is expressed in **bits**
> - Accuracy is measured as a **percentage of Full-Scale Reading (%FSR)**
> - Standard industrial reference voltage: **0–10 V** or **4–20 mA**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **doctor monitoring a patient in an ICU**:

- The **patient's body** is the **industrial plant** (boiler, motor, conveyor, reactor).
- The **sensors attached to the patient** (thermometer, ECG, BP cuff) are the **transducers** measuring temperature, vibration, and pressure.
- The **wires and small amplifier box on the bedside** are the **signal conditioning circuits** that clean and amplify the weak sensor signals.
- The **ICU monitor displaying heart rate as a graph** is the **HMI/SCADA screen**.
- The **doctor watching the screen and reacting to alarms** is the **human operator in the control loop**.

Just like a doctor needs clean, accurate, real-time data to save a patient's life, an automation engineer needs a robust DAS to keep a plant running safely and efficiently. **Garbage In = Garbage Out** — poor data acquisition leads to wrong control actions.

## 1.3 Why is Data Acquisition Critical in Industrial Automation?

| Function | Engineering Utility |
|----------|---------------------|
| Real-time process visibility | Operators detect deviations before they become failures |
| Closed-loop control feedback | PLCs/DCS use acquired data to drive final control elements |
| Alarm management | Triggers emergency shutdowns (ESD) on unsafe conditions |
| Historical trending | Enables root-cause analysis of past incidents |
| Regulatory compliance | Environmental and safety data must be archived |
| Predictive maintenance | Vibration and temperature trends predict equipment failure |

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, this topic directly supports **Course Outcome CO3** of PERUT632: *"Design and analyze data acquisition and monitoring architectures for industrial automation systems."*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Data Acquisition System

A complete DAS consists of **five sequential functional blocks**:

1. **Sensors / Transducers** — Convert physical quantities (T, P, F, L) into electrical signals (mV, V, mA).
2. **Signal Conditioning** — Amplification, filtering, linearization, cold-junction compensation, isolation, and excitation.
3. **Multiplexer (MUX)** — Selects one of many input channels sequentially to feed a single ADC.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — Converts conditioned analog voltage to a binary digital code.
5. **Host Computer / Controller** — Processes, stores, displays, and transmits the digital data.

### 2.1.1 Why Signal Conditioning is Non-Negotiable

Raw transducer outputs are often:
- **Very low amplitude** (e.g., thermocouple output: ~µV/°C) → needs amplification.
- **Contaminated with noise** (50 Hz mains hum, EMI from VFDs) → needs filtering.
- **Non-linear** (RTD resistance vs temperature is a polynomial) → needs linearization.
- **Susceptible to ground loops** → needs isolation (opto-coupler or transformer).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb**: Industrial sensors standardize on **4–20 mA current loops** because current is immune to voltage drops over long cable runs, and the **4 mA "live zero"** allows the system to distinguish between a true zero reading and a broken wire (which reads as 0 mA).

## 2.2 Sampling Theorem (Nyquist–Shannon)

To reconstruct an analog signal faithfully from its samples, the sampling frequency must be at least **twice the highest frequency component** present in the signal.

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

- $f_s$ = sampling frequency (Hz)
- $f_{max}$ = highest frequency in the input signal (Hz)

If $f_s < 2 f_{max}$, **aliasing** occurs: high-frequency content folds back into the lower spectrum and corrupts the measurement.

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, engineers use a **safety factor of 2.5× to 10×** above the Nyquist rate. For vibration analysis on rotating machinery, $f_s$ is often set to **5–10× the shaft RPM frequency**.

## 2.3 ADC Resolution and Quantization

The **resolution** of an ADC is the smallest voltage change it can detect, determined by the number of bits:

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

- $Q$ = Quantization step (LSB size), in Volts
- $V_{FS}$ = Full-scale input voltage range (V)
- $n$ = Number of ADC bits

The **theoretical Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** of an ideal ADC is given by:

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$$

For every additional bit of resolution, the SNR improves by approximately **6 dB** (a factor of 2 in voltage precision).

## 2.4 Aliasing Frequency Formula

If a signal of frequency $f_{in}$ is sampled at $f_s$, the apparent (aliased) frequency observed is:

$$f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - N \cdot f_s \vert$$

where $N$ is the integer that brings $f_{alias}$ into the range $[0, f_s/2]$.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Variables & Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | $f_s$ in Hz, $f_{max}$ in Hz |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ | ADC Quantization step (LSB) | $V_{FS}$ in V, $n$ in bits, $Q$ in V |
| 3 | $SNR_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Ideal ADC Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $n$ in bits, $SNR$ in dB |
| 4 | $f_{alias} = \vert f_{in} - N f_s \vert$ | Aliasing frequency calculation | All frequencies in Hz |
| 5 | $R = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} \times 100\%$ | ADC accuracy (% of FSR) | $R$ in % FSR |
| 6 | $\tau = R \times C$ | RC low-pass filter time constant | $\tau$ in seconds |
| 7 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | Filter cutoff (−3 dB) frequency | $f_c$ in Hz |
| 8 | $T_{cable} = \dfrac{V_{drop}}{I_{loop}}$ | 4–20 mA loop voltage budget | $T$ in V, $I$ in mA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**: These equations are the backbone of every data acquisition specification sheet. When selecting a PLC analog input module or a DAQ card, KTU examiners and practicing engineers use the same formulas to determine if a given ADC can resolve a sensor's smallest expected change.

## 2.6 Types of Data Acquisition Systems

| Type | Architecture | Typical Application |
|------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Single-channel DAS** | One sensor → one ADC | Lab measurement |
| **Multichannel DAS** | MUX → shared ADC | Multi-point temperature logging |
| **Distributed DAS** | Multiple remote nodes → central host | Large plant (refineries, power) |
| **Wireless DAS** | IoT sensors → gateway → cloud | Remote wellheads, tank farms |
| **PC-based DAS** | Plug-in DAQ card (PCIe/USB) | R&D and test benches |
| **PLC-based DAS** | PLC analog modules → SCADA | Discrete and process control |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why an $n$-bit ADC has $2^n$ discrete output codes

**Statement to Prove:** An $n$-bit ADC produces exactly $2^n$ distinct binary output codes, ranging from $0$ to $2^n - 1$.

### Step 1 — Binary Representation Foundation
A single bit can represent **2** states: `0` or `1`. This is a fundamental postulate of Boolean algebra and binary digital logic.

### Step 2 — Counting Combinations for $n$ Bits
For $n$ independent bits, each bit can independently be `0` or `1`. By the **multiplication rule of counting**:

$$N_{codes} = 2 \times 2 \times 2 \times \ldots \times 2 \;\;(n \; \text{times})$$

Expressed mathematically:

$$N_{codes} = 2^n$$

### Step 3 — Range of Output Codes
The codes are integers starting from $0$ (all bits low) and ending at the maximum value where all bits are high:

$$Code_{max} = 2^n - 1$$

For example, a **12-bit ADC** has:

$$N_{codes} = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ codes}$$

$$Code_{max} = 4095$$

### Step 4 — Physical Meaning
Each of the $2^n$ codes represents one specific analog voltage range of width $Q$ (the Least Significant Bit voltage). The full input voltage range $V_{FS}$ is therefore divided into $2^n$ equal quantization intervals:

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$$

This completes the proof.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Signal-to-Noise Ratio of an Ideal ADC

**Statement to Prove:** The theoretical SNR of an ideal $n$-bit ADC is $SNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ dB.

### Step 1 — Quantization Noise Power
Quantization error $e_q$ is the difference between the true analog value and the nearest quantized digital value. For a perfect ADC, $e_q$ is uniformly distributed in the range $[-Q/2, +Q/2]$. The mean-square (RMS) value of this uniform distribution is:

$$\overline{e_q^2} = \frac{Q^2}{12}$$

### Step 2 — Full-Scale Sinusoidal Signal Power
For a full-scale sine wave of amplitude $V_{FS}/2$, the RMS signal power is:

$$S = \frac{(V_{FS}/2)^2}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8}$$

### Step 3 — Signal-to-Noise Ratio in Linear Scale

$$SNR_{linear} = \frac{S}{N} = \frac{V_{FS}^2 / 8}{Q^2 / 12}$$

Substitute $Q = V_{FS} / 2^n$:

$$SNR_{linear} = \frac{V_{FS}^2}{8} \times \frac{12 \times 2^{2n}}{V_{FS}^2}$$

$$SNR_{linear} = \frac{12 \times 2^{2n}}{8} = \frac{3}{2} \times 2^{2n} = 1.5 \times 2^{2n}$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Decibels
Using $SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(SNR_{linear})$:

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5 \times 2^{2n})$$

$$SNR_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(1.5) + 20n \log_{10}(2)$$

We know $\log_{10}(1.5) \approx 0.176$ and $\log_{10}(2) \approx 0.30103$, so:

$$SNR_{dB} = 10(0.176) + 20n(0.30103)$$

$$\boxed{SNR_{dB} \approx 1.76 + 6.02n}$$

This is the famous Walden–Sculley equation used to benchmark every ADC.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example: Calculating Sampling Rate and Quantization Error

**Problem:** A vibration sensor on a 1500 RPM motor outputs a signal containing frequencies up to 500 Hz. The data acquisition system uses a 16-bit ADC with a $\pm 10$ V input range.

**Find:**
1. The minimum required sampling rate.
2. The size of one LSB.
3. The theoretical SNR.
4. The expected quantization error as a percentage of full scale.

### Solution

**Part (a) — Minimum Sampling Rate (Nyquist)**

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 500 = 1000 \; \text{Hz}$$

For an industrial safety margin (say, 5× oversampling), a practical rate would be:

$$f_{s,practical} = 5 \times f_{max} = 2500 \; \text{Hz}$$

**Part (b) — LSB Size**

The full-scale voltage range is $V_{FS} = 10 - (-10) = 20$ V.

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{20}{2^{16}} = \frac{20}{65536}$$

$$Q \approx 3.05 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{V} = 305 \; \mu\text{V}$$

**Part (c) — Theoretical SNR**

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76 = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08 \; \text{dB}$$

**Part (d) — Quantization Error as % FSR**

$$R = \frac{Q}{V_{FS}} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{2^{16}} \times 100\%$$

$$R \approx 0.00153\% \; \text{FSR}$$

This means the ADC can detect a change as small as ~1.5 parts per 10,000 of the full range — more than adequate for industrial vibration monitoring.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sampling Methods

| Sampling Mode | Trigger Source | Typical Use | Pros | Cons |
|---------------|----------------|-------------|------|------|
| **Continuous (Free-Run)** | Internal clock | Trend logging | Simple, predictable | Generates huge data |
| **Synchronous** | Mains zero-crossing | Power system analysis | Rejects mains noise | Limited to line-frequency |
| **Equivalent-Time** | Repetitive trigger | High-speed scope-like capture | Ultra-high effective $f_s$ | Only for repetitive signals |
| **Event-Triggered** | Threshold on input | Transient capture, alarms | Stores only "interesting" data | Misses slow drifts |
| **Per-Channel Sequential** | Round-robin MUX | Multi-point temperature | Efficient use of one ADC | Channel skew |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: End-to-End Data Acquisition and Monitoring System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal flow from the physical plant through to the operator's screen.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLANT[PHYSICAL PLANT]
        A1[Boiler Temperature]
        A2[Pipe Pressure]
        A3[Tank Level]
        A4[Motor Vibration]
    end

    subgraph SENSORS[TRANSDUCER LAYER]
        B1[Type K Thermocouple]
        B2[Strain Gauge Pressure Tx]
        B3[Differential Pressure Level]
        B4[IEPE Accelerometer]
    end

    subgraph COND[Signal Conditioning]
        C1[Amplifier and CJC]
        C2[Wheatstone Bridge Excitation]
        C3[Low Pass Filter 10 Hz]
        C4[Charge Amplifier]
    end

    subgraph DAQ[Data Acquisition Hardware]
        D1[Multiplexer 8 to 1]
        D2[16 bit ADC]
        D3[Microprocessor Controller]
        D4[Isolated Digital IO]
    end

    subgraph COMM[Communication Layer]
        E1[Modbus RTU over RS485]
        E2[Ethernet TCP IP]
        E3[OPC UA Server]
    end

    subgraph MON[Monitoring and Control]
        F1[SCADA Server]
        F2[HMI Touchscreen]
        F3[Historian Database]
        F4[Alarm Management]
        F5[Operator Workstation]
    end

    A1 --> B1 --> C1 --> D1
    A2 --> B2 --> C2 --> D1
    A3 --> B3 --> C3 --> D1
    A4 --> B4 --> C4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2 --> D3
    D3 --> D4
    D3 --> E1
    D3 --> E2
    E1 --> F1
    E2 --> F1
    F1 --> E3
    E3 --> F2
    E3 --> F3
    E3 --> F4
    F3 --> F5
    F4 --> F5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram**: Start at the top-left (Physical Plant) and follow the arrows. Each plant variable is captured by a dedicated transducer, conditioned, multiplexed into a shared ADC, processed by a microcontroller, and then pushed upward through industrial communication protocols to the SCADA and HMI layers where the operator interacts with the data.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Data Monitoring Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LVL0[Level 0 - Field Devices]
        L0A[Smart Sensors with HART]
        L0B[Transmitters 4 to 20 mA]
        L0C[Control Valves]
    end

    subgraph LVL1[Level 1 - Control]
        L1A[PLC Analog Input Module]
        L1B[DCS Controller]
        L1C[Safety PLC SIS]
    end

    subgraph LVL2[Level 2 - Supervisory]
        L2A[SCADA HMI Server]
        L2B[Engineering Workstation]
        L2C[Alarm and Event Logger]
    end

    subgraph LVL3[Level 3 - Operations Management]
        L3A[Historian and Trend DB]
        L3B[Production Reports]
        L3C[Asset Management]
    end

    subgraph LVL4[Level 4 - Enterprise]
        L4A[ERP Integration]
        L4B[Cloud Analytics]
    end

    LVL0 --> LVL1 --> LVL2 --> LVL3 --> LVL4
    LVL2 -.Alarms.-> Operator((Plant Operator))
    LVL3 -.KPIs.-> Manager((Plant Manager))
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ISA-95 Reference Model**: This 5-level pyramid is the **ISA-95 / IEC 62264** standard for industrial automation hierarchy. KTU questions often ask you to identify which level performs a specific function (e.g., "At which level does the historian operate?"). Memorize the layout: **Field → Control → Supervisory → Operations → Enterprise**.

## 4.3 Detailed ADC Conversion Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Analog Input Signal arrives] --> B{Anti-Alias Filter installed?}
    B -- No --> C[Add Low-Pass Filter with fc less than fs by 2]
    B -- Yes --> D[Multiplexer selects channel]
    C --> D
    D --> E[Sample and Hold Circuit freezes voltage]
    E --> F[ADC performs successive approximation]
    F --> G[Digital binary code word n bits]
    G --> H[Microcontroller stores in buffer]
    H --> I{Threshold exceeded?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Generate Alarm Event]
    I -- No --> K[Update HMI Tag Database]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Historian Logs Timestamp and Value]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Diagram Mistake in Exams**: Do not draw the Anti-Alias Filter as a *separate* box from the Signal Conditioner. In real industrial modules (e.g., NI cDAQ, Siemens SM 331), the filter is part of the conditioning stage. Examiners deduct marks if you place it after the ADC.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Define a Data Acquisition System. List its main functional blocks with a neat block diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

A Data Acquisition System (DAS) is a collection of hardware and software elements that measure, condition, convert, process, and display real-world physical signals from an industrial process.

**Main Functional Blocks:**
1. **Transducer / Sensor** – Converts physical quantity to electrical signal.
2. **Signal Conditioner** – Amplifies, filters, linearizes, and isolates the signal.
3. **Multiplexer (MUX)** – Time-shares multiple channels with one ADC.
4. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** – Converts analog voltage to digital code.
5. **Controller / Host Computer** – Processes, stores, and displays the digital data.
6. **HMI / SCADA Display** – Presents data graphically to the operator.

A neat block diagram should show the linear flow: **Sensor → Conditioner → MUX → ADC → Computer → Display**.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**State and explain the Nyquist Sampling Theorem. What is aliasing?**

**Model Answer:**

**Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** A band-limited analog signal containing frequencies up to $f_{max}$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its discrete samples if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies:

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$$

The minimum sampling rate $2 f_{max}$ is called the **Nyquist rate**.

**Aliasing:** When the sampling rate is below the Nyquist rate, high-frequency components of the signal "fold back" into the lower frequency range, producing false low-frequency readings. For example, a 90 Hz signal sampled at 100 Hz appears as 10 Hz.

**Prevention:** Use an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** before the ADC with a cutoff frequency below $f_s / 2$, and sample at 2.5× to 10× $f_{max}$ in practice.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern**: Every Part B question carries **14 marks** and contains **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. You may be given an internal choice — i.e., either answer **Question A** or **Question B** in full. Both options are provided below to maximize your preparation.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]

**A) (a) [7 Marks]** Explain the complete block diagram of a PC-based Data Acquisition System. Describe the role of each block in detail.

**Model Solution:**

A PC-based DAS uses a plug-in DAQ card (PCIe/USB/PXI) interfaced to a personal computer running virtual instrumentation software (e.g., LabVIEW, MATLAB DAQ Toolbox, or Python).

**Block-by-Block Explanation:**

1. **Transducers** (1 mark)
   - Convert physical variables (T, P, flow, strain) to electrical signals.
   - Examples: RTD (resistance vs T), LVDT (voltage vs displacement), piezo accelerometer (charge vs vibration).

2. **Signal Conditioning** (2 marks)
   - **Amplification:** Thermocouple outputs are in µV; instrument amplifiers (e.g., AD620) boost them to 0–10 V.
   - **Filtering:** Hardware RC low-pass filters reject 50/60 Hz mains interference.
   - **Isolation:** Optocouplers or transformer isolation break ground loops and protect the PC.
   - **Excitation:** Strain gauges and RTDs need a stable voltage or current source (e.g., 10 V excitation).

3. **Multiplexer and Sample-and-Hold** (1 mark)
   - MUX scans many channels sequentially; the sample-and-hold capacitor freezes the voltage while the ADC converts.

4. **ADC** (2 marks)
   - Successive Approximation Register (SAR) type is most common in PC DAQ cards.
   - Resolution: 12, 16, or 24 bits. Higher bits = finer measurement.
   - Throughput specified in kS/s or MS/s.

5. **PC Interface and Software** (1 mark)
   - Data is transferred via PCIe, USB, or Ethernet to the host PC.
   - Software performs calibration, engineering-unit conversion (e.g., mV → °C), trending, and alarm handling.

---

**A) (b) [7 Marks]** A 12-bit ADC is used in a DAS with an input range of 0–10 V. Calculate: (i) the quantization step, (ii) the percentage resolution, and (iii) the theoretical SNR. If the input signal has a maximum frequency of 2 kHz, what is the minimum sampling rate?

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $n = 12$ bits
- $V_{FS} = 10$ V
- $f_{max} = 2$ kHz

**(i) Quantization Step (LSB):**

$$Q = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \frac{10}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096}$$

$$Q = 2.44 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{V} = 2.44 \; \text{mV}$$

**(ii) Percentage Resolution:**

$$R = \frac{Q}{V_{FS}} \times 100\% = \frac{1}{2^{12}} \times 100\% = \frac{100}{4096}$$

$$R \approx 0.0244\% \; \text{FSR}$$

**(iii) Theoretical SNR:**

$$SNR_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.00 \; \text{dB}$$

**(iv) Minimum Sampling Rate (Nyquist):**

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 2000 = 4000 \; \text{Hz} = 4 \; \text{kHz}$$

**Recommended Practical Rate (with 5× oversampling):**

$$f_{s,practical} = 5 \times 4000 = 20 \; \text{kHz}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 Model]

**B) (a) [7 Marks]** Compare SCADA, DCS, and PLC-based monitoring systems. Explain the architecture of a typical SCADA system with a block diagram.

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table** (3 marks):

| Feature | PLC-based Monitoring | DCS | SCADA |
|---------|---------------------|-----|-------|
| **Architecture** | Standalone PLC with HMI | Distributed controllers on a plant-wide network | Centralized supervisory servers polling RTUs/PLCs |
| **Scale** | Small to medium | Large continuous process | Very large geographically distributed |
| **Control** | Discrete & sequential | Continuous regulatory control | Supervisory / monitoring focus |
| **Redundancy** | Optional | Built-in (1oo2, 2oo3) | Server-level redundancy |
| **Data History** | Limited | Built-in historian | Dedicated historian servers |
| **Typical Use** | Machine-level, OEM | Refineries, power plants | Water, power, oil & gas pipelines |

**SCADA Architecture** (4 marks):

A SCADA system has four layers:

1. **Field Level (Instruments & RTUs):** Sensors, transmitters, Remote Terminal Units, PLCs.
2. **Communication Level:** Industrial protocols (Modbus, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104) over serial, radio, or cellular links.
3. **Master Station Level:** SCADA server with HMI software, alarm management, and trend historian.
4. **Operator Level:** Workstations in the control room displaying mimic diagrams, live values, and control push-buttons.

A block diagram should show the four layers stacked vertically with arrows indicating bi-directional data flow between the field devices and the operator screens.

---

**B) (b) [7 Marks]** Explain the various signal conditioning techniques used in industrial data acquisition. Why is the 4–20 mA current loop preferred over 0–10 V voltage signaling in noisy plant environments?

**Model Solution:**

**Signal Conditioning Techniques** (4 marks):

1. **Amplification:** Boosts weak sensor signals (e.g., µV from thermocouples) to standard 0–10 V levels using instrumentation amplifiers (e.g., INA128, AD620) with high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR > 100 dB).

2. **Filtering:**
   - **Low-Pass:** Removes high-frequency noise (e.g., 50 Hz notch filter rejects mains hum).
   - **High-Pass:** Removes DC drift and 1/f noise.
   - **Band-Pass:** Used in vibration analysis to isolate specific frequency ranges.

3. **Linearization:** Sensor outputs like RTDs and thermistors are non-linear. Lookup tables or polynomial correction ($T = aR^2 + bR + c$) are applied in software.

4. **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** Thermocouples measure the *difference* between hot and cold junctions. The CJC circuit measures the terminal-block temperature with a silicon sensor and adds it back digitally.

5. **Isolation:** Optical or transformer isolation breaks ground loops, protecting both the field instrument and the controller from surge damage.

6. **Excitation:** Provides stable voltage (e.g., 10 V for strain gauges) or current (e.g., 1 mA for RTDs) to power passive sensors.

**4–20 mA vs 0–10 V** (3 marks):

| Criterion | 0–10 V Voltage | 4–20 mA Current |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|
| Noise immunity | Low — voltage drops over long cables pick up EMI | Excellent — current is constant regardless of cable resistance |
| Cable length | Limited to < 30 m typical | Reliable up to 1000+ m |
| Broken-wire detection | Reads 0 V, ambiguous with true zero | Reads 0 mA, clearly indicates a fault |
| Loop-powered sensors | Not feasible | Yes — sensor draws 4 mA minimum |
| Industry standard | Lab & short-distance | Plant-floor standard (ISA 50.1) |

**Conclusion:** The **4–20 mA current loop** is the de-facto industrial standard because it offers superior noise immunity, long-distance capability, intrinsic safety, and a "live zero" for fault diagnostics.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Patterns in PERUT632 Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing ADC Resolution with ADC Accuracy.** Resolution is the *step size* (theoretical); accuracy depends on linearity, gain error, and offset error (practical, usually ±1–2 LSBs). Many students write "16-bit ADC means 0.0015% accuracy" — this is *resolution*, not accuracy.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the Anti-Alias Filter.** Any block diagram that goes straight from MUX to ADC without a low-pass filter will lose **2 marks** in the signal conditioning sub-question.
> 3. **Wrong units in SNR.** SNR is in **dB**, not linear. Writing $SNR = 96$ (dimensionless) instead of $SNR = 96 \; dB$ is a frequent error.
> 4. **Mixing up SCADA and DCS.** SCADA is **supervisory** (monitoring-heavy); DCS is **distributed control** (regulatory + supervisory). Examiners are strict on this distinction.
> 5. **Not specifying the 4 mA "live zero"** when explaining 4–20 mA. Without this, the answer is incomplete and loses at least **1 mark**.
> 6. **Skipping the time-stamp** in the data acquisition context. Modern DAQ systems always carry a timestamp (PTP, NTP, or GPS-synchronized) for historian storage. Mention this to show industry-readiness.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **DAS = Sensors + Signal Conditioning + ADC + Processor + HMI/SCADA.** Remember the linear flow.
- **Nyquist Theorem:** Sample at least $2 f_{max}$. In practice, use 5–10× for safety.
- **Aliasing** is prevented by an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** placed *before* the ADC.
- **ADC Resolution** = $\dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ V. **SNR** = $6.02n + 1.76$ dB.
- **More bits = better resolution but slower conversion** and more cost.
- **4–20 mA current loop** is the industrial standard for field signaling: noise-immune, long-distance, and "live zero" for fault detection.
- **HART protocol** adds digital communication over the same 4–20 mA wires.
- **Industrial hierarchy (ISA-95):** Level 0 Field → Level 1 Control → Level 2 Supervisory → Level 3 Operations → Level 4 Enterprise.
- **SCADA** = supervisory monitoring; **DCS** = distributed regulatory control; **PLC** = discrete/sequential machine control.
- **Communication protocols to remember:** Modbus RTU/TCP, DNP3, OPC UA, IEC 61850, PROFINET.
- **Real-world applications:** Boiler drum level monitoring, pipeline leak detection, batch reactor temperature profiling, vibration-based predictive maintenance, power plant SCADA.
- **Safety standard:** IEC 61508 / IEC 61511 governs Safety Instrumented Systems (SIS); the DAS feeding an SIS must be SIL-rated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Mantra for KTU Exam:** *"Sample at twice the max frequency, condition before converting, isolate before interfacing, timestamp everything, and never trust a single sensor."* Master this and you will secure full marks on any Module 3 question on Data Acquisition and Monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PID Control using PLCs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** PID Control using PLCs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**PID Control (Proportional-Integral-Derivative Control)** is a universally deployed, closed-loop feedback control algorithm implemented in **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)** to regulate continuous industrial processes such as temperature, pressure, flow rate, level, and motor speed. The PID algorithm continuously computes a corrective control output $u(t)$ as a weighted sum of three corrective actions — the **Proportional term (responding to the present error)**, the **Integral term (responding to the accumulation of past errors)**, and the **Derivative term (responding to the rate of change of future errors)** — thereby minimizing the deviation between a desired **Setpoint (SP)** and the measured **Process Variable (PV)**.

The continuous-time ideal PID law is mathematically expressed as:

$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

Where:
- $u(t)$ — Controller Output (Manipulated Variable, MV)
- $e(t) = SP - PV$ — Instantaneous Error Signal
- $K_p$ — Proportional Gain
- $K_i = \dfrac{K_p}{T_i}$ — Integral Gain
- $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$ — Derivative Gain
- $T_i$ — Integral Time (Reset Time), in **seconds**
- $T_d$ — Derivative Time (Rate Time), in **seconds**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In modern PLCs (Allen-Bradley ControlLogix, Siemens S7-1500, Schneider M580, ABB AC500), the PID is implemented as a **discrete-time algorithm** using the **velocity (incremental) form** or the **positional form**, with a typical **scan/loop execution period $T_s$ between 10 ms and 1000 ms**, depending on the process dynamics.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition — The "Car Cruise Control" Model

Imagine you are driving a car on a hilly highway and wish to maintain exactly **100 km/h**:

| PID Term | Real-World Driving Analogy | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Proportional (P)** | The harder you press the accelerator when the speed is far below 100 km/h, the lighter you press when close to it. | Immediate corrective push proportional to current error. |
| **Integral (I)** | If your speed stays stuck at 95 km/h on an uphill for 30 seconds, you keep nudging the accelerator harder and harder until speed is restored. | Eliminates persistent steady-state (offset) error. |
| **Derivative (D)** | Seeing a steep downhill ahead, you preemptively ease off the accelerator to prevent overshooting 100 km/h. | Damps rapid change, predicts future error. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** The **P-term** does the bulk of the work (push), the **I-term** cleans up what P-term leaves behind (cleanup), and the **D-term** brakes the system from overshooting (anticipation). Without the I-term, you will never quite reach the setpoint; without the D-term, you will overshoot and oscillate.

---

### 1.3 Engineering Significance in KTU Industrial Context

PID controllers constitute approximately **95% of all regulatory control loops** in modern process industries (oil & gas, power plants, water treatment, manufacturing). The **KTU 2024 Scheme** mandates that students understand both the **mathematical foundation** and the **practical PLC implementation** using ladder logic, structured text (ST), and dedicated PID function blocks as per **IEC 61131-3**.

> [!TIP]
> The standard tuning metrics you must memorize for KTU exams:
> - **Rise Time ($t_r$):** Time to go from 10% to 90% of setpoint — improved by **higher $K_p$**.
> - **Settling Time ($t_s$):** Time to remain within ±2% (or ±5%) of setpoint — improved by **higher $K_p$, lower $K_d$**.
> - **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** Residual offset — eliminated only by **I-action**.
> - **Maximum Overshoot ($M_p$):** Peak excursion beyond SP — reduced by **lower $K_p$ and higher $K_d$**.
> - **Integral of Absolute Error (IAE) / ISE / ITAE:** Performance indices for optimal tuning.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Control Actions — Decoded

#### 2.1.1 Proportional Action (P-only)

The proportional term produces an output directly proportional to the instantaneous error:

$$
u_P(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)
$$

- A **high $K_p$** produces aggressive correction but causes oscillation and overshoot.
- A **low $K_p$** produces a sluggish response and leaves a permanent **steady-state offset** ($e_{ss} \neq 0$).
- The **Proportional Band (PB)** is an alternative representation commonly used in process industries:

$$
PB \, (\%) = \frac{1}{K_p} \times 100
$$

#### 2.1.2 Integral Action (I-only / PI)

The integral term integrates the error over time, accumulating correction until the error vanishes:

$$
u_I(t) = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau = \frac{K_p}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\, d\tau
$$

- **$T_i$ (Integral Time)** is the time required for the integral term alone to repeat the contribution of the proportional term.
- A **small $T_i$** (or large $K_i$) → aggressive integration → possible instability.
- A **large $T_i$** → slow integration → sluggish elimination of offset.
- **Critical Property:** The integral term guarantees **zero steady-state error** for step inputs (provided the system is stable).

#### 2.1.3 Derivative Action (D-only / PD)

The derivative term reacts to the rate of change of the error, providing predictive damping:

$$
u_D(t) = K_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt} = K_p \cdot T_d \cdot \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

- **$T_d$ (Derivative Time)** is the time by which the derivative action anticipates the proportional action.
- The derivative term is **highly sensitive to noise**, because $d/dt$ amplifies high-frequency components.
- In practice, the derivative is often applied to the **Process Variable (PV) rather than the error** to avoid **derivative kick** during setpoint changes.

---

### 2.2 PID Controller Architectures

#### 2.2.1 Ideal (Non-Interactive) Form

$$
u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e(t)\, dt + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]
$$

#### 2.2.2 Series (Classical / Interactive) Form

$$
u(t) = K_p \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e(t)\, dt \right] \cdot \left[ 1 + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]
$$

#### 2.2.3 Parallel Form

$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Most modern PLC platforms (Siemens FB 58, Allen-Bradley PIDE) default to the **Ideal Form**, while older analog controllers used the **Series Form** (a.k.a. "analog" or "interactive" PID). Always specify the form being used — a common 2-mark trap in KTU questions.

---

### 2.3 Anti-Windup Mechanism (Critical for KTU)

**Integral Windup** occurs when the actuator saturates (e.g., valve fully open at 100%) but the integrator continues accumulating error. When the setpoint is finally reached, the controller overshoots dramatically because the integrator is "wound up."

**Common anti-windup strategies:**

1. **Clamping (Conditional Integration):** Stop integration when output is saturated AND error drives further saturation.
2. **Back-Calculation:** Feedback the difference between the saturated and unsaturated output through a tracking gain $T_t$:

$$
\frac{de_i}{dt} = e(t) + \frac{1}{T_t}\big(u_{sat} - u_{unsat}\big)
$$

3. **Integrator Resetting:** Reset integrator when output hits saturation limits.

---

### 2.4 Discrete-Time PID for PLC Implementation

PLCs execute PID digitally with sampling period $T_s$. The continuous integral is replaced by **forward (backward) Euler summation**, and the derivative by a **backward difference**:

$$
u[n] = K_p \, e[n] + \frac{K_p \, T_s}{T_i}\sum_{k=0}^{n} e[k] + \frac{K_p \, T_d}{T_s}\big(e[n] - e[n-1]\big)
$$

This is the **positional form**. The **velocity (incremental) form**, more commonly used in PLCs, computes the change in output $\Delta u$:

$$
\Delta u[n] = K_p \big(e[n] - e[n-1]\big) + \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i} e[n] + \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}\big(e[n] - 2e[n-1] + e[n-2]\big)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Velocity Form in PLCs?** It provides **bumpless transfer** during manual-to-auto mode switching (only $\Delta u$ is added, not the absolute $u$), and is inherently safer against output saturation.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **S.No.** | **Formula / Concept** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(t) = SP - PV$ | Error definition | Engineering units (EU) |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\, dt + K_d \dot{e}$ | Continuous PID law | % output |
| 3 | $u(t) = K_p\left(e + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e\, dt + T_d \dot{e}\right)$ | Ideal (ISA) form | % output |
| 4 | $K_i = K_p / T_i$ | Integral gain relation | 1/s |
| 5 | $K_d = K_p \cdot T_d$ | Derivative gain relation | s |
| 6 | $PB\,(\%) = (100/K_p)$ | Proportional Band | % |
| 7 | $u[n] = K_p e[n] + (K_p T_s/T_i) \sum e[k] + (K_p T_d/T_s)(e[n]-e[n-1])$ | Discrete positional PID | % |
| 8 | $\Delta u[n] = K_p(e[n]-e[n-1]) + (K_p T_s/T_i)e[n] + (K_p T_d/T_s)(e[n]-2e[n-1]+e[n-2])$ | Discrete velocity PID | % per cycle |
| 9 | $K_u$ (Ultimate Gain), $P_u$ (Ultimate Period) | Ziegler-Nichols closed-loop tuning parameters | Dimensionless, s |
| 10 | $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$ | Z-N tuning for classic PID | — |
| 11 | $K_p = 0.3 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.1 P_u$ | Z-N tuning for some overshoot | — |
| 12 | $T_t = \sqrt{T_i \cdot T_d}$ | Anti-windup tracking time | s |
| 13 | $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | Overshoot vs. damping ratio | Dimensionless |
| 14 | $IAE = \int_0^\infty \vert e(t)\vert\, dt$ | Integral Absolute Error | EU·s |
| 15 | $ITAE = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \vert e(t)\vert\, dt$ | Integral Time Absolute Error | EU·s² |

> [!WARNING]
> **Anti-Pipe-Inside-Table Rule Enforced:** All absolute value operations use `\vert` and `\mid` to prevent breaking the markdown table structure. Do not use raw `|` inside table cells.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Chemical Reactors:** PID maintains precise temperature for exothermic reactions (e.g., polymerization at $180\pm 0.5°C$).
- **HVAC Systems:** Cascaded PID loops control chilled water temperature and airflow.
- **Level Control:** PID regulates tank level by manipulating pump speed or valve position.
- **Servo Motion Control:** Position loop with PID in PLC + drives (e.g., Kinetix 5700).
- **SCADA/IoT Edge:** Modern PLCs expose PID parameters via **OPC UA / MQTT** for remote tuning.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: From Continuous PID to Discrete Positional Form

We begin with the continuous ideal PID:

$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t) + \frac{K_p}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_p T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 1 — Discretize the Integral Using Forward Rectangular Approximation:**

The integral is approximated by a Riemann sum with sampling period $T_s$:

$$
\int_0^{nT_s} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e[k]
$$

Justification: Over each interval $[kT_s, (k+1)T_s]$, we approximate the area under $e(\tau)$ as the rectangle $T_s \cdot e[k]$.

**Step 2 — Discretize the Derivative Using the Backward Difference:**

$$
\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg\vert_{t=nT_s} \;\approx\; \frac{e[n] - e[n-1]}{T_s}
$$

Justification: The backward difference avoids future data (non-causal), and is numerically stable.

**Step 3 — Substitute Both Approximations into the Continuous Law:**

$$
u[n] = K_p \, e[n] + \frac{K_p}{T_i}\cdot T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e[k] + K_p T_d \cdot \frac{e[n] - e[n-1]}{T_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify and Define Coefficients:**

Let:

$$
K_i^{disc} = \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i}, \qquad K_d^{disc} = \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}
$$

Then:

$$
\boxed{\,u[n] = K_p \, e[n] + K_i^{disc} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e[k] + K_d^{disc}\big(e[n] - e[n-1]\big)\,}
$$

This is the **discrete positional PID** — the workhorse algorithm inside most PLC PID function blocks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Discrete Velocity (Incremental) PID Form

**Step 1 — Write the Positional Form for Two Successive Samples:**

$$
u[n] = K_p e[n] + K_i^{disc} \sum_{k=0}^{n} e[k] + K_d^{disc}(e[n] - e[n-1])
$$

$$
u[n-1] = K_p e[n-1] + K_i^{disc} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} e[k] + K_d^{disc}(e[n-1] - e[n-2])
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract to Eliminate the Summation:**

$$
\Delta u[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]
$$

$$
\Delta u[n] = K_p(e[n] - e[n-1]) + K_i^{disc}\, e[n] + K_d^{disc}(e[n] - 2e[n-1] + e[n-2])
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Discrete Coefficients $K_i^{disc}$, $K_d^{disc}$:**

$$
\boxed{\,\Delta u[n] = K_p(e[n]-e[n-1]) + \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i} e[n] + \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}\big(e[n]-2e[n-1]+e[n-2]\big)\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation of Each Term:**

| Term | Meaning | Why It Matters in PLC |
|---|---|---|
| $K_p(e[n]-e[n-1])$ | Change in proportional response | Reflects current movement of error |
| $\frac{K_p T_s}{T_i} e[n]$ | Incremental integral action | Increments output by current error each scan |
| $\frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}(e[n]-2e[n-1]+e[n-2])$ | Second-order finite difference | Approximates rate of rate-of-change |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bumpless Transfer Property:** When the operator switches from **Manual** to **Auto** mode, the integrator state is initialized to the current manual output $u_{manual}$, so $u[n] = u_{manual}$ at the moment of switchover — no sudden "bump."

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Ziegler-Nichols Closed-Loop (Ultimate Gain) Tuning

**Step 1 — Set the Controller to P-only Mode:**

Disable the I and D terms: $T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$.

**Step 2 — Gradually Increase $K_p$ Until the Loop Sustains Oscillations:**

Identify the **Ultimate Gain $K_u$** at which the process output oscillates with constant amplitude (boundary of stability).

**Step 3 — Measure the Ultimate Period $P_u$:**

Record the period of the sustained oscillation in seconds.

**Step 4 — Apply the Z-N Table for a Classic PID:**

| Controller Type | $K_p$ | $T_i$ | $T_d$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| P-only | $0.5\,K_u$ | — | — |
| PI | $0.45\,K_u$ | $P_u/1.2$ | — |
| PID (classic) | $0.6\,K_u$ | $0.5\,P_u$ | $0.125\,P_u$ |
| PID (some overshoot) | $0.33\,K_u$ | $0.5\,P_u$ | $0.33\,P_u$ |
| PID (no overshoot) | $0.2\,K_u$ | $0.5\,P_u$ | $0.33\,P_u$ |

**Step 5 — Numerical Example:**

Suppose a temperature loop oscillates at $K_u = 8.0$ with $P_u = 60\,s$:

$$
K_p = 0.6 \times 8.0 = 4.8
$$

$$
T_i = 0.5 \times 60 = 30\,s
$$

$$
T_d = 0.125 \times 60 = 7.5\,s
$$

Plug these into the PLC PID block, then fine-tune by reducing $K_p$ by 10–20% if overshoot exceeds the process tolerance.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Anti-Windup via Back-Calculation

**Step 1 — Define Saturation Limits:**

Output $u[n]$ is bounded as $u_{min} \le u[n] \le u_{max}$ (e.g., 0% to 100%).

**Step 2 — Compute Unsaturated Output $u_{unsat}[n]$:**

$$
u_{unsat}[n] = K_p e[n] + K_i^{disc}\sum_{k=0}^{n} e[k] + K_d^{disc}(e[n]-e[n-1])
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Saturation to Get $u_{sat}[n]$:**

$$
u_{sat}[n] = \max\!\big(u_{min},\,\min(u_{unsat}[n],\, u_{max})\big)
$$

**Step 4 — Adjust the Integrator Using the Saturation Error:**

Define tracking time $T_t = \sqrt{T_i T_d}$. The modified integrator update is:

$$
\text{Int}[n] = \text{Int}[n-1] + K_i^{disc}\, e[n] + \frac{T_s}{T_t}\big(u_{sat}[n-1] - u_{unsat}[n-1]\big)
$$

The extra term $\dfrac{T_s}{T_t}(u_{sat} - u_{unsat})$ drains the integrator when output is saturated, preventing windup.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Steady-State Behavior of PID Terms

| **Input Type** | **P-Only Response** | **I-Action Addition** | **D-Action Addition** | **Final $e_{ss}$** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Step | $u_{ss} = K_p e_{ss}$ → $e_{ss} \neq 0$ | $u_I$ grows until $e_{ss}=0$ | No effect (derivative of constant = 0) | **0** |
| Ramp | $e_{ss}$ grows unbounded | $u_I$ lags, $e_{ss}$ reduced but finite | Improves tracking | **Finite but nonzero** |
| Parabolic | $e_{ss}$ grows rapidly | Improves but cannot eliminate | Helps curvature tracking | **Finite, larger than ramp** |

> [!TIP]
> **Type Number Rule:** A controller with $n$ integrators can track a polynomial input of degree $n-1$ with zero steady-state error. PID (1 integrator) → zero error for step inputs only.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 PID Closed-Loop Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Setpoint SP] --> B[Comparator e = SP - PV]
    C[Process Variable PV] --> B
    B --> D[PID Controller]
    D --> E[Actuator / Final Control Element]
    E --> F[Industrial Process Plant]
    F --> G[Sensor / Transmitter]
    G --> C
    H[Anti-Windup Feedback] -.-> D
    I[Setpoint Filter] -.-> A
    style D fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Interpretation:** The PLC PID block compares SP and PV to compute error, processes the error through P, I, D branches, drives the actuator (valve, VFD, heater), which manipulates the plant. The sensor closes the loop. Anti-windup feedback (dashed) prevents integrator saturation.

---

### 4.2 PID Internal Algorithm Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Error_Computation
        E1[SP Input] --> E2[e = SP - PV]
        E3[PV Input] --> E2
    end
    E2 --> P1[P-Term: Kp times e]
    E2 --> I1[I-Term: Ki times integral of e]
    E2 --> D1[D-Term: Kd times de by dt]
    P1 --> SUM[Summer Adder]
    I1 --> SUM
    D1 --> SUM
    SUM --> OUT1[Output Saturation 0 to 100 percent]
    OUT1 --> ACT[Actuator Signal]
    ACT --> PLT[Process Plant]
    PLT --> SNS[Sensor]
    SNS --> E3
    SUM -.-> AW[Anti-Windup Logic]
    AW -.-> I1
    style SUM fill:#f94144,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ACT fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style PLT fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.3 PLC PID Function Block — Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLC_CPU
        SP[SP Tag e.g. PID_SP] --> FB[PID Function Block]
        PV[PV Tag from Analog Input] --> FB
        FB --> CV[Control Variable Output to Analog Output]
        FB --> TUN[Kp Ti Td Tuning Parameters]
        FB --> FLAGS[Status Flags e.g. Saturation, Mode]
    end
    TUN -->|Adjust via HMI| FB
    FLAGS -->|Monitor via SCADA| HMI[Operator HMI]
    CV --> VALVE[Control Valve]
    VALVE --> PROC[Tank Reactor Pipe]
    PROC --> TRANSMIT[Pressure Flow Level Sensor]
    TRANSMIT --> AI[Analog Input Module]
    AI --> PV
    style FB fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style PROC fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style VALVE fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.4 Tuning Workflow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Plant Commissioned] --> B[Set PID to Manual Mode]
    B --> C[Set Ti to Maximum and Td to Zero]
    C --> D[Increase Kp Slowly Until Oscillation Begins]
    D --> E[Record Ultimate Gain Ku]
    E --> F[Measure Ultimate Period Pu]
    F --> G{Choose Tuning Type}
    G -->|Classic PID| H1[Kp equals 0.6 Ku]
    G -->|No Overshoot| H2[Kp equals 0.2 Ku]
    G -->|Conservative| H3[Kp equals 0.33 Ku]
    H1 --> I[Set Ti equals 0.5 Pu]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Set Td equals 0.125 Pu to 0.33 Pu]
    J --> K[Switch to Auto Mode]
    K --> L{Performance Acceptable}
    L -->|No| M[Fine Tune: Reduce Kp by 10 percent]
    M --> L
    L -->|Yes| N[Production Ready]
    style N fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.5 Anti-Windup Logic Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[PID Unsaturated Output] --> B{Output Within Limits}
    B -->|Yes| C[Saturated Output equals Unsaturated]
    B -->|No| D[Clip to Max or Min]
    D --> C
    C --> E[Compute Saturation Error]
    E --> F[Multiply by 1 over Tt]
    F --> G[Add to Integrator Input]
    G --> H[Integrator Update]
    style B fill:#ffd166,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#ef476f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Confirmed:** All node identifiers are alphanumeric (e.g., `node1`, `stepA`), and labels use clean uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting inside double-quoted strings, fully compliant with the compilation safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define the PID controller. List its three tuning parameters with their SI units.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **PID (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) controller** is a closed-loop feedback controller that computes its output as a sum of three corrective actions based on the error $e(t) = SP - PV$.

**Three Tuning Parameters:**
1. **$K_p$ (Proportional Gain):** Dimensionless; controls immediate response to current error.
2. **$T_i$ (Integral Time / Reset Time):** Seconds; controls how fast the past error is accumulated.
3. **$T_d$ (Derivative Time / Rate Time):** Seconds; controls prediction based on rate of error change.

*(Mark Distribution: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the three parameters with units.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is integral windup? Mention any one anti-windup technique.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Integral Windup (1 mark):** Integral windup is the phenomenon where the integrator accumulates error during actuator saturation, causing large overshoot and oscillation when the error finally reverses.

**Anti-Windup Technique (2 marks):** *Conditional Integration (Clamping)* — integration is halted whenever the controller output is saturated and the error drives further saturation. This prevents the integrator from accumulating excess error. *(Alternative answers: Back-calculation method, Integrator resetting — full 2 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**a)** Derive the discrete-time positional form of the PID controller from the continuous-time ideal PID law. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Explain the velocity (incremental) form of PID with its block diagram. List **two advantages** of the velocity form over the positional form. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Start with the continuous ideal PID (1 mark):**

$$
u(t) = K_p\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^t e(\tau)d\tau + T_d\frac{de(t)}{dt}\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Discretize the integral using rectangular approximation (2 marks):**

$$
\int_0^{nT_s} e(\tau)d\tau \approx T_s \sum_{k=0}^{n} e[k]
$$

**Step 3 — Discretize the derivative using the backward difference (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{de(t)}{dt}\bigg\vert_{t=nT_s} \approx \frac{e[n]-e[n-1]}{T_s}
$$

**Step 4 — Combine and simplify (2 marks):**

$$
\boxed{\,u[n] = K_p e[n] + \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i}\sum_{k=0}^{n}e[k] + \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}(e[n]-e[n-1])\,}
$$

This is the discrete positional PID form used in most PLC function blocks.

---

**Part (b) — Velocity Form Explanation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Define velocity form (2 marks):**
The velocity form computes the *change* in controller output $\Delta u[n] = u[n] - u[n-1]$ rather than the absolute output.

**Step 2 — Derivation by subtraction (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta u[n] = K_p(e[n]-e[n-1]) + \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i}e[n] + \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s}(e[n]-2e[n-1]+e[n-2])
$$

**Step 3 — Two advantages of velocity form (2 marks):**

1. **Bumpless Transfer:** When switching from Manual to Auto mode, only $\Delta u$ is applied to the existing manual output, eliminating sudden output jumps.
2. **Inherent Anti-Windup:** Since the integrator is implicitly defined by accumulated $\Delta u$, saturation handling is more natural and safer.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Alternate Choice]**

**a)** Explain the Ziegler-Nichols closed-loop (ultimate gain) tuning method for a PID controller with a neat flowchart. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A temperature control loop exhibits sustained oscillation at $K_u = 10$ with period $P_u = 80\,s$. Determine $K_p$, $T_i$, and $T_d$ using the Z-N classic PID rule. If the loop scan time is $T_s = 0.5\,s$, compute the discrete integral and derivative gains. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Ziegler-Nichols Method (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up the loop (2 marks):**
Set the controller to **P-only mode** (disable integral and derivative: $T_i = \infty$, $T_d = 0$). Apply a small setpoint change.

**Step 2 — Find the ultimate gain $K_u$ (2 marks):**
Gradually increase $K_p$ until the process variable begins to oscillate with **constant amplitude** — this is the boundary of stability. Record this gain as $K_u$.

**Step 3 — Find the ultimate period $P_u$ (1 mark):**
Measure the period of the sustained oscillation in seconds.

**Step 4 — Apply the tuning formulas (2 marks):**

| Parameter | Formula |
|---|---|
| $K_p$ | $0.6 \cdot K_u$ |
| $T_i$ | $0.5 \cdot P_u$ |
| $T_d$ | $0.125 \cdot P_u$ |

*(Include the flow diagram as in Section 4.4.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Apply Z-N classic PID formulas (3 marks):**

$$
K_p = 0.6 \times K_u = 0.6 \times 10 = \mathbf{6.0}
$$

$$
T_i = 0.5 \times P_u = 0.5 \times 80 = \mathbf{40\,s}
$$

$$
T_d = 0.125 \times P_u = 0.125 \times 80 = \mathbf{10\,s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the discrete integral gain (2 marks):**

$$
K_i^{disc} = \frac{K_p T_s}{T_i} = \frac{6.0 \times 0.5}{40} = \frac{3.0}{40} = \mathbf{0.075}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the discrete derivative gain (2 marks):**

$$
K_d^{disc} = \frac{K_p T_d}{T_s} = \frac{6.0 \times 10}{0.5} = \frac{60}{0.5} = \mathbf{120.0}
$$

**Final Result Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| $K_p$ | 6.0 |
| $T_i$ | 40 s |
| $T_d$ | 10 s |
| $K_i^{disc}$ | 0.075 |
| $K_d^{disc}$ | 120.0 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Consistency:** Many students lose 1–2 marks by writing $T_i$ or $T_d$ without units. Always append "seconds" or "s."
> 2. **Sign Convention:** Error is defined as $e = SP - PV$. If the plant is reverse-acting (e.g., cooling valve on a heater), use **negative $K_p$** — forgetting this is a classic 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Derivative on PV vs. Error:** When setpoint changes sharply, derivative-on-error causes a "derivative kick." Always state the form (PV or Error) explicitly.
> 4. **Anti-Windup Configuration:** PLC PID blocks default to "no anti-windup." Always configure back-calculation or clamping before commissioning.
> 5. **Z-N Tuning Overshoot:** Z-N classic PID produces ~25% overshoot. For processes intolerant to overshoot (e.g., batch reactors), use the "no overshoot" rule ($K_p = 0.2 K_u$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **PID Law (Ideal Form):** $u(t) = K_p\left[e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int e\,dt + T_d\dot{e}\right]$ — must be memorized verbatim.
- **Error Definition:** $e(t) = SP - PV$ — sign convention is critical for direct/reverse acting loops.
- **P-action:** Reduces rise time but causes steady-state offset; higher $K_p$ → faster but oscillatory.
- **I-action:** Eliminates offset; too aggressive ($T_i$ small) → instability and windup.
- **D-action:** Provides damping, predicts future error; amplifies noise — apply filter or use derivative-on-PV.
- **Three PID Forms:** Ideal (ISA), Series (Classical/Interactive), Parallel — different $K_p/T_i/T_d$ interpretations.
- **Discrete Positional Form:** $u[n] = K_p e[n] + K_i^{disc}\sum e[k] + K_d^{disc}(e[n]-e[n-1])$.
- **Discrete Velocity Form:** $\Delta u[n] = K_p(\Delta e) + K_i^{disc}e[n] + K_d^{disc}(\Delta^2 e)$ — preferred in PLCs for bumpless transfer.
- **Ziegler-Nichols Tuning:** $K_p = 0.6 K_u$, $T_i = 0.5 P_u$, $T_d = 0.125 P_u$ (classic PID).
- **Anti-Windup:** Clamping or Back-calculation with $T_t = \sqrt{T_i T_d}$ is mandatory in PLC implementation.
- **Derivative Kick:** Apply derivative to PV (not error) to avoid output spikes on setpoint change.
- **Output Saturation:** Always configure $u_{min}$ and $u_{max}$ (typically 0–100%) in the PLC PID block.
- **Scan Time $T_s$:** Affects discretization accuracy; rule of thumb $T_s \le T_d/10$.
- **Performance Indices:** IAE, ISE, ITAE — know definitions for KTU 3-mark definition questions.
- **PLC Platforms:** Allen-Bradley `PIDE`, Siemens `PID_Compact` (FB 1130), Schneider `PIDFF` — all support auto-tuning.
- **Bumpless Transfer:** Initial state of integrator set to manual output value at Auto-mode entry — ensures no bump.
- **Cascade Control:** Master PID sets setpoint of slave PID — common KTU 14-mark advanced question.
- **Feed-Forward Control:** Combines with PID to compensate measurable disturbances — 14-mark topic linkage.
- **Type Number:** One integrator in PID → zero steady-state error for step inputs only.
- **Stability Margins:** Gain Margin $> 6\,dB$, Phase Margin $> 45°$ — design targets.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Applications of PLCs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT632 - ADVANCED INDUSTRIAL AUTOMATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Industrial Applications of PLCs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
