### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**A (a)** Discuss the architecture and key enabling technologies of **Industrial Internet of Things (IIoT)** in a smart factory. Explain the role of OPC UA and MQTT in industrial communication. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
IIoT is a network of industrial sensors, actuators, controllers, and computing nodes interconnected via IP-based protocols to enable real-time data acquisition, analytics, and closed-loop control in manufacturing environments.

**Reference Architecture (3 marks — write the 5-layer stack):**

1. **Perception / Sensor Layer** — vibration, temperature, current, vision, RFID sensors; transducer + signal-conditioning front end.
2. **Network / Communication Layer** — wired (Industrial Ethernet, PROFINET, EtherCAT) and wireless (5G URLLC, LoRaWAN, Wi-Fi 6).
3. **Edge / Fog Computing Layer** — local gateways (Siemens IOT2050, Cisco IC3000) running lightweight inference.
4. **Cloud / Platform Layer** — hyperscale storage, big-data lakes (AWS IoT, Azure IoT Hub, Siemens MindSphere).
5. **Application / Service Layer** — dashboards, predictive maintenance, OEE monitoring, ERP/MES integration.

**OPC UA (1.5 marks):**
- *Open Platform Communications Unified Architecture* is a machine-to-machine (M2M) protocol defined by IEC 62541.
- Provides *platform-independent, secure, service-oriented* information modelling using the *Address Space* concept.
- Supports both client-server and publish-subscribe (over UDP) modes.
- Used for *interoperability* between PLCs, SCADA, MES, and ERP from different vendors.

**MQTT (1.5 marks):**
- *Message Queuing Telemetry Transport* is a lightweight, publish-subscribe protocol over TCP/IP, defined by ISO/IEC 20922.
- Uses a *broker* (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) and *topics* for asynchronous messaging.
- Ideal for *low-bandwidth, high-latency, unreliable* networks — typical of wireless IIoT nodes.
- Three QoS levels: 0 (at most once), 1 (at least once), 2 (exactly once).

**A (b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Digital Twin** concept. Differentiate between a *Digital Model*, *Digital Shadow*, and *Digital Twin*. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
A Digital Twin is a dynamic, real-time virtual replica of a physical asset, process, or system that mirrors its state through bidirectional data exchange, enabling simulation, prediction, and optimisation.

**Block Diagram (2.5 marks):** *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2 — describe it in text form.)*

The diagram must show:
- *Physical asset* on the left (e.g., CNC machine).
- Bidirectional arrows connecting it to a *virtual model* containing physics-based models (FEM, CFD), data-driven surrogate (LSTM), and a simulation engine.
- Outputs to *actuation setpoints* and *maintenance alerts* flowing back to the physical asset.

**Differentiiation Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Digital Model | Digital Shadow | Digital Twin |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Flow** | Manual, no automation | **One-way** automated (physical → virtual) | **Bidirectional** automated |
| **Real-Time Sync** | No | Partial / continuous | Full |
| **Decision Autonomy** | Human-in-loop | Human-in-loop | Machine can act autonomously |
| **Example** | CAD drawing | SCADA dashboard | Siemens Xcelerator live twin |

*(Award 1 mark per correct row.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Alternative [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**B (a)** Explain the concept of **Collaborative Robots (Cobots)**. List the four collaborative modes defined by ISO/TS 15066 and discuss safety considerations in human–robot workspaces. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
A cobot is a robot designed for direct physical interaction with a human worker within a shared, defined workspace, certified under ISO/TS 15066 (a technical specification extending ISO 10218).

**Why Cobots (1 mark):**
Traditional industrial robots must be enclosed in safety cages due to high speeds and forces. Cobots eliminate this barrier using *force / speed / power limiting* and *rounded geometry*, enabling flexible deployment in SMEs and mixed-model assembly.

**Four Collaborative Modes (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Safety-Rated Monitored Stop (SRMS):** Robot stops when human enters the workspace; resumes only after human exits.
2. **Hand-Guiding (HG):** Operator physically guides the robot end-effector through a teach pendant with force sensors; used for intuitive programming.
3. **Speed and Separation Monitoring (SSM):** Robot dynamically reduces speed as the human approaches the minimum protective distance (computed via safety-rated laser scanners / vision).
4. **Power and Force Limiting (PFL):** Robot's joints are intrinsically force-limited via torque sensors and compliant design; any contact force must stay below biomechanical thresholds:
   - Quasi-static contact: **F ≤ 150 N**, **P ≤ 50 N/cm²**
   - Transient contact: stricter limits (e.g., head: F ≤ 65 N).

**Safety Considerations (2 marks):**
- Risk assessment per **ISO 12100** and **ISO/TS 15066**.
- Defined *biomechanical thresholds* for pain onset.
- Emergency stop buttons at every workstation.
- Colour-coded safety zones (green / yellow / red).
- Training operators in cobot behaviour.

---

**B (b)** A CNC machining cell operates for **two 8-hour shifts**, with the following day's data:
- Planned breaks: **1 hour** total
- Unplanned downtime: **45 minutes**
- Total parts produced: **820**
- Good parts: **780**
- Ideal cycle time: **0.55 minutes / part**

Compute the **OEE**, identify the **biggest loss category**, and recommend **one Industry 4.0 intervention** to improve it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Planned Production Time (PPT):** *(1 mark)*

$$
PPT = (2 \times 8 \times 60) - 60 = 960 - 60 = 900\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Step 2 — Run Time:** *(1 mark)*

$$
Run\ Time = 900 - 45 = 855\ \text{minutes}
$$

**Step 3 — Availability:** *(1 mark)*

$$
A = \dfrac{855}{900} = 0.9500 = 95.00\%
$$

**Step 4 — Performance:** *(1 mark)*

$$
P = \dfrac{0.55 \times 820}{855} = \dfrac{451}{855} = 0.5274 = 52.74\%
$$

**Step 5 — Quality:** *(1 mark)*

$$
Q = \dfrac{780}{820} = 0.9512 = 95.12\%
$$

**Step 6 — OEE:** *(1 mark)*

$$
OEE = 0.9500 \times 0.5274 \times 0.9512 = 0.4767 = 47.67\%
$$

Step-by-step multiplication:

$$
0.9500 \times 0.5274 = 0.5010
$$

$$
0.5010 \times 0.9512 = 0.4766
$$

$$
\boxed{OEE \approx 47.67\%}
$$

**Step 7 — Loss Identification & Recommendation:** *(1 mark)*

The **Performance loss is dominant** (P = 52.74%, far below the 95% world-class target). This means the machine is running at roughly half its ideal speed.

**Recommendation:** Deploy an **AI-based spindle vibration analytics** system on an **Edge AI gateway (NVIDIA Jetson)**. The model will:
- Continuously monitor tool wear via FFT spectral features.
- Optimise feed rate and spindle speed in real time using a *reinforcement learning* agent.
- Push commands back to the CNC via **OPC UA** — closing the Industry 4.0 loop.

**Expected outcome**: P rises to ~90%, lifting OEE to ~80%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not multiply percentages as whole numbers.** Convert A, P, Q to *decimal form* before multiplying (e.g., 0.95, not 95). Many students lose 2–3 marks here.
> - **Do not skip units** in PPT, Run Time, and Ideal Cycle Time — they *must* be in the same unit (minutes).
> - **Do not confuse Digital Shadow with Digital Twin.** Shadow is *one-way*, Twin is *bidirectional*. Examiners check this distinction.
> - **Do not write "Industry 5.0 replaces Industry 4.0".** It *extends* it. Wrong phrasing ⇒ 1-mark penalty.
> - **Always cite ISO/IEC standard numbers** (e.g., ISO/TS 15066 for cobots, IEC 62443 for OT security, IEC 62541 for OPC UA). Examiners reward standard-aware answers.
> - **In 14-mark answers, draw the diagram first** and label every block. A well-labelled diagram = 2–3 guaranteed marks even if textual explanation is incomplete.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Memorise):**

- **Industry 4.0** = connectivity + digitalisation; **Industry 5.0** = human-centric + sustainable.
- **Nine Pillars**: IIoT, Big Data, AI/ML, CPS, Digital Twin, AR/VR, Additive Mfg, Cobots, Cloud/Edge.
- **OEE = A × P × Q** — world-class ≥ **85%**; loss categories in order: Availability < Performance < Quality typically drives OEE.
- **MTBF / MTTR availability**: $A_{sys} = MTBF / (MTBF + MTTR)$.
- **Cobot safety**: ISO/TS 15066 — quasi-static F ≤ 150 N, P ≤ 50 N/cm².
- **OPC UA** (IEC 62541) = platform-independent, secure, info-modelling; **MQTT** (ISO/IEC 20922) = lightweight pub/sub over TCP for low-bandwidth nodes.
- **5G URLLC** latency < 1 ms; **eMBB** = bandwidth; **mMTC** = 10⁶ devices / km².
- **Digital Twin** = *bidirectional*, real-time; *Digital Shadow* = one-way; *Digital Model* = static.
- **Cybersecurity standard**: IEC 62443 (defence-in-depth for OT).
- **AI classifier metrics**: Precision, Recall, F1-score, Accuracy — all in range [0, 1].
- **Edge computing** can save up to **99%** of WAN bandwidth for vision systems.
- **Predictive maintenance** reduces MTTR by ~30% and cuts unplanned downtime by ~70%.
- **Blockchain** trade-off: tamper-proof but throughput-limited; use Hyperledger Fabric for industrial permissioned ledgers.
- **Sustainable manufacturing KPIs**: kg CO₂ / unit, kWh / unit, % recycled material, % renewable energy.
- **Cyber-Physical System (CPS)** = the *theoretical* integration of computation, networking, and physical processes; IIoT is the *practical implementation*.
- **TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking)** enables deterministic Ethernet for closed-loop motion control over standard cabling.
- **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** is the Industry 4.0 standardised digital nameplate for every shop-floor asset.
- **Always draw labelled diagrams** in 14-mark answers — they are the easiest 2–3 marks to secure.
- **Always state a numerical benchmark** (OEE ≥ 85%, latency < 1 ms, density 10⁶/km²) wherever applicable.
